Predicting Patient Length of Stay (LOS) is a crucial task for optimizing hospital efficiency, resource allocation, and care quality in resource-limited settings. However, the inherent challenges of data skewness, outlier sensitivity, and imbalanced datasets make it a daunting task. Machine learning (ML) has been proposed as a potential solution, but its effectiveness in low-resource contexts remains unexplored. This study aims to develop and evaluate ML models for predicting prolonged LOS (≥ 6 days) at Debre Markos Comprehensive Specialized Hospital in Ethiopia, utilizing electronic health records (EHRs) to inform operational planning. A retrospective analysis of 27,268 patient records from 2017 to 2024 was conducted, with data preprocessing involving feature engineering, one-hot encoding, and addressing class imbalance using the Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE). Eight ML models were trained and evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC metrics, with feature importance assessed via permutation analysis. The results indicate that SMOTE significantly improved model performance, particularly in terms of recall and F1-scores. Gradient Boosting emerged as the top-performing model, achieving an AUC of 0.70, accuracy of 81%, precision of 72%, recall of 63%, and F1-score of 0.76. The key predictors of prolonged LOS were identified as ward type, age, pregnancy-related conditions, respiratory/digestive diagnoses, and rural residence. The findings suggest that Gradient Boosting with SMOTE can effectively predict prolonged LOS in resource-constrained settings using EHR data, offering potential benefits for bed management, staffing, and resource allocation. The results align with Ethiopia's Health Sector Transformation Plan goals for efficient healthcare delivery, and future work should focus on real-time validation and integration into hospital systems.

This study aims to elucidate the intrinsic strength of face-centered cubic (FCC) high-entropy alloys (HEAs) through a data-driven approach. Conventional strengthening theories have limitations in capturing the complex behavior of HEAs, necessitating a novel framework to investigate the composition-dependent strength of these alloys. Utilizing large-scale molecular dynamics simulations, we investigate the dislocation mobility in HEAs under various temperatures and compositions, revealing a complex glide behavior characterized by jerky and wavy motion due to localized pinning. Our analysis at 0 K yields a critical resolved shear stress (CRSS) that correlates linearly with the standard deviation of atomic pinning strength. To further understand the underlying mechanisms, we identify atomic features describing local structural and compositional fluctuations, and develop a symbolic model to predict the variability of atomic pinning strength using the Sure Independence Screening and Sparsifying Operator method. This framework offers a comprehensive understanding of the intrinsic strength of HEAs and provides a predictive tool for designing strong, compositionally complex alloys with tailored properties.

Automated essay grading has the potential to revolutionize English education by minimizing human bias and increasing assessment scalability. To address the limitations of traditional rule-based and machine learning approaches, this study proposes a novel hybrid scoring model that integrates Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (Bi LSTM), Transformer encoder, and Light Gradient Boosting Machine (Light GBM). Leveraging the Deep Essays Dataset from Kaggle, which comprises diverse student populations with human-annotated scores, the proposed model undergoes a multi-stage process involving deep text preprocessing, shallow and semantic feature extraction, and context representation fusion facilitated by Bi LSTM and Transformer layers. The extracted features are then fed into a Light GBM regressor to predict final scores. Experimental results on large-scale data demonstrate that the hybrid model significantly outperforms existing methods in terms of Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), Mean Absolute Error (MAE), and Pearson correlation metrics. Moreover, the model's accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score consistently exceed 99%, indicating its stability. Graphical analysis using Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) and Precision-Recall (PR) plots further underscores the robustness of the system. The findings validate the efficacy of combining deep neural networks and gradient boosting to identify linguistic and semantic patterns in essays, thereby paving the way for fair, reliable, and scalable assessment in English education.

This study aimed to develop and evaluate an interpretable machine learning model for early detection of type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) within the PERSIAN Dena Cohort. A significant challenge in T2DM management is its gradual development and often delayed diagnosis, leading to the emergence of complications. To address this issue, we developed an Extra Trees Classifier (ETC) that prioritizes probability calibration, fairness, and clinical interpretability. Our analysis involved 3,203 adults aged 35-70 years, with 79 variables considered, from which 15 informative predictors were retained after preprocessing and feature elimination. The ETC was optimized using randomized hyperparameter search and evaluated through ten-fold cross-validation with an additional internal-external split. We employed isotonic regression to calibrate probability estimates and utilized SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) and Morris sensitivity analysis to examine model transparency and feature influence. The results showed moderate discrimination and stable internal consistency, with cross-validated accuracy of 0.69 ± 0.03 and AUC of 0.69 ± 0.04. However, after calibration, the model's performance declined, indicating under-confident probability estimates. Our analysis revealed that fasting blood sugar, fatty-liver status, age, kidney-stone history, and triglycerides were dominant predictors, while lifestyle factors such as beverage and vegetable intake exerted secondary influence. The study highlights the importance of interpretability and fairness in clinical AI, underscoring the need for further research to expand predictor diversity, address class imbalance, and validate the model across other cohorts to develop a more generalizable and interpretable model for early T2DM risk prediction.

The proliferation of online information has necessitated the development of recommender systems (RSs) as critical tools for filtering and generating personalized recommendations to customers in the e-commerce sector. These systems leverage user preferences, past reviews, and product ratings to facilitate informed decision-making. This comprehensive review synthesizes research articles from top-tier journals and conferences published between 2020 and 2025, examining various facets of RSs. The study focuses on emerging techniques aimed at enhancing system performance, commencing with an overview of existing RS literature and prevailing research challenges. It delves into cutting-edge methodologies, including machine learning, deep learning, large language models, and conversational RSs, which are pivotal in the evolution of RSs. Furthermore, the review addresses significant research gaps and identifies key factors influencing RS platforms, providing a nuanced understanding of their current state and future trajectory. Ultimately, this study aims to provide a comprehensive understanding of the current landscape and future directions of RSs in the e-commerce industry, informing the development of more effective and user-centric RSs.

This study investigates the efficacy of machine learning and deep learning algorithms in classifying pulmonary diseases using standardized spirometry features from the Global Lung Initiative 2012 dataset. Spirometry, a widely employed lung function test, plays a pivotal role in early diagnosis and monitoring of pulmonary diseases. The objective of this research is to evaluate the performance of various machine learning and deep learning models in distinguishing between different pulmonary conditions. A comprehensive analysis of demographic variables and spirometry parameters was conducted to classify pulmonary diseases into both standard four-class and extended five-class problems. A range of algorithms, including logistic regression, support vector machine, k-nearest neighbor, random forest, XGBoost, deep neural networks, and convolutional neural networks, were applied to the dataset. To enhance model accuracy, preprocessing and standardization techniques were employed. The performance of these models was assessed using evaluation metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score, F1-macro, and area under the receiver operating characteristic curve. The results indicate that tree-based models, specifically XGBoost and random forest, achieved the highest generalization performance, with XGBoost demonstrating a success rate of 99.24 ± 0.59% accuracy. In contrast, deep learning models, such as deep neural networks and convolutional neural networks, exhibited lower accuracy rates of 81.61 ± 5.83% and 77.57 ± 5.55%, respectively. These findings suggest that machine learning-based approaches hold significant potential for early diagnosis of pulmonary diseases, but further optimization is required to improve their performance. This study provides valuable insights for the integration of artificial intelligence-supported spirometry analysis into clinical practice, emphasizing the need for validation of these methods on larger patient populations to facilitate their adoption in the healthcare system.

Molecular representation learning is a pivotal area of research that bridges machine learning and chemistry, enabling the prediction of molecular properties and complex tasks such as drug discovery by encoding molecules as numerical vectors. However, existing methods often struggle to generalize well when faced with out-of-distribution (OOD) data, a common scenario in real-world molecular environments characterized by dynamic and uncertain conditions. To address this limitation, we introduce a novel framework called EISG, which integrates environmental inference and subgraph generation to enhance the performance of molecular representation learning models on OOD data. EISG comprises an unsupervised environmental classification model that identifies latent variables generated by different distributions, and a subgraph extractor based on information bottleneck theory that extracts invariant representations from molecular graphs related to prediction labels. By combining new learning objectives, EISG enables the model to recognize invariant graph representations across various environments, leading to improved OOD generalization. Our experimental results demonstrate that EISG exhibits robust generalization capabilities across diverse OOD settings, underscoring its potential to address a significant challenge in molecular representation learning.

The increasing reliance on artificial intelligence (AI) algorithms in decision-making processes has highlighted the importance of high-quality training data. However, the availability of large-scale medical datasets is often hindered by strict privacy regulations, resulting in reduced data sizes and underrepresentation of certain demographic groups. This data scarcity can be detrimental to AI performance, as algorithms may replicate these data shortcomings. To address this issue, researchers have explored the use of synthetic data generation to augment existing datasets. This study examines the current state of synthetic data generation methods, focusing on statistical, probabilistic, and machine learning-based approaches for tabular data. A comprehensive review of high-quality peer-reviewed articles from various databases reveals that synthetic data not only increases dataset volume but also enhances data quality by promoting diversity and representation of underrepresented groups. Techniques such as Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) and its variants have demonstrated significant improvements in classification performance, with reported gains of up to 12%. Nonetheless, the quality of synthetic data is contingent upon the underlying generation process, the characteristics of the real-world data used for modeling, and the evaluation metrics employed to assess data quality. Our findings underscore the potential of synthetic data generation as a viable solution to address data scarcity and improve AI performance in medical applications.

The metabolic reprogramming of cancer cells, characterized by lactate accumulation, plays a pivotal role in tumor progression. However, the spatial and cell-type-specific effects of lactate in lung adenocarcinoma (LUAD) remain poorly understood. To elucidate these dynamics, we employed a multi-omics approach combining single-cell transcriptomics, spatial transcriptomics, spatial metabolomics, and immunofluorescence imaging with clinical survival data. Our analysis revealed distinct lactate profiles in LUAD tissues, with high-lactate tumors exhibiting increased epithelial and fibroblast abundance, whereas T/NK cells and monocytes/macrophages were enriched in low-lactate samples. Spatial metabolomics further demonstrated cell-type-specific lactate and pyruvate distributions, with endothelial cells showing minimal lactate accumulation. Notably, endothelial subclusters in high-lactate tissues displayed angiogenic and stress-response signatures, strongly associated with poor prognosis. Machine-learning models, including random forest, elastic-net regression, SVM, ANN, and decision-tree models, consistently identified endothelial and fibroblast programs as key determinants of high-lactate states and adverse clinical outcomes. Our study demonstrates that lactate significantly influences the LUAD microenvironment by driving angiogenesis, immune suppression, and prognostic stratification, underscoring the potential of lactate-centered pathways as novel therapeutic targets.

The increasing prevalence of herbal medicines and natural dietary supplements in modern pharmacotherapy has raised concerns about herb-drug interactions (HDIs), which can compromise drug efficacy or safety by altering pharmacokinetic or pharmacodynamic processes. Despite their clinical significance, HDIs remain understudied due to the complexity and cost of experimental validation. To address this knowledge gap, we have developed a novel computational framework for predicting the effects of HDIs. Our approach utilizes a heterogeneous graph that integrates three types of interactions: drug-drug interactions (DDIs), drug-target interactions (DTIs), and herb-target interactions (HTIs). To capture the intricate relationships within this complex graph, we employ metapath-guided random walks to generate node embeddings, which are then combined with molecular fingerprints, tabular descriptors, and target node encodings to train various supervised classifiers. Our extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of this approach, particularly with tree-based models such as XGBoost and Random Forest, which achieve high predictive performance in multi-class classification tasks. These results highlight the potential of AI-powered, graph-based methodologies to support integrative and precision medicine by enabling scalable and accurate prediction of herb-drug interaction effects, ultimately facilitating safer and more effective pharmacotherapy.

The escalating prevalence of stress-related health issues underscores the necessity for accurate and reliable stress detection systems. This study presents a machine learning-based framework for stress classification using two prominent multimodal physiological datasets: NEURO and WESAD. To effectively analyze the temporal and structural patterns in physiological time-series data, we employ a novel approach that transforms raw signals into image representations using advanced signal processing techniques, including Gramian Angular Summation Fields, Gramian Angular Difference Fields, and Markov Transition Fields. Unlike conventional methods that focus on time-axis transformations, our approach emphasizes feature-axis transformations to capture distinctive patterns relevant for distinguishing stress states. The integration of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) enhances classification performance by extracting spatial features from these visual representations. Furthermore, Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) visualizations identify the most influential input regions driving the model's decision, providing valuable insights into physiological indicators of stress. Our results demonstrate strong generalization across both datasets, with feature-axis transformations based deep learning models achieving approximately 98.3% accuracy, while classical models like XGBoost and LightGBM achieve around 99% accuracy. The integration of Grad-CAM significantly enhances the model's interpretability by validating the relevance of learned features. Overall, this study showcases the potential of combining advanced signal transformation techniques with explainable deep learning to develop robust and interpretable stress detection systems.

Abdominal organ segmentation in medical imaging is a complex task due to the intricate anatomy and low contrast of abdominal structures. To address this challenge, we introduce a novel deep learning architecture, MSAE-U-Net, designed to achieve robust and precise segmentation. The proposed model integrates three innovative modules: the Multi-Scale Deformable Convolutional Block Attention Module (MS-D-CBAM) enables multi-scale attention, the Adaptive Global Context Refinement Attention Module (AGCR-ASPP) refines context dynamically, and the Rectangular Self-Calibration Module (RCM) recalibrates spatial features. These components synergistically enhance both local detail and global context in segmentation. Evaluating MSAE-U-Net on the Dresden Surgical Anatomy dataset reveals superior performance, with a Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) of 93.13%, Intersection over Union (IoU) of 92.01%, and precision of 94.67%. Notably, MSAE-U-Net outperforms existing models, including U-Net, nnU-Net, and AbdomenNet, demonstrating its potential for clinical applications, such as preoperative planning and automated organ identification. The results of this study underscore the potential of MSAE-U-Net as a reliable tool for abdominal organ segmentation in medical imaging.

Brain tumors are a significant contributor to cancer-related fatalities worldwide, underscoring the imperative need for early and accurate diagnosis. The advent of advanced medical imaging technologies has facilitated the development of computer-aided diagnostic systems, which rely heavily on image classification techniques. This study proposes the development and evaluation of five novel hybrid models for the multi-class classification of brain tumors. By integrating convolutional neural networks (CNNs) with Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks and Support Vector Machines (SVMs), the proposed models aim to leverage the strengths of each component. Specifically, the CNNs are employed for feature extraction, while the LSTMs capture temporal dependencies in the extracted features, and the SVMs provide robust classification capabilities. To address class imbalance and enhance the diversity of the training dataset, data augmentation techniques are applied. Experimental results demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed models, with the ResNet152 + LSTM + SVM model achieving exceptional performance metrics, including 99% accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score, when optimized using the RMSprop optimizer. These findings suggest that the proposed hybrid models hold promise for improving the accuracy and reliability of brain tumor classification, ultimately contributing to enhanced patient outcomes.

The convergence of telecommunications and computer vision technologies has led to significant advancements in functionality and application scope. Despite the distinct development of these fields, their integration has been underexplored, hindering the realization of their combined potential. In challenging environments, such as those characterized by obstructions or limited lighting, the fusion of radio sensing and computer vision can provide substantial benefits in problem-solving. The integration of heterogeneous and multi-modal data through machine learning algorithms enables the extraction of additional knowledge from raw and low-level data, fostering the creation of a higher-level abstraction that can enhance numerous applications. This study introduces the CONVERGE vision-radio concept, a paradigm that seeks to leverage the synergy between telecommunications and computer vision to achieve a unified "View-to-Communicate, Communicate-to-View" framework. The CONVERGE vision is underpinned by a set of supporting use cases and a proposed architecture, which integrates wireless communications and computer vision to create a novel experimental infrastructure. This infrastructure will provide open datasets comprising both experimental and simulated data, facilitating new research opportunities in five key verticals: telecommunications, automotive, manufacturing, media, and healthcare. The ultimate goal is to stimulate innovative research and development in these areas, driving the advancement of converged telecommunications and computer vision technologies.

This study undertakes a comprehensive analysis of host responses to coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) and other respiratory viral infections, including influenza and respiratory syncytial virus (RSV), to elucidate the underlying mechanisms of these diseases. By integrating COVID-19 Host Genetics Initiative (HGI) genome-wide association study (GWAS) data and publicly available multi-omics datasets, we constructed an explainable artificial intelligence model to compare host infection mechanisms across these viral illnesses. Our analysis reveals that type I interferon signaling is a shared antiviral pathway active in host responses to all three viruses, while distinct mechanisms are also observed, such as the unique induction of coagulation and renin-angiotensin system dysregulation in COVID-19, robust T-cell activation in influenza, and excessive neutrophil-driven inflammation in RSV. Genetic risk pathway fingerprints from GWAS indicate that COVID-19 severity is associated with variants in interferon and inflammatory pathways, whereas host genetic effects in influenza are linked to distinct receptor usage, with minimal overlap. Mendelian randomization identified key causal proteins for COVID-19 severity, including ABO and inflammatory mediators, suggesting that host glycomic and immune factors modulate disease outcomes. Our model successfully distinguished COVID-19 from other viral infections, with SHAP interpretation confirming the predominance of these mechanisms in model predictions. This study provides a comparative map of host responses in COVID-19 versus influenza and RSV, offering insights into both common antiviral defenses and unique pathogenic pathways. These findings inform the development of targeted therapies and broad-spectrum antivirals to mitigate severe respiratory viral diseases.

This study addresses critical challenges in 3D human pose estimation (HPE) and energy expenditure estimation (EEE), focusing on handling complex activities and enhancing generalization. To address these challenges, we propose a unified end-to-end framework, Pose2Met, which jointly optimizes 3D HPE and EEE. At its core is STAPFormer, a novel Transformer model incorporating a SpatioTemporal Aggregated Pose (STAP) representation for efficient motion modeling. This representation enables Pose2Met to learn a unified pose–metabolism strategy, predicting both 3D pose and energy expenditure from 2D pose inputs. Our approach achieves comparable performance to traditional 2D-3D-expenditure pipelines while significantly improving computational efficiency and robustness. Experimental results demonstrate that STAPFormer achieves state-of-the-art performance on Human3.6M with a mean per joint position error (MPJPE) of 38.2 mm, outperforming existing models. Furthermore, Pose2Met's unified learning framework enables 2D pose-based energy expenditure estimation to approach the accuracy of 3D pose-based prediction, showcasing enhanced robustness and generalization. These findings underscore the significance of high-quality motion representations for both HPE and EEE, highlighting Pose2Met's potential for intelligent fitness and healthcare applications.

This study addresses the challenges of sentiment analysis in short, noisy social-media messages, where linguistic irregularities such as slang, emojis, and misspellings hinder predictive accuracy and obscure the reasoning behind model outputs. To mitigate these issues, we propose a novel hybrid model that integrates a convolutional block, a bidirectional long short-term memory network (BiLSTM), and a multi-head self-attention mechanism with a noise-invariant contrastive head (NICH). The NICH component normalizes noisy tokens before encoding, enabling the model to focus on sentiment-bearing information. Our model is trained in a two-phase process, initially freezing word embeddings and then fine-tuning them. We evaluate the proposed approach on two distinct benchmarks: Sentiment140, a large-scale dataset of tweets, and IMDb, a collection of movie reviews. Compared to classical baselines, including logistic regression, support vector machine, and random forest, as well as a reference Attention-BiLSTM model, our approach achieves superior performance. Notably, it also demonstrates competitive results with a compact Transformer-based model, DistilBERT, achieving accuracy rates of 90.63% and 91.90% on Sentiment140 and IMDb, respectively, along with F1 scores of 0.9079 and 0.9203. Furthermore, our model provides token-level explanations through attention scores and a perturbation-based metric, offering valuable insights into the reasoning behind sentiment predictions. These results have practical implications for real-time monitoring of customer feedback and brand perception in noisy social-media streams.

This study addresses the pressing need for effective predictive tools in the fintech industry, where startups face an alarmingly high failure rate. To mitigate this issue, we develop a deep learning framework based on 1D Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for categorizing fintech startup outcomes into four distinct classes: Initial Public Offering (IPO), Merger & Acquisition (M&A), Closure, and Unicorn status. Leveraging Crunchbase data, the proposed model extracts intricate patterns from key attributes such as team composition, funding velocity, investor diversity, and company age. To address the inherent class imbalance, we employ ADASYN oversampling, a technique that enhances the representation of minority classes. Our results demonstrate that the CNN model achieves competitive performance, with an accuracy of 90.8% and macro F1 score of 89.6%, outperforming traditional models such as XGBoost, which achieves 92.9% accuracy and 91.9% macro F1. This study highlights the potential of CNNs as a viable deep-learning alternative for multiclass startup outcome prediction, offering a valuable tool for investors and policymakers seeking to inform their decisions.

In the context of smart agricultural IoT systems, the integration of diverse devices and dynamic environmental conditions poses significant challenges for resource allocation. To address these challenges, we present a novel framework that leverages the capabilities of 6G edge computing to optimize resource management. By harnessing the ultra-low latency and high-bandwidth features of 6G, our framework enables real-time communication and efficient task offloading, while a reinforcement learning component dynamically adjusts the resource allocation strategy in response to changing environmental states and task requirements. A carefully designed reward function balances competing objectives of latency reduction, resource utilization, and energy efficiency. Experimental evaluations demonstrate the efficacy of our approach, achieving a 35% reduction in latency and a 25% improvement in resource utilization compared to traditional methods, with scalability validated across increasing device densities and task complexities. This research contributes a flexible and adaptive solution for smart agriculture, applicable to applications such as precision irrigation, autonomous farming, and drone-based surveillance.

This study investigates the potential of large language models (LLMs) in the healthcare domain, where the efficient utilization of extensive patient data and medical records significantly enhances diagnostic accuracy and disease-risk prediction. As the healthcare industry continues to grapple with the complexities of big data and artificial intelligence, there is a pressing need for a comprehensive framework to evaluate the application of LLMs in medical data analysis. To address this need, we propose the HLLM-Potential Framework, a novel evaluation framework designed to assess the applicability and performance of LLMs in medical data analysis through comparative experiments with traditional models. Utilizing publicly available and standardized cardiovascular datasets, which encompass both structured and unstructured data, we trained and evaluated various LLMs on medical data for disease prediction and health-risk assessment. Our results demonstrate that LLMs can effectively process both structured and unstructured medical data, achieving high accuracy and recall rates in cardiovascular disease prediction tasks. Notably, the multimodal LLM Janus Pro 7B achieved an accuracy rate of 85% in ECG image classification. Our findings suggest that LLMs possess stronger interactivity and generalization capabilities compared to traditional machine learning and deep learning models, with reduced dependence on data quality and feature engineering. These advantages, including high efficiency, flexibility, and multimodal integration, position LLMs as a promising tool for widespread adoption in the medical and healthcare domains.

In various fields like medical imaging, remote sensing, machine learning, and data science, the recovery of sparse signals is a fundamental problem. These signals often exhibit non-negativity due to the physical nature of the measured quantities, such as pixel intensities, chemical concentrations, or power spectra. While significant research has focused on general sparse recovery and more recent work has explored non-negative sparse recovery, a unified theoretical and algorithmic framework that can seamlessly handle both cases has been lacking. This study proposes a novel feasible sequential quadratic programming (SQP) framework that treats general and non-negative sparse recovery as special instances of a single constrained optimization model, distinguished solely by the geometry of the trust region used to enforce feasibility. The framework is grounded in the Smoothed-ℓ0 (SL0) approximation, which provides a smooth, non-convex surrogate for the combinatorial ℓ0 norm. By leveraging the exact diagonal structure of the Hessian of the SL0 objective, we derive accurate second-order search directions and achieve a superlinear convergence rate, surpassing the performance of first-order methods prevalent in the literature. For non-negative recovery, we integrate interior-point ideas directly into the SQP subproblem, employing an ellipsoidal trust region to maintain all iterates within the non-negative orthant, thereby ensuring stability and theoretical soundness. The non-negative method is initialized with a simple, robust all-ones vector, circumventing potential instabilities associated with pseudo-inverse-based initializations. We establish global convergence of the unified framework under the standard Mangasarian–Fromovitz Constraint Qualification (MFCQ). Numerical experiments on synthetic data and real-world tasks, such as face recognition, demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms a wide range of state-of-the-art algorithms in terms of recovery accuracy, phase transition behavior, and robustness to noise.

This study addresses a critical limitation of Bayesian causal forests (BCF) in estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTE) in the presence of confounding variables. The accuracy of BCF estimates heavily relies on the precise estimation of propensity scores (PS), and misspecification can lead to biased treatment effect estimates. To mitigate this issue, we introduce BCF with covariate balancing propensity scores (BCF-CBPS), which seamlessly integrates covariate balancing into the PS estimation process. By substituting conventional PS with CBPS, our approach significantly enhances covariate balance, enabling BCF to accurately capture true treatment effect heterogeneity. Our research demonstrates, through extensive Monte Carlo simulations and real-world applications, that BCF-CBPS outperforms standard BCF and other machine learning methods in terms of precision and robustness for HTE estimation. The results underscore the efficacy of combining covariate balancing with the BCF model for reliable treatment effect estimation in complex observational studies, thereby enhancing the validity and generalizability of causal inference.

This study introduces a machine learning framework for analyzing and categorizing modern and contemporary Korean paintings based on visual and semantic features extracted from image data. By integrating a pre-trained vision-language architecture with a multi-level analysis, the proposed method efficiently captures detailed formal characteristics, including color attributes from various color spaces and quantified texture patterns. The extracted feature vectors are then clustered and evaluated using majority-label assignment, achieving an overall accuracy of 82.4%, surpassing single-feature baselines (RGB: 82.0%, HSV: 81.3%, histogram: 51.0%, LBP: 68.8%, and GLCM: 73.7%). The results show improved per-artist precision, enhanced boundary-case discrimination, and increased robustness for low-sample categories. Furthermore, the analysis is extended through automatic image captioning and zero-shot style assignment by matching image and text embeddings. The findings demonstrate the effectiveness of machine learning-based image analysis in providing a quantitative and objective approach to identifying and distinguishing visual characteristics of modern and contemporary Korean paintings, offering a novel methodology for art-historical interpretation.

Landslide risk assessment is a critical component of disaster relief operations and sustainable land use planning, as it enables accurate mapping of susceptibility zones. However, conventional landslide detection techniques often fall short due to their labor-intensive nature, subjectivity, and limited accuracy, particularly in regions with inaccessible terrain. To address these challenges, this study introduces an explainable machine learning approach for landslide risk identification. The proposed method employs a multistage fusion architecture that integrates knowledge on potential landslide-inducing factors to improve the classification of various landslide types. Initially, an information gain filter-based feature selection technique is utilized to identify the most relevant features, which are then used to train multiple linear and ensemble classification models. The resulting ensemble model demonstrates enhanced performance in landslide prediction tasks compared to other machine learning models, and its explainability is facilitated through the application of effective visualization techniques. The developed approach offers a promising solution for landslide risk assessment, providing a more accurate and reliable means of identifying susceptible areas and informing informed decision-making in disaster management and land use planning.

The proliferation of social media platforms has led to an exponential growth in text data, making text analysis an indispensable technique for various business applications. As users increasingly engage with social media platforms to share their opinions and sentiments, the analysis of these online comments has become a crucial aspect of understanding public opinion. Sentiment analysis (SA), a subfield of natural language processing, aims to automatically identify and classify opinions expressed in text as positive, negative, or neutral. However, the extraction of high-quality social media data is often hindered by platform-specific APIs, privacy constraints, and data usage limitations. To address this challenge, we propose a novel framework, TSAPM-BERT, which integrates a weighted attention mechanism and sentiment lexicon information with a BERT-based architecture and optimized learning rate to enhance aspect-level sentiment classification. We evaluate the performance of TSAPM-BERT on a benchmark emotion dataset, comparing it to traditional machine learning and deep learning baselines. The experimental results demonstrate that TSAPM-BERT achieves state-of-the-art performance, with a training accuracy of 99.37% and testing accuracy of 98.52%, outperforming competing methods. This study provides a reproducible framework that bridges the gap between high-accuracy sentiment classification and the practical constraints of social media data usage, offering a valuable contribution to the field of sentiment analysis.

This study introduces a comprehensive image dataset for surface defect detection on waterborne painted wood products, specifically designed for deployment in industrial settings. The dataset comprises 13,400 high-resolution images, categorized into four distinct defect types: scratches, cracks, bubbles, and holes, with 3,645, 3,498, 3,256, and 3,001 images, respectively. These images were captured using specialized industrial cameras in a real-world production facility in Jiangshan, China, ensuring a high level of authenticity and relevance. To ensure accuracy, a team of professional annotators rigorously labeled the data. The resulting dataset serves as a critical resource for researchers and practitioners seeking to develop and evaluate deep learning models for multi-class defect detection in industrial assembly lines. By leveraging this dataset, machine learning solutions can be developed to facilitate timely defect detection and remediation, ultimately contributing to the preservation of finish integrity and enhancement of final product quality.

The occurrence of concept drift in machine learning-based systems poses a significant challenge to the reliability and accuracy of pre-trained models. To mitigate this issue, effective drift detection and adaptation mechanisms are essential components in operational systems. Recent research has focused on deep learning-based methods, particularly autoencoders, which have proven versatile in handling various tasks. Autoencoders learn the data distribution and measure the deviation of new data through reconstruction loss, making them a promising approach for drift detection. However, the performance of autoencoder-based drift detection methods heavily relies on the type of autoencoder and the selection of optimal parameters and hyperparameters. This study proposes a framework to optimize the performance of autoencoder-based drift detection methods. We provide a comprehensive evaluation of various autoencoder types, including denoising, variational, and standard autoencoders, and develop a systematic approach to select the best hyperparameters using grid search and Bayesian optimization. Our experiments on eight datasets, comprising four synthetic and four real-world datasets, demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework in identifying the optimal autoencoder configuration with improved drift detection performance. The results highlight the potential of our framework to enhance the robustness and accuracy of machine learning-based systems in operational scenarios.

This study addresses a critical limitation in utilizing social media data for urban and regional analysis, as well as public health monitoring, by developing a classification framework that accurately infers tweet locations from textual and contextual information within the posts themselves. Given that only a small fraction of Twitter posts are geotagged, this approach enables the reliable extraction of location information for a vast majority of tweets. The proposed framework boasts worldwide coverage and dynamically adjusts its spatial granularity to accommodate varying levels of post activity, providing country-level predictions for less active regions and state-level predictions for high-activity zones. A comprehensive evaluation of the algorithm's performance across diverse global regions reveals the impact of various factors on its accuracy. Notably, our analysis indicates that strategically discarding tweets with limited location hints can significantly enhance overall model quality, underscoring the importance of data filtering in improving the effectiveness of location inference. The results of this study contribute to the development of more accurate and robust methods for harnessing social media data in various fields, ultimately enriching our understanding of global phenomena and trends.

In various machine learning applications, the availability and quality of data significantly impact model performance. However, certain domains such as predictive maintenance pose unique challenges due to the scarcity or high cost of data, particularly when dealing with rare events like faults. In these contexts, generating synthetic data can be a valuable resource. Although synthetic data generation has been extensively explored in fields like computer vision and natural language processing, it remains underinvestigated in time series domains. Recent studies have proposed various techniques for time series data augmentation, including geometric transformations, interpolation, and generative models like GANs and VAEs. Notably, the application of denoising models, specifically diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs), has not been thoroughly examined in this area. This research focuses on the development and evaluation of a novel methodology, D3A-TS, which leverages DPMs for time series data augmentation. Additionally, the study investigates the utility of meta-attributes in conditioning the data augmentation process. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of D3A-TS in generating synthetic data for training classification and regression models. To validate the findings, six diverse datasets from different domains were employed, featuring varying input sizes and output types. An extensive ablation study was conducted to further substantiate the outcomes, underscoring the potential of D3A-TS in addressing the challenges of time series data scarcity.

This study explores the integration of artificial intelligence in the creative process of film and television scriptwriting, examining the potential and limitations of using machine learning and natural language processing to generate scripts, construct plots, and develop characters. To address this objective, a deep neural network-based script generation model was developed and trained on a substantial dataset of film and television scripts. The model's performance was evaluated in terms of its ability to produce coherent and well-structured scripts, demonstrating a notable level of creativity and fluency. However, the model's limitations in generating long scripts, handling intricate plotlines, and conveying nuanced emotional expressions were also identified. To overcome these challenges, future research will focus on enhancing the model's capacity for complex plot analysis and emotional expression, as well as optimizing its computational efficiency and output quality. The findings of this study provide a preliminary foundation for the practical application of artificial intelligence in creative industries, offering a novel direction for technological innovation and further exploration in the field of scriptwriting.

This study addresses the pressing need for comprehensive soil moisture data, particularly in deep soil horizons, which is essential for understanding the Earth's energy and water cycles. Existing soil moisture products predominantly focus on surface layers, leaving a significant knowledge gap in the lower soil profiles. To bridge this gap, we developed a novel machine learning-based approach using XGBoost to generate a global, daily, seamless multilayer soil moisture dataset (SWSM) spanning the period 2002-2021. The SWSM dataset offers high-resolution estimates at a 0.05° spatial resolution for three distinct depth horizons: 0-10 cm, 10-30 cm, and 30-60 cm. Our rigorous validation against in situ observations yielded impressive results, with Pearson correlation coefficients exceeding 0.90 and root mean square errors below 0.05 across all depths, indicating the dataset's high accuracy. Furthermore, a feature importance assessment revealed depth-dependent patterns consistent with established hydrological principles, underscoring the dataset's physical consistency. The SWSM dataset, with its extensive temporal coverage, fine spatial resolution, and multi-layer structure, presents a valuable resource for a wide range of applications, including hydrologic modeling, agricultural water management, and climate change research.

This study introduces a novel machine learning dataset, ML-TWiX, which offers a comprehensive record of global monthly total water storage anomalies (TWSA) spanning from 1980 to 2012, with a spatial resolution of 0.5° × 0.5°. To address the limitations of existing satellite-based TWSA records, which are restricted to a relatively short period, we leverage machine learning techniques to reconstruct TWSA for the pre-satellite era. Employing an ensemble of three machine learning models - Random Forest, XGBoost, and Gaussian Process Regression - we trained the models on a combination of global hydrological and land surface model simulations. Each model was used independently to predict TWSA, and the resulting outputs were combined through ensemble averaging to generate a unified product with spatially explicit uncertainty estimates. The accuracy of ML-TWiX was validated against multiple independent datasets, including satellite laser ranging, water mass balance closure, and global mean sea level budget estimates. The resulting dataset provides a continuous and reliable reconstruction of global TWSA, offering valuable insights for hydrological, climate, and water resource assessments, and facilitating a more comprehensive understanding of long-term climate and hydrological processes.

The rapidly changing nature of the modern workforce poses a significant challenge in bridging the gap between the skills taught in educational institutions and the demands of the industry. As industries continue to evolve, there is a pressing need for up-to-date knowledge and competencies among the workforce. This study proposes a machine learning-driven framework to dynamically match industry skill requirements with educational curricula and generate adaptive course recommendations. A comprehensive dataset is compiled from a variety of sources, including job postings, industry reports, company career pages, and labor market analysis, to capture the latest skill demands across multiple sectors. An advanced machine learning algorithm, incorporating a Dynamic Termite Life Cycle Optimizer-driven Euclidean-Support Vector Machine (DTLC-Euclidean SVM), is employed to predict both current and emerging skill demands in the job market. By analyzing patterns in graduate skillsets and employer requirements, the system identifies gaps and aligns educational offerings accordingly. The framework's dynamic matching algorithm utilizes similarity metrics and k-Means Clustering techniques to map industry needs to existing course content, while an automated course generation module suggests new or updated courses to address identified skill shortages. The framework's performance metrics, including accuracy, precision, recall, and f1-score, demonstrate its robustness and reliability in bridging the skill gap. This data-driven approach enables continuous curriculum adaptation, fostering stronger alignment between academia and industry, and supports educators and policymakers in developing responsive, targeted educational programs that prepare students for real-world career opportunities, enhancing employability and addressing the demands of a rapidly evolving labor market.

The evaluation of Physical Education (PE) teaching effectiveness is a critical aspect of promoting students' physical development, engagement, and lifelong health outcomes. However, conventional assessment methods often struggle to capture the complexities of PE instruction due to subjectivity and limited scope. This study aims to develop a comprehensive framework for evaluating PE teaching effectiveness using Multimodal Machine Learning (MML) to analyze diverse educational data sources. A multimodal dataset was compiled, incorporating video and audio recordings of instructional delivery, physical movement patterns captured through wearable sensors and motion capture technology, and student feedback. To ensure data quality and consistency, two preprocessing techniques were employed: wavelet denoising to clean sensor signals and z-score normalization to standardize data across modalities. The Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG) feature extraction method was used to capture detailed movement patterns and posture characteristics from video sources, generating a spatial representation of physical activities. The proposed assessment framework, Enhanced Marine Predators-driven Explainable Boosting Machine (EMP-EBM), leverages the strengths of EMP's global optimization and EBM's transparent structure to provide a hybrid model. The results demonstrate the EMP-EBM's superior performance, achieving an accuracy of 98.07%, precision of 97.85%, recall of 98.31%, and F1-score of 98.07%. This study contributes to the application of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in education by offering a more objective, adaptive, and multifaceted evaluation approach for PE teaching effectiveness.

Advances in non-contact vital sign monitoring have the potential to revolutionize patient care in clinical settings by reducing the need for invasive and time-consuming procedures. However, the development of reliable and accurate monitoring systems for multiple patients simultaneously remains a significant technological challenge. To address this issue, we have constructed a comprehensive radar-based dataset for testing and evaluating methods for multi-patient vital sign monitoring. In collaboration with the Ostrava University Training Hospital, our dataset comprises recordings from advanced medical simulation mannequins, simulating a wide range of physiological states, from normal resting conditions to life-threatening emergencies. The dataset features diverse sensor configurations, including varying angles and distances, as well as different radar parameter settings, resulting in over 100 unique experimental sessions. Our preliminary analysis of beamforming-based processing techniques demonstrates promising results, with mean absolute errors of approximately 6.6 beats per minute for heart rate and 1.47 beats per minute for respiratory rate, nearing clinical thresholds. This dataset, fully documented and publicly accessible in accordance with FAIR principles, provides a valuable resource for researchers to develop and refine advanced signal processing and machine learning pipelines, ultimately contributing to the development of non-invasive multi-patient monitoring systems.

This study addresses the pressing issue of intraoperative acquired pressure injury (IAPI) in elderly cardiac surgery patients, a significant concern due to their compromised skin integrity and reduced blood flow. The accurate prediction of IAPI risk is crucial for implementing targeted preventive measures and personalized care strategies. To develop a reliable risk prediction model, we leveraged a comprehensive dataset of 12,222 patients aged 60 years and older from the MIMIC-IV database, incorporating 24 clinically relevant features. A stacking ensemble of seven machine learning classifiers was trained and evaluated using stratified fivefold cross-validation with 10 repetitions and bootstrapped confidence intervals (2,000 resamples) to quantify uncertainty from missing data. Multiple imputations (m = 2000) were employed to address missing data, and external validation was conducted on an independent dataset of 355 patients from Nanjing First Hospital, with resampling to mitigate class imbalance. The ensemble model demonstrated robust performance in both internal (AUC = 0.857) and external (AUC = 0.783) validation, with stable performance across imputations (mean AUC: 0.997 ± 0.004). Calibration analysis revealed good agreement between predicted and observed risks, and key predictors such as atrial fibrillation, lymphocyte count, and operative time were identified as clinically plausible and robustly associated with IAPI risk. This study presents a validated and interpretable ensemble learning framework for early IAPI risk prediction in elderly cardiac surgery patients, offering a clinically actionable tool for perioperative risk stratification and prevention.

Diabetes has become a pressing global health concern, affecting millions worldwide. Early and accurate prediction of diabetes is critical for preventing severe complications and enhancing patient outcomes. Machine learning techniques, particularly regression-based approaches, have emerged as valuable tools for modeling intricate relationships between risk factors and disease progression. Recent advancements in feature selection, regularization, and ensemble methods have significantly improved the predictive capabilities of these algorithms, enabling more precise and personalized healthcare interventions. However, the proliferation of diverse regression techniques, datasets, and evaluation metrics has created a challenging landscape for fair comparisons. This study aims to address this issue by conducting a comprehensive analysis of current trends in diabetes prediction, focusing on regression models. The primary objectives of this research are to investigate the performance and evolution of regression-based machine learning techniques, develop a standardized framework for evaluating and benchmarking diabetes prediction models, assess the effectiveness of leading regression algorithms in this domain, and identify future research directions that prioritize the development of robust, explainable, and patient-centric prediction models. By shedding light on the current state of diabetes prediction and outlining a path forward, this study seeks to inform the development of more effective and reliable machine learning models for clinical applications.

Early detection of Alzheimer's Disease (AD) is crucial for effective intervention and management. This study presents a novel approach to AD diagnosis using Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) scans, combining the efficiency of SqueezeNet, a lightweight convolutional neural network, with ensemble stacking of machine learning models. Leveraging a comprehensive dataset of 3,382 images categorized into four classes (Mild, Moderate, Very Mild Dementia, and Non-Dementia), SqueezeNet extracts relevant features while minimizing computational requirements. A 10-fold cross-validated ensemble stacking model integrates predictions from Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs) with varying architectures, XGBoost, Cat Boost, and AdaBoost, enhancing robustness and accuracy. The proposed framework achieves exceptional performance, with an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 94.9% and accuracy of 89%, surpassing individual models and addressing class imbalance through rigorous validation. The SqueezeNet architecture's fire module enables efficient computation with reduced parameters, making it suitable for resource-constrained clinical environments. The model's performance is evaluated using F1-score, recall, accuracy, and precision metrics, demonstrating the superiority of the stacking model over single classifiers. This innovative framework provides a scalable, reliable, and cost-effective solution for real-time AD detection and classification in clinical settings.

In the realm of malware classification, high-dimensional feature spaces pose significant obstacles to machine learning performance. This study undertakes a comparative assessment of four dimensionality-reduction techniques to mitigate these challenges. The evaluated methods include Principal Component Analysis (PCA), Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection (UMAP), and Autoencoder-based reduction, applied to opcode-frequency representations of malware. Utilizing a comprehensive dataset comprising 82,569 samples and 1796 opcodes, we investigate the impact of each reduction technique across multiple target dimensions and two classifier architectures: Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) and a three-layer Multilayer Perceptron (MLP). Our results indicate that LDA demonstrates strong separability at lower dimensions, while PCA excels at higher dimensions where variance preservation is crucial. Conversely, Autoencoder-based reduction consistently yields high accuracy with compact representations, whereas UMAP shows limited benefits for tabular opcode data. The findings of this study highlight the trade-offs between linear and non-linear reduction strategies, offering valuable insights for selecting efficient feature compression methods in large-scale malware analysis.

This study addresses the pressing need for cost-effective methods to determine zygosity in twin research, particularly in cases where genetic testing is not feasible. By leveraging machine learning techniques, we aim to develop a scalable framework for predicting zygosity based on physical similarity features, thereby reducing the reliance on expensive genetic testing. Our dataset consists of 5,077 Iranian twin pairs, collected from the Iranian School-aged Twin Registry between 2018 and 2021. To mitigate the class imbalance issue, we employed the Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) to enhance the representation of minority classes. We trained and evaluated eight machine learning algorithms, including K-nearest neighbors, support vector machine, logistic regression, random forest, decision tree, and boosting techniques such as Gradient Boosting Classifier, XGBoost, and AdaBoost Classifier, on an 80:20 train-test split. Model hyperparameters were optimized using GridSearchCV, and performance was assessed using multiple metrics. The results indicate that XGBoost achieved the highest accuracy of 85.57% and F1-score of 86.72% after optimization, outperforming other algorithms. Notably, XGBoost demonstrated comparable performance to Logistic Regression and Gradient Boosting, but its marginal lead and interpretability made it a preferred choice. The SHAP algorithm was used to identify the most influential physical traits contributing to zygosity prediction. Our study establishes XGBoost with SMOTE oversampling as a reliable framework for twin zygosity prediction, paving the way for future external validation and comparison with genetic testing results.

Epileptic seizure recognition from electroencephalogram (EEG) data is a crucial aspect of early diagnosis and clinical decision-making in neurology. However, the sharing of sensitive patient data across institutions raises significant privacy concerns, particularly in centralized machine learning approaches. To address these concerns, this study presents a novel framework for privacy-preserving epileptic seizure recognition using federated and explainable machine learning techniques. A publicly available EEG-derived dataset was utilized, comprising five original classes that were reformulated into a binary classification task distinguishing seizure and non-seizure events. To prevent information leakage, the dataset was partitioned at the patient level, with separate training and testing sets created. A simulated federated learning environment was established, comprising five clients with both independent and non-independent data distributions, and executed over fifty communication rounds using the Federated Averaging strategy. A range of machine learning models, including Support Vector Machine, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, and K Nearest Neighbors, were evaluated using standardized hyperparameter configurations. Model performance was assessed using accuracy, area under the ROC curve, confusion matrices, and learning curves. To provide insight into model decision-making, SHAP and LIME were employed as explainability methods. The results demonstrate that federated learning can achieve competitive performance while preserving data privacy, and that explainable methods offer valuable transparency in seizure classification decisions. This study serves as a proof-of-concept evaluation, highlighting the methodological feasibility of this approach rather than its clinical deployment.

The proliferation of digital documents has led to an increased reliance on tables as a fundamental format for structured data representation across various sectors, including healthcare, education, and finance. However, the conversion of table content from images into editable and machine-readable formats remains a challenging task, hindering processing efficiency and accuracy. Current approaches, such as those based on convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and Transformers, have made significant strides but still struggle to address complex table layouts, diverse formats, and high computational costs. To address these limitations, we introduce the Spatial Pyramid Pooling-enhanced Multi-modal Linear Transformer (SPPLTR), a novel table recognition model that integrates spatial pyramid pooling with a causal linear Transformer framework. The proposed model incorporates a spatial pyramid context fusion module to capture both structural and textual information in table images, and a causal linear Transformer to enhance understanding of table structures. Furthermore, SPPLTR incorporates a multi-head causal linear attention mechanism with automatic mixed precision support, facilitating efficient and stable inference. Experimental results on the PubTabNet dataset demonstrate that SPPLTR achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming existing models across various evaluation metrics. Our model presents a promising solution for table recognition tasks and lays the groundwork for future advancements in structured document understanding.

This study addresses the critical need for reliable icing detection in wind turbine systems operating in cold climates, where missed events can compromise structural integrity. A novel intelligent information fusion framework is proposed, integrating vibration-based structural dynamics with environmental data through an automated pipeline of Operational Modal Analysis (OMA) and temperature compensation. To evaluate the framework's efficacy, five machine learning classification architectures—Optimized Threshold, Random Forest, XGBoost, Support Vector Machine, and Gated Recurrent Unit—were rigorously tested using rolling-window time-series cross-validation on high-fidelity experimental data from a large-scale climate chamber. The framework prioritizes recall to minimize false negative errors, ensuring the safety-critical nature of the application, and incorporates non-parametric statistical validation to quantify model stability. Results indicate that a physics-informed approach, specifically the Optimized Threshold model, achieved the highest operational reliability, outperforming more complex ensemble and deep learning methods in both safety and statistical stability. This study demonstrates that meticulous feature-level fusion and domain-specific engineering can enhance system resilience more effectively than increased algorithmic complexity, providing a scalable foundation for multi-sensor fusion and adaptive predictive maintenance in renewable energy infrastructures.

This study addresses the pressing issue of machine-generated text on social media platforms, particularly in the context of deepfake content, which poses significant challenges for maintaining trust and preventing the spread of misinformation. The identification of such content is crucial for mitigating the negative consequences of disinformation. To combat this issue, we develop an intelligent framework for detecting machine-generated textual content on social media platforms. Our framework leverages the DeBERTaV3 model, combined with the TweepFake and PHEME datasets, to analyze tweets and social media posts and determine whether they were created by human or bot accounts. The efficacy of our proposed model is evaluated against a range of deep learning frameworks, including BERT, RoBERTa, SVM-RBF, random forest, SVM, CNN, and LSTM. The results demonstrate that our model achieves a high accuracy of 97.12% in fake news detection, outperforming other models in terms of efficiency and effectiveness. The experimental findings highlight the importance of the DeBERTa architecture's design, data preprocessing techniques, and embedding methods in facilitating accurate tweet classification and bot detection. Furthermore, our study evaluates the scalability and computational efficiency of fake news detection, providing insights into the potential applications and limitations of our proposed framework.

This study investigates the performance of artificial intelligence (AI) credit-scoring models in comparison to traditional statistical models using a comprehensive dataset of 1000 real-life loan applications. The primary objective is to determine whether machine learning methods offer superior predictive accuracy, interpretability, and stability in the face of macroeconomic downturns compared to conventional approaches. Four distinct models were developed: logistic regression and decision trees as examples of classical methods, and XGBoost gradient-boosting ensemble and multilayer perceptron neural networks as AI-based alternatives. Utilizing the R programming environment (version 4.3.1), a 70:30 train-test split was employed, along with inner cross-validation and a harmony search algorithm to optimize hyperparameters. The results indicate that XGBoost achieved the highest cumulative gain curve (0.89) and area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (0.89), outperforming the neural network (CGC: 0.87; AUC: 0.87) and logistic regression (CGC: 0.76; AUC: 0.76). SHAP analysis revealed that credit amount, loan duration, and age are key predictors. Furthermore, the XGBoost model demonstrated stability during stress-testing simulations, maintaining an AUC of 0.83, whereas logistic regression showed a significant decline to 0.68. These findings support the notion that cutting-edge AI tools possess superior predictive capabilities and interpretability, making them viable alternatives to traditional credit-scoring systems in modern financial institutions.

This study examines the effectiveness of GPT-4 in classifying imaging protocols based on text-based clinical indications, comparing its performance to that of human experts and established fine-tuned models. A comprehensive evaluation was conducted on GPT-4, a fine-tuned BERT model, and a fine-tuned LLaMA-3 model, using a dataset of 11 head MRI protocol categories. The models' predictions were assessed against expert-validated ground truth labels using F1 scores, and their reasoning coherence was reviewed by a board-certified radiologist to evaluate interpretability. Notably, GPT-4 achieved a moderate F1 score of 0.83, lagging behind the 0.91, 0.93, and 0.96 scores of BERT, LLaMA-3, and human experts, respectively. However, GPT-4 generated the most human-like explanations, demonstrating a nuanced understanding of clinical language and imaging rationale. Furthermore, when GPT-4 was conditioned with the output of a fine-tuned classifier, its accuracy improved significantly, suggesting that collaborative decision-making between general and specialized models can enhance both performance and transparency. These findings indicate that GPT-4 can provide interpretable text-based outputs that may support clinical decision-making when used with appropriate safeguards, underscoring its potential as a valuable tool in radiology workflows.

This study delves into the molecular underpinnings of clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC), a highly aggressive malignancy linked to genetic mutations in VHL, PBRM1, and SETD2. Despite the significance of single-nucleotide variants, the impact of structural variations (SVs) and three-dimensional (3D) genome architecture on ccRCC progression remains poorly understood. To address this knowledge gap, we conducted a comprehensive multi-omics analysis to identify novel SV-associated oncogenic targets and elucidate the complex interplay between SVs and 3D chromatin architecture in ccRCC. Our results reveal that SVs drive oncogenic dysregulation through 3D genome reorganization, uncovering an uncharacterized pathogenic enhancer hijacking event that leads to the constitutive activation of the proto-oncogene SEMA5B. Furthermore, we developed a machine learning-based prognostic framework using enhancer hijacking signatures, providing a valuable tool for predicting patient outcomes in ccRCC. This research contributes to a deeper understanding of the mechanisms underlying ccRCC and translates these findings into a clinically applicable prognostic model, offering new avenues for the diagnosis and treatment of this aggressive cancer.

Anemia affects a substantial global population, with over one billion individuals worldwide experiencing low hemoglobin levels due to iron or vitamin deficiencies, blood loss, or genetic conditions such as sickle cell anemia and thalassemia. The diagnosis of anemia is often hindered by costly blood tests, limited medical personnel, and inadequate infrastructure in rural areas. Recent advancements in machine learning have enabled the analysis of complete blood count records and images of blood smears, eye conjunctiva, lip mucosa, fingernails, and palms to facilitate anemia detection, potentially eliminating the need for expert healthcare professionals. This study evaluates the efficacy of various artificial intelligence techniques for diagnosing three types of anemia: iron deficiency, sickle cell disease, and thalassemia. By comparing machine learning, deep learning, optimization algorithms, and hybrid approaches in terms of accuracy, key features, research gaps, and future prospects, this research aims to identify the most effective methods for anemia diagnosis. Furthermore, an examination of dataset sizes and sources is conducted to explore any correlations with model accuracy. The integration of artificial intelligence into anemia diagnosis offers a non-invasive, quick, and affordable solution with explainable AI techniques, enhancing physician trust and usability, particularly in remote areas. While traditional invasive tests remain the preferred choice for accurate anemia diagnosis, AI can still be beneficial when integrated into these methods, reducing the burden on healthcare professionals and aiding in diagnostic confirmation.

This study investigates the application of machine learning models in predicting the productivity of housing projects in Iraq, a sector critical to the country's economic development. The objective of this research is to develop and evaluate the predictive capabilities of three advanced machine learning techniques - artificial neural networks (ANN), extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost), and support vector regression (SVR) - using actual data from 67 housing projects in Iraq. Seven input variables, including project design and implementation characteristics, were used to predict a single output variable, represented by the actual implementation productivity of the projects. The evaluation results indicate that the XGBoost model achieved the highest predictive accuracy, with a determination coefficient of R2 = 0.998 and a relative error (MAPE) of 0.8%. In comparison, the ANN model also demonstrated strong predictive performance (R2 = 0.92 and MAPE = 4.1%). Conversely, the SVR model exhibited lower accuracy (R2 = 0.813 and MAPE = 6.6%). The feature importance analysis revealed that built-up area and quantities of brick and concrete works were the most influential factors in determining productivity. The findings suggest that machine learning techniques, specifically XGBoost and ANN, can serve as valuable tools for supporting informed decision-making in the early stages of housing project planning by accurately predicting project performance based on available data.

Advances in machine learning and computational methods have significantly accelerated the discovery of novel materials by enabling accurate predictions of material properties at a reduced cost. However, the primary challenge lies in providing high-quality input data and optimizing model parameters. High-entropy materials, a distinct class of valuable materials, hold great promise in overcoming the limitations of conventional battery materials. By leveraging quantum computing and machine learning algorithms, researchers can design and fabricate new materials for energy storage applications, such as batteries and supercapacitors, prior to chemical synthesis. This study focuses on the intersection of quantum computing and high-entropy materials, particularly high-entropy MXenes, and explores the encoding and decoding of material crystal structures, a crucial aspect in creating a comprehensive database for high-entropy materials. Furthermore, we investigate the application of deep learning algorithms in material discovery, prior to synthesis, and their potential in identifying high-entropy materials suitable for energy storage devices. Our research also examines the potential of emerging quantum computing and artificial intelligence approaches in determining the structure of high-entropy materials, which could revolutionize the field of energy storage and beyond.

This study proposes an integrated artificial intelligence-driven framework for enhancing Management Information Systems (MIS) capabilities in analyzing sustainable supply chain optimization and environmental impact assessment. To address the complex tasks involved, a multi-faceted approach is employed, combining classical machine learning regression models (Linear Regression, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost, SVR) with a deep learning-based Gated TabTransformer (GTT) for predicting greenhouse gas emissions. Additionally, a Variational Graph Autoencoder (VGAE) is utilized for unsupervised anomaly detection, and a hybrid interpretability model is constructed using Explainable Boosting Machines (EBM) and SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) to provide transparent and interpretable insights. The results demonstrate that Linear Regression and ensemble models exhibit superior performance in terms of accuracy and robustness, with notable achievements including an R2 value of 0.9995 and a mean absolute error of 0.0036. The Gated TabTransformer also showcases strong generalization capabilities with an R2 value of 0.9883, while the VGAE effectively identifies anomalies in emission patterns by capturing both topological and feature-level deviations. Furthermore, the proposed framework enables interpretable local and global insights through EBM-SHAP, facilitating data-driven decision making with enhanced transparency. Overall, this comprehensive framework bridges the gap between sustainability, artificial intelligence, and MIS, advancing the state-of-the-art in sustainability analytics for supply chain optimization.

In recent years, the deployment of Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) in military and civilian applications has increased significantly due to their ability to provide real-time data collection and monitoring capabilities. However, their susceptibility to various types of routing attacks, including wormhole, sinkhole, sybil, and black hole attacks, poses a significant threat to their reliability and security. As WSNs consist of numerous small, low-cost devices deployed in exposed and unsecured locations, they are particularly vulnerable to attacks that exploit the unreliability of wireless networks and the susceptibility of sensor nodes. To address this issue, a novel Intrusion Detection System (IDS) is proposed for cluster-based WSNs, where a Cluster Head (CH) is chosen within each cluster and a Group Head (GH) oversees the behavior of nodes and the network. This research presents a Dual Level Node Authentication Model with Node Behaviour Analysis (DLNA-NBA), which utilizes machine learning techniques for accurate intrusion detection in WSNs. The proposed model demonstrates a significant enhancement in intrusion detection capability, achieving 98.9% accuracy in dual-level node authentication and 99.2% accuracy in intrusion detection, outperforming traditional models. The improved node verification, optimized feature utilization, and efficient behavioral pattern analysis ensure secure communication and reduced false alarms in WSN environments, making it an effective solution for securing these networks.

This study investigates the application of machine learning (ML) models for predicting sports-related injuries in professional football players. A dataset of 300 male athletes aged 18-28, monitored over two seasons (2021-2022), was analyzed to identify factors associated with injury risk. Daily data on training workload, recovery, wellness, heart-rate variability, playing time, and injury history were collected and preprocessed using normalization, one-hot encoding, and feature selection via LASSO regression and recursive feature elimination. Missing data were addressed through multiple imputation by chained equations, while class imbalance was corrected using SMOTE and weighting. ML models, including logistic regression, decision tree, and random forest, were trained using 10-fold cross-validation and evaluated for performance metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC. Results showed that random forest models outperformed other models, achieving high accuracy (85.6 ± 2.1%) and AUC (90.5 ± 1.6%) scores. The application of explainable AI techniques, such as SHAP and LIME, revealed that prior injury, training intensity, and recovery time were the strongest predictors of injury risk, enabling individualized risk assessment. These findings highlight the potential of ensemble ML methods for providing actionable insights into injury prevention and optimization of training strategies. Future research should aim to expand the validation of these models across multiple sports and incorporate additional physiological and genetic factors to enhance predictive accuracy and generalizability.
