This study investigates the cost-effectiveness of artificial intelligence in precision medicine (AI-PM) by synthesizing available evidence from economic evaluations. A comprehensive search of five major databases was conducted to identify relevant studies, and data was extracted and assessed for risk-of-bias using the ECOBIAS checklist. Quantitative summaries of cost-utility analyses and regression analyses incorporating machine learning were performed to explore value heterogeneity. The results indicate that AI-PM was found to be cost-saving or cost-effective in 89% of base-case analyses, with incremental cost-effectiveness ratios ranging from dominant to $129,174 per quality-adjusted life-year (QALY). The interquartile ranges of incremental costs, QALY gains, and net monetary benefits suggest modest health gains at minimal additional costs, but also highlight significant value heterogeneity. The study identifies modeling choices and system-level factors as key sources of heterogeneity in estimated net monetary benefits. Furthermore, the analysis reveals low adaptability and underreporting of key value factors, leading to substantial uncertainties in the adoption of AI-PM. These findings have important implications for healthcare decision-making and highlight the need for further research to address the complexities surrounding AI-PM value assessment.

This study aimed to develop a predictive model for postoperative sepsis following upper urinary tract stone surgery using machine learning (ML) and the Shapley Additive Explanations (SHAP) algorithm. Sepsis is a significant cause of perioperative mortality in this patient population, necessitating the development of accurate risk stratification tools. We analyzed data from 7,464 patients, incorporating 155 pre- and postoperative features, to identify key variables associated with urosepsis. Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator (LASSO) regression and correlation analysis were employed to select the most relevant features. Eight ML algorithms were utilized to develop the predictive model, which was evaluated using 10-fold cross-validation, Receiver Operating Characteristic curve, learning curve, and decision curve. The SHAP algorithm was applied to determine variable importance. Our results indicate that 116 out of 155 variables differed significantly between the urosepsis and non-urosepsis groups, with eight predictive variables identified: postoperative IL-6, SAA, PCT/ALB, NLPR, PT, ALB, HCT, and neutrophil. Light Gradient Boosting Machine achieved the best performance, with AUCs of 1.0, 0.90, and 0.88 for the training, validation, and test cohorts, respectively. The model demonstrated good fit, strong calibration, and positive clinical utility. The SHAP analysis revealed postoperative PCT/ALB, neutrophil, IL-6, ALB, and PT as key predictors. A publicly accessible web application was developed to facilitate risk stratification and personalized intervention in clinical practice. This study demonstrates the development and validation of a robust, interpretable model for predicting postoperative sepsis, offering a practical tool for early risk stratification and intervention in patients undergoing upper urinary tract stone surgery.

The increasing adoption of artificial intelligence (AI) in higher education has led to a shift from voluntary engagement to mandatory usage, resulting in psychological and emotional strain among students. Traditional technology adoption models often overlook the challenges posed by compulsory digital environments, where system design flaws can cause cognitive overload and student resistance. This study aims to develop and validate a student-centered framework that examines the impact of AI system design on students' negative technological experiences, and how these experiences subsequently influence techno-resistance and academic performance. The framework focuses on three key design aspects: system reliability, task routinization, and information richness. A quantitative research design was employed, utilizing data collected from 229 undergraduate students across Malaysian universities. Exploratory factor analysis, correlation analysis, and multiple regression were used to investigate relationships among key constructs and validate the proposed model. The results indicate that system reliability and information richness significantly reduce students' negative experiences, whereas task routinization has no significant effect. Notably, negative technological experience emerged as a key mediator, amplifying techno-resistance and decreasing academic performance. These findings suggest that the mandatory implementation of AI technologies does not automatically translate into improved educational outcomes. This study contributes to the understanding of techno-resistance as an adaptive response to cognitive and emotional strain resulting from poorly designed AI systems in compulsory educational settings, and offers practical implications for the design of more student-aligned AI educational technologies.

This study investigates the potential of machine learning algorithms in simulating quantum many-body systems, a task often attributed to quantum computers. Recent research has demonstrated the capability of classical machine learning algorithms to predict various properties of quantum systems using classical data, raising questions about the necessity of quantum computing for such tasks. While previous studies have proposed quantum advantages for specific learning tasks, these advantages are largely limited to cryptographic functions and do not reflect real-world laboratory scenarios. In contrast, this work focuses on a physically relevant task: learning quantum observables from classical data. We demonstrate quantum advantages for two types of observables: linear combinations of Pauli strings and unitarily parametrized observables. Our results establish clear boundaries between tasks that can be efficiently solved using classical learning and those that require quantum resources. By assuming only that BQP-hard processes cannot be simulated by polynomial-size classical circuits, we derive classical hardness results and provide a non-trivial quantum learning algorithm. Our findings provide insight into the utility of quantum resources for learning problems in quantum many-body physics, suggesting practical avenues for potential quantum learning improvements.

Recent advancements in generative adversarial networks (GANs) have significantly impacted the field of deep learning, with their ability to generate high-fidelity synthetic data garnering substantial attention. As a pivotal framework in modern machine learning, GANs have undergone numerous architectural refinements to enhance performance and training stability. Notable models such as DCGAN, WGAN, StyleGAN, and BigGAN have improved the quality and diversity of generated data while addressing the inherent challenges of the training process. GANs are now widely applied across various domains, including high-resolution image synthesis, speech generation, medical data reconstruction, educational data simulation, and style transfer. Despite these achievements, persistent challenges persist, including convergence instability, mode collapse, sensitivity to hyperparameters, and the difficulty of evaluating synthetic data quality. Furthermore, the integration of GANs with reinforcement learning, attention-based architectures, and large language models (LLMs) is an emerging area of research, aiming to generate meaningful, stable, and user-aligned content. This study provides a comprehensive analysis of 45 distinct GAN architectures, categorizing them by learning paradigm, application domain, network design, associated datasets, and open challenges. The framework offers a coherent perspective on current trends and future opportunities in the GAN landscape, providing researchers and practitioners with a valuable resource for navigating the evolving field.

Urban recommender systems are increasingly pivotal in shaping the delivery of equitable, efficient, and citizen-centric urban services as cities integrate pervasive sensing, data platforms, and artificial intelligence. This comprehensive review synthesizes a diverse range of peer-reviewed research across six key urban domains: mobility, healthcare, energy, tourism, retail, and e-governance. By examining the application of collaborative filtering, content-based methods, hybrid designs, deep learning, and context-aware approaches to urban decision-making, our study reveals a taxonomy that highlights the varying levels of adoption across these domains. Notably, mobility and tourism have been the primary focus of empirical studies, whereas deep and graph-based techniques remain underutilized in governance and energy sectors. Our analysis demonstrates how the integration of heterogeneous data sources, including IoT streams, geospatial signals, environmental indicators, and demographic attributes, can support personalized recommendations under constraints such as latency, reliability, and data privacy. The review also highlights recent advances in addressing sparsity and cold start issues through graph neural models, sequence modeling, and transfer learning, as well as operational enablers such as edge inference and streaming architectures for real-time recommendation. Furthermore, we examine the risk and governance dimensions of urban recommender systems, including privacy preservation, fairness, exposure balance, explainability, and mechanisms for audit and oversight. Our study identifies opportunities for the application of recommender systems in pollution management, citizen education, and participatory platforms that enhance civic engagement. Additionally, we outline the potential of emerging technologies such as hybrid physical-virtual interactions, digital twins, immersive interfaces, generative models, and quantum algorithms to transform personalization and oversight in city-scale settings. Finally, we emphasize the need for field evaluations and standardized benchmarks that jointly assess accuracy, latency, robustness to distribution shift, and equity in urban recommender systems.

Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a complex neurodegenerative disorder with a multifaceted pathophysiology, where the interplay between immune and metabolic processes remains poorly understood. Recent genome-wide association studies have implicated various immune and metabolic pathways in AD, but the specific cellular mechanisms underlying these associations are still unclear. To elucidate the relationship between AD and immunometabolism, we employed a bidirectional two-sample Mendelian randomization approach to identify single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP)-mediated associations between AD and distinct immunometabolic signatures. Our analysis revealed a complex interplay between immune cell types and metabolic pathways, with both positively and negatively correlated components. Notably, single-cell omics analysis of patient brains uncovered distinct astrocyte subpopulations, one of which was enriched for glutamate-glutamine uptake and metabolism, and was positively associated with AD, while another was characterized by amino acid metabolism and transport, and was negatively associated. In contrast, peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) primarily displayed AD-negative metabolic signatures accompanied by downregulated immune responses. Building on these findings, we developed and optimized a blood transcriptome-based AD prediction model using a gene set derived from blood immune cells that is negatively associated with AD. This model, applicable to both European and Asian populations, enables pre-symptomatic detection of familial AD, effectively discriminates AD from other neurodegenerative disorders, and is amenable to clinical implementation. Our study provides novel insights into the critical role of immunometabolism in AD and offers a practical predictive tool for large-scale, routine population screening.

This study aimed to develop machine learning models for predicting the optimal dosage in strabismus surgery, specifically for horizontal non-paretic strabismus, based on preoperative characteristics. To achieve this, a comprehensive retrospective analysis of patients undergoing strabismus surgery at the University Clinic of Ulm between 2003 and 2022 was conducted. A refined dataset of 767 patients was selected for further analysis. A multilayer perceptron was employed using the PyTorch neural network framework to evaluate the data and build a predictive model. Explainable artificial intelligence techniques were applied to identify the most influential features contributing to the predictions. The results showed that the Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno algorithm yielded the highest number of accurate predictions within the acceptable range. The root mean square error for both labels was 0.54 mm for L1 and 0.71 mm for L2, with 69% and 55% of the data falling within the acceptable range, respectively. The preoperative angle of deviation was found to be the most critical feature for dosage predictions, followed by near angle of deviation, simultaneous cover test, Hirschberg test angle, and refraction of own glasses used. The developed neural network demonstrates potential in assisting surgeons in making informed decisions regarding dosage in strabismus surgery, with future improvements expected with an expanded dataset.

The proliferation of fake news on social media poses significant threats to civic trust and public safety, with emotionally charged content facilitating its rapid dissemination through retweets, replies, and shares. To combat this issue, we focus on identifying anomalous propagation subgraphs indicative of coordinated misinformation campaigns. Our proposed framework, Multi-Graph Neural Network Attention-based Propagation Learning for Emotion-Aware Anomaly Detection (MAPLE), integrates sentiment features from users' historical posts with network structures. By combining multiple Graph Neural Networks (GCN, GAT, and GraphSAGE) through an attention-based fusion mechanism, MAPLE captures the complex interplay between user behavior and network dynamics. To detect anomalies, subgraph embeddings are evaluated using a One-Class Support Vector Machine. Theoretical analyses demonstrate the framework's ability to preserve mutual information, reduce variance, amplify anomaly margins, and maximize entropy. Experimental evaluations on the PolitiFact and GossipCop datasets reveal that MAPLE outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving F1-score improvements of 43% and 2.17% respectively, while maintaining robustness across datasets. By integrating emotional context and theoretical foundations, MAPLE offers a unified multi-Graph Neural Network fusion framework for subgraph anomaly detection, addressing a critical gap in existing research that often treats structural or sentiment cues in isolation.

High-dimensional datasets often pose significant challenges in machine learning, as they frequently contain redundant or irrelevant features that increase computational complexity and hinder classifier generalization. Inefficient handling of such data can lead to suboptimal performance and reduced model reliability. Traditional binary metaheuristics, which rely on static control parameters, are prone to premature convergence and lack adaptability, limiting their effectiveness in complex optimization tasks. To address these limitations, this study introduces the Dynamic Binary Swordfish Movement Optimization Algorithm (DBSMOA), a novel binary optimizer inspired by the foraging dynamics of swordfish. DBSMOA extends the original Swordfish Movement Optimization Algorithm (SMOA) by incorporating dynamic behavioral adaptation mechanisms that strike a balance between exploration and exploitation throughout the optimization process. The algorithm employs a sigmoid-based probabilistic mapping and dynamically reassigns agent roles based on performance-driven elitism to maintain diversity and stability during search. Experimental evaluations on 52 benchmark datasets demonstrate that DBSMOA achieves superior performance, outperforming twelve state-of-the-art binary optimizers in terms of classification accuracy, feature subset size, and computational efficiency. The results highlight DBSMOA's robustness, scalability, and suitability for high-dimensional feature selection and other binary optimization tasks, underscoring its potential as a reliable and efficient optimization tool.

This study aimed to develop a non-invasive machine learning model utilizing routine blood biomarkers to assess the risk of high-grade cervical intraepithelial neoplasia (CIN2/3) and potentially serve as a triage tool before colposcopy. Current cervical cancer screening methods face challenges in accessibility and invasiveness, particularly in resource-limited settings. A comprehensive dataset of 248 patients, comprising 128 high-grade CIN and 120 low-grade CIN cases, was analyzed. Twenty-nine clinical characteristics and blood test measurements were considered for model development, and four feature selection algorithms were employed to identify key predictors. Eleven machine learning algorithms were used for model training, with the dataset split into training and testing cohorts. Model performance was evaluated using a range of metrics, including learning curves, receiver operating characteristic curves, area under the curve, Brier score, calibration curves, precision-recall curves, and decision curve analysis. The Support Vector Machine algorithm achieved the best predictive performance, with an area under the curve of 0.75 (95% confidence interval: 0.69-0.80) and a Brier score of 0.21 (95% confidence interval: 0.17-0.28). The model's feature importance was assessed using the SHapley Additive exPlanation approach, which identified creatinine, red blood cell count, neutrophil ratio, direct bilirubin, and monocyte count as key predictors. A web-based calculator was developed for clinical deployment, enabling real-time risk stratification. The results suggest that this machine learning model has strong potential for clinical utility in assessing the risk of high-grade CIN, particularly in resource-limited settings.

This study addresses the complex challenge of designing functional materials with optimized multiple interdependent properties, a crucial requirement for various applications. In the context of boron-doped graphene (BDG), both the band gap and work function are critical factors influencing its performance in electronic and catalytic applications. However, existing machine learning approaches primarily focus on predicting a single property and rely on manually engineered features, thereby limiting their applicability. To overcome this limitation, we propose an adaptive edge-aware graph convolutional neural network with multi-task learning (AEGCNN-MTL) for simultaneous prediction of multiple material properties. Our method was evaluated on a dataset of 2613 DFT-computed BDG structures, yielding high accuracy (R² = 0.9905 for band gap and 0.9778 for work function) and outperforming single-task graph neural network baselines under identical training budgets. Furthermore, when applied to the QM9 benchmark, AEGCNN-MTL demonstrated strong generalization capability by delivering competitive performance across 12 diverse quantum chemical properties. These findings underscore the potential of AEGCNN-MTL as a scalable and accurate tool for high-throughput, multi-property screening and the data-driven discovery of multifunctional materials.

This study introduces a pioneering approach to generating a comprehensive fingerprint for crystalline materials, striking a balance between computational efficiency and human interpretability. The proposed fingerprint consists of two distinct components: a structural representation capturing the crystal lattice arrangement and a compositional component, referred to as Pettifor embedding, which characterizes the chemical makeup of the material. The Pettifor embedding is constructed in a non-orthogonal space where each axis corresponds to a chemical element, and the angles between axes quantify the similarity between elements. This unique encoding enables the representation of chemical composition as a point on a unit sphere within this space. Our results demonstrate that Pettifor embeddings significantly outperform conventional elemental embeddings in machine learning models focused on compositional analysis. By leveraging the Pettifor embeddings to define a distance metric and applying dimensionality reduction techniques, we develop a two-dimensional global map of thermodynamically stable crystalline compounds. This map, despite its simplicity, effectively separates material classes based on fundamental physical properties, providing valuable insights into the structure-property relationships of crystalline materials.

This study addresses the pressing need for high-resolution and consistent monitoring of carbon, water, and energy fluxes in terrestrial ecosystems, particularly in fragmented landscapes such as UK agricultural areas. These ecosystems play a crucial role in regulating the climate by absorbing approximately one-third of anthropogenic CO2 emissions. To bridge the existing knowledge gap, we developed the Unified FLUXes (UFLUX) ensemble, a comprehensive dataset of gross primary productivity, evapotranspiration, and sensible heat fluxes. UFLUX integrates eddy covariance data, satellite observations, and machine learning techniques to provide a globally consistent dataset across multiple spatial and temporal scales. The ensemble comprises approximately 60 members, spanning global (monthly, 0.25°), European (daily, 0.25°; biannual, 100 m), and UK (daily, 100 m) scales. Our results show that UFLUX effectively captures over 80% of flux variability, with low mean absolute errors, and accurately reproduces climate responses and interannual patterns consistent with existing literature. Although uncertainties in net carbon flux remain, UFLUX holds significant potential for supporting climate policymaking and land management decisions, ultimately contributing to the pursuit of a carbon-neutral future.

The proliferation of multiplayer gaming has sparked a relentless arms race between anticheat and cheat developers, necessitating the development of robust anti-cheat mechanisms to ensure fair play. Traditional anti-cheat methods, such as signature-based detection and memory scanning, have been widely employed but have proven inadequate against novel and adaptive threats. These conventional techniques rely on static signatures and rule-based approaches, which can be easily evaded by sophisticated cheats. In contrast, modern AI-driven solutions, including computer vision-based aimbot detection and behavioral analysis using deep learning models, offer proactive and adaptive capabilities that can detect and counter zero-day threats. However, existing literature lacks a comprehensive framework that integrates the strengths of conventional techniques with the dynamic detection power of AI. This study addresses this gap by proposing a novel hybrid security framework model that leverages the efficiency of signature-based detection for known exploits while harnessing the adaptive capabilities of AI for zero-day threats. The proposed architecture combines cryptographic technologies such as Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs) for process isolation and Hierarchical Identity-Based Encryption (HIBE) for secure authentication, creating a resilient, multilayered defense. The findings of this study provide a technical reference and architectural blueprint for designing the next generation of secure, cheat-resistant online gaming systems, with potential applications extending to other high-stakes, high-risk environments.

The optimization of sintered neodymium-iron-boron (NdFeB) magnets is crucial for high-performance applications, yet their complex structure-property relationships and limited data hinder efficient optimization. To address this challenge, we have compiled a comprehensive database of 1994 industrial and academic samples, enabling a systematic evaluation of active learning (AL) strategies on both classical and quantum-enhanced regressors. Our analysis reveals distinct design heuristics between industrial and academic data, underscoring the importance of domain-aware approaches. Furthermore, we develop a novel methodological framework for integrating quantum kernel regression into an AL framework, leveraging bootstrapped ensembles to quantify uncertainty. Our results demonstrate that the effectiveness of AL is contingent upon the choice of model, with significant acceleration observed in certain cases (Random Forest, Support Vector Regression) and diminished or even detrimental effects in others (XGBoost, quantum-enhanced Support Vector Regression). These findings provide critical insights for strategic machine learning applications in materials discovery, highlighting the need for careful model selection and tailored approaches to maximize the benefits of AL.

This study explores the application of machine learning techniques to model and predict Energetic Intelligence, a multidimensional construct encompassing emotional, spiritual, and physical aspects of human intelligence. By leveraging data commonly collected in organizational settings, our research aims to develop predictive models that can accurately forecast Energetic Intelligence. We employed a data-driven approach, collecting survey responses from a diverse sample and utilizing a range of supervised learning algorithms to identify key predictors. The performance of our models was evaluated using standard metrics, with the most effective models achieving an R2 score of 0.73 through a process of optimization and simplification. Notably, variables such as Flow, Flourishing, and Emotional Vitality emerged as critical features in model training, underscoring their significance in predicting Energetic Intelligence. Our findings highlight the potential of machine learning to support psychological research and provide practical tools for assessing and developing Energetic Intelligence in real-world settings. By integrating AI methodologies with psychological theory, our work demonstrates the value of data-driven insights into human potential and well-being, offering a promising avenue for future research and application.

The increasing reliance on integrated circuits (ICs) in cyber-physical systems has raised concerns about their vulnerability to malicious hardware attacks, particularly hardware Trojans. These stealthy threats are malicious implants in the circuitry that remain dormant until triggered, potentially causing functional disruptions or sensitive information leakage. Traditional IC validation methods often fail to detect Trojans as they are designed to mimic normal circuit behavior and exhibit minimal changes in power consumption, timing, and area. To address this challenge, we propose a novel dual-domain feature extraction strategy that combines time-domain features with frequency-domain characteristics of power traces. This approach is integrated into a robust Trojan detection framework that leverages both traditional machine learning models, such as random forest, gradient boosting, and naïve bayes, and deep learning models, including deep neural network, long short-term memory, and graph neural network. A stacked ensemble classifier is employed to combine the strengths of these six baseline models, enabling the detection of Trojan-infected circuits via side-channel power analysis. Our evaluation on the advanced encryption standard (AES)-Trojan benchmark demonstrates that the ensemble-based detection method outperforms the individual baseline models, achieving a macro-averaged area under the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve (AUC) of 0.987. This approach remains golden-chip-free, relying solely on observable characteristics of untrusted chips, such as side-channel emissions, to detect anomalies.

Cardiovascular disease (CVD) is a major global health concern, necessitating the development of efficient and accurate prediction methods. Traditional diagnostic approaches are often hindered by time-consuming processes, high costs, and susceptibility to human error, underscoring the potential of machine learning (ML) for enhanced risk assessment. However, ML-based CVD prediction faces several challenges, including the presence of outliers, irrelevant features, class imbalance, and limited interpretability. To address these issues, this study proposes a comprehensive framework that integrates advanced data preprocessing, feature selection, data balancing, ensemble learning, and explainable AI (XAI) techniques. The framework employs the Z-score method to identify and correct outliers, and XGBoost-based feature selection to identify the most relevant predictors from a pool of 13 variables, thereby enhancing model efficiency. Techniques such as SMOTE, SMOTETomek, and SMOTE+RUS are utilized to mitigate class imbalance. A diverse set of eight ML models, including Logistic Regression, KNN, SVM, Random Forest, XGBoost, Gradient Boosting, AdaBoost, and Gaussian Naive Bayes, are developed and rigorously evaluated using a range of performance metrics. The results demonstrate that a voting ensemble combining the top-performing models achieves superior accuracy and AUC-ROC values on the Cleveland Heart Disease dataset, with SHAP and LIME analyses providing valuable insights into model predictions, ultimately contributing to improved patient risk stratification and clinical decision-making.

Predicting hospitalization decisions for febrile patients in Emergency Departments (EDs) remains a pressing concern, as delayed interventions can significantly impact mortality rates. This study introduces a novel predictive model for ED disposition using pre-trained language models, leveraging triage data from 25,405 febrile patient visits. The model integrates numerical vital signs and self-reported symptoms to identify patients requiring hospitalization or readmission within 72 hours. By employing a unique methodology that converts numerical vital signs into narrative text, the GatorTronS architecture enables comprehensive integration with clinical narratives. Our results demonstrate the model's superior performance compared to traditional machine learning approaches and other biomedical language models, with area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) of 0.9226, area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC) of 0.8767, and macro F₁-score of 0.8446. Notably, demographic analysis reveals that hospitalization is more prevalent among elderly patients, males, and those arriving by ambulance. This study's findings have significant implications for emergency care, enabling early identification of patients requiring admission and facilitating more efficient resource allocation, ultimately leading to improved patient outcomes and ED operational efficiency.

This study investigates the complex interplay between genetic predisposition, lifestyle, and all-cause mortality in the context of obesity. Despite the well-established link between genetic susceptibility to obesity and lifestyle factors, the nuanced relationships among these variables remain poorly understood. To address this knowledge gap, we developed and validated a machine learning model that integrates genetic data from a large-scale UK Biobank dataset and an independent Nanfang Hospital cohort. The model generates an obesity-related polygenic risk score (OPRS), enabling the evaluation of the associations between genetic risk of obesity, lifestyle, and all-cause mortality. Our results indicate that the model's predictive accuracy is robust, with area under the receiver operating characteristic curve values of 0.621, 0.616, and 0.565 for the training, internal, and external test cohorts, respectively. Notably, individuals with a high OPRS exhibit a linear relationship with increased all-cause mortality, particularly among those with normal weight or overweight status. Furthermore, our analysis reveals that adherence to a healthy lifestyle among individuals with a high genetic risk of obesity significantly reduces the risk of all-cause mortality, with a notable 59% reduction observed in comparison to those who do not adopt such habits. These findings underscore the critical role of lifestyle in mitigating the adverse effects of genetic predisposition on obesity-related mortality.

In the realm of post-mastectomy reconstruction, precise estimation of breast volume and weight is a crucial aspect of patient care. However, current methods often rely on costly or complex procedures, hindering their accessibility. To address this challenge, we developed a machine learning framework that leverages a combination of demographic and anthropometric data to provide an accurate and efficient solution. Our study involved collecting data from 199 patients between 2021 and 2023, followed by a comprehensive workflow comprising data collection, pre-processing, feature selection, model training, and performance evaluation. We employed three distinct feature selection techniques: domain expert knowledge, Spearman's rank correlation, and the Boruta algorithm, to identify the most relevant characteristics for breast volume and weight estimation. These features were then used to train linear regression, random forest regression, and support vector regression models, with their performance evaluated using the coefficient of determination (R2) and Pearson's correlation coefficient. Our analysis revealed significant correlations between breast volume or weight and key patient characteristics, including BMI, breast cup size, ptosis severity, and anthropometric measurements. Notably, the optimal linear regression model, which integrated both domain-expert and statistically selected features, achieved R2 values of 81.8% for breast volume and 72% for breast weight. The results demonstrate the potential of integrating machine learning with clinical data to provide a practical, efficient, and cost-effective method for preoperative breast assessment, overcoming the limitations of traditional imaging or mathematical models.

This study focuses on the simulation of fracture evolution and material failure in various materials, encompassing a broad range of applications including infrastructure, aerospace, reservoir production, and seismic events. A comprehensive dataset has been generated through the utilization of two numerical solvers: the phase-field method and the combined finite-discrete element method (FDEM). These solvers exhibit distinct formulations, levels of physical fidelity, and computational efficiency. The dataset comprises five materials: PBX, anisotropic shale, tungsten, aluminum, and steel. For each material, the phase-field method has been applied to 400,000 simulations, with 200,000 cases under uniaxial tension and 200,000 cases under biaxial tension. In contrast, the FDEM method has been employed for 90,000 simulations, evenly distributed among PBX, shale, and tungsten under uniaxial loading. All simulations commence with randomly generated initial fracture patterns, and each entry includes temporal data capturing the dynamics of fracture propagation. This extensive dataset is designed to facilitate the development of machine learning models for predicting material failure, providing a robust foundation for advancing future research and innovation in these critical areas.

This study addresses the challenge of accurately diagnosing developmental dysgraphia, a neurological disorder affecting children's writing abilities, by developing a novel multimodal machine learning approach that integrates both online and offline handwriting data. The complex symptoms and frequent co-occurrence of dysgraphia with other disorders complicate diagnosis, underscoring the need for a more comprehensive assessment tool. Our research focuses on the relationship between online and offline handwriting modalities, which have been analyzed separately in previous studies. To investigate this relationship, we created a new multimodal dataset by transforming an existing online handwriting dataset into its offline counterpart, generating corresponding handwriting images. We trained Support Vector Machine (SVM) and Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) classifiers on online and offline features separately, then implemented multimodal feature fusion and soft-voted ensemble methods. Furthermore, we introduced a novel ensemble with conditional feature fusion, which selectively integrates feature-level fusion based on confidence scores. Our results demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves an accuracy of 88.8%, outperforming single-modality SVMs by 12-14 percentage points and existing methods by 8-9 percentage points. The findings of this study contribute to the development of an efficient initial assessment tool for handwriting difficulties through multimodal analysis of word and pseudoword data, ultimately supporting dysgraphia diagnosis. The results highlight the potential of multimodal learning in enhancing dysgraphia assessment, paving the way for more accessible and effective diagnostic tools.

This study investigates the limitations of traditional recommender systems that rely on a single type of user-item interaction, such as purchase history or ratings, to predict user preferences. In contrast, real-world user behavior encompasses a diverse range of interactions, including item clicks, cart additions, and browsing history, which collectively provide a more comprehensive understanding of user interests. To address this gap, multi-behavior recommender systems have emerged as a promising approach, integrating multiple interaction types to enhance recommendation quality. This research aims to provide an in-depth examination of multi-behavior recommender systems, focusing on three critical components: data representation, feature encoding, and model optimization. Through a systematic analysis of existing research, we categorize and compare various multi-behavior recommender systems based on their approaches to these components, highlighting both commonalities and differences. Furthermore, we identify key areas for future research and development in this field, with the goal of advancing the effectiveness and efficiency of multi-behavior recommender systems in real-world applications.

This study focuses on the development of a machine learning-based framework for predicting the inhibitory potency and activity class of small molecules targeting Coagulation Factor X (FXa), a key therapeutic target for treating various cardiovascular diseases. The framework is designed to address the ongoing challenge of creating safer and more selective FXa inhibitors. A comprehensive dataset of 6400 compounds from ChEMBL was compiled, standardized, and encoded using a set of 391 non-redundant Mordred descriptors. Following systematic filtering, a robust QSAR model was established using a combination of ExtraTreesRegressor and XGBoostClassifier algorithms. The regression model achieved a high correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.760 and a low root mean squared error (RMSE) of 0.831 on an independent test set, while the classification model demonstrated exceptional accuracy (0.91), precision, recall, and receiver operating characteristic area under the curve (ROC-AUC) of 0.962. SHAP analysis revealed that electrostatic, topological, and polar surface descriptors were the primary contributors to FXa inhibitory potency, providing valuable insights into the underlying mechanisms. The proposed QSAR pipeline exhibits strong predictive performance, interpretability, and rigorous validation, making it a practical tool for virtual screening and rational design of novel FXa inhibitors.

This study explores the graph-related capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs), examining their ability to reproduce and generate graphs. We evaluate their performance on two tasks: reciting well-known graphs from the literature and generating Erdős–Rényi random graphs. To assess their accuracy, we introduce a metric that measures the occurrence of graph hallucinations, where LLMs return incorrect information as factual. Our results indicate that the amplitude of graph hallucinations can serve as an indicator of LLM superiority, with a notable correlation observed between graph hallucinations and the Hallucination Leaderboard, a ranking system based on a large-scale evaluation of prompts. In the generation task, we find that most LLMs exhibit surprisingly good and reproducible performance. These findings provide a foundation for further investigation into the emergent properties of LLMs and serve as a benchmark for their improvement. The study bridges the gap between network science and machine learning by shedding light on the graph-related capabilities of LLMs, a crucial aspect of their functionality.

This study addresses the pressing need for objective pain assessment in clinical practice, where traditional methods heavily rely on subjective self-reporting. Pain is a multifaceted experience encompassing sensory and emotional components, rendering it challenging to accurately evaluate. Recent research has focused on harnessing physiological signals to develop objective pain assessment techniques, leveraging the impact of pain on the Autonomic Nervous System. This investigation synthesizes recent advancements in pain biomarkers and machine learning methods for pain assessment using physiological signals, underscoring the growing interest and unmet demand in this field. A thorough examination of the literature from 2014 to 2024 reveals a dichotomy in signal feature behavior across various pain types, necessitating the development of tailored assessment models. Our analysis also reviews the current state-of-the-art models for pain assessment, comparing classical machine learning and deep learning techniques. The findings of this study contribute to the development of more effective and reliable pain assessment tools, ultimately enhancing patient care and treatment outcomes.

The increasing reliance on machine learning algorithms in various aspects of society has underscored the need to address their susceptibility to adversarial attacks through data manipulation. The prospect of near-term quantum computers executing quantum machine learning (QML) algorithms has sparked significant interest in their potential vulnerabilities. This study investigates the inherent robustness of QML algorithms against adversarial tampering, leveraging principles from many-body physics to identify the quantum mechanisms underlying this protection. Our research demonstrates that QML algorithms can offer inherent defenses against certain types of attacks, guaranteeing robustness in specific scenarios against adversaries armed with classical methods. We establish that quantum classifiers exhibit resistance to: weak perturbations of data from the trained distribution, local attacks if they are insufficiently entangled, and universal adversarial attacks if they are sufficiently complex. Our theoretical findings are substantiated by numerical evidence, illustrating the practical applicability of our theorems and the resulting robustness of quantum classifiers in real-world scenarios. This line of inquiry presents a novel pathway to leveraging quantum advantages in machine learning, distinct from the conventional pursuit of improved model speed or accuracy.

The accurate and efficient monitoring of railway corridors relies heavily on the availability of high-resolution spatial data to ensure operational safety and efficiency. Despite its importance, data-driven railway infrastructure assessment has been hindered by the scarcity of large-scale, finely annotated 3D benchmarks. To address this limitation, we introduce a comprehensive dataset, SemanticRail3D, comprising 438 high-resolution point clouds, each segmented into 12 semantic classes and grouped into instance labels with per-point intensity information. Building upon this foundation, we develop SemanticRail3D-V2, a machine learning-ready dataset that incorporates a preprocessing pipeline to align and section raw scans into uniform blocks, and a novel evaluation protocol that combines metric-based anomaly detection with a probabilistic analysis of class distributions and spatial relationships. A rigorous validation process involving domain experts ensured the quality of the dataset, resulting in five training shards, a validation set, and a held-out test set. The SemanticRail3D dataset, augmented with the V2 enhancements, provides a richly annotated benchmark for semantic and instance segmentation in railway environments, facilitating research in condition monitoring and asset management. This dataset has the potential to significantly contribute to the development of more accurate and efficient railway infrastructure assessment methods.

Clinical Decision Support Systems (CDSS) have revolutionized healthcare by enhancing medical decision-making and improving patient outcomes. However, developing rules for a CDSS is a labor-intensive process, often hindered by the time-consuming iterations between clinicians and developers. This study investigates the potential of large language models (LLMs) and large reasoning models (LRMs) in generating triaging rules for a CDSS. We employed various LLMs (GPT-3.5, GPT-4, GPT-4o, Gemini, Claude 3.5 Sonnet) and LRMs (GPT-o1-mini, Grok-4, Claude 4 Sonnet) using alternative prompting techniques to generate rule sets for a CDSS. The generated rules were compared to those from our Pandemic Intervention Monitoring System (PiMS), a triaging CDSS developed in collaboration with clinicians to monitor COVID-19 patients. The effectiveness of the generated rules was evaluated based on accuracy, interpretability, and rule complexity. Our results show that LLMs produced COVID-19 screening rule sets when not provided with specific variables from the PiMS rule set, but their accuracy ranged from 31.62% to 70.71%. When prompted with PiMS variables, LLMs exhibited lower interpretability and rule complexity compared to the PiMS rule set. LRMs demonstrated varying levels of interpretability, ranging from 3 to 94, and achieved an average accuracy of 31.62% to 81.70%. Our findings suggest that while LLMs and LRMs can generate initial rule sets for CDSS, their limitations in emulating clinical rule sets due to simplicity and lack of complex reasoning necessitate further research. Integrating LLMs and LRMs with decision trees may improve their effectiveness in generating high-quality rules for CDSS.

Epidemic response has witnessed a significant transformation with the integration of artificial intelligence (AI) in recent years. AI has emerged as a valuable tool in predicting disease patterns, controlling outbreaks, and optimizing the distribution of medical resources. This study aims to provide a comprehensive review of AI's contributions to epidemic response, with a particular focus on its application to Lassa fever. The review begins by examining the epidemiological characteristics and transmission patterns of Lassa fever, laying the foundation for understanding the potential of AI-driven approaches. Key AI technologies, including machine learning, deep learning, and natural language processing, are analyzed for their impact on surveillance, diagnostics, and treatment innovation. The study highlights successful implementations of AI in predictive modeling for outbreak identification and enhanced vaccine research. However, the integration of AI in epidemic contexts is hindered by challenges such as limited epidemiological data, high computational requirements, and difficulties in incorporating AI within existing healthcare infrastructures. These issues are particularly pronounced in Lassa fever management, where data scarcity and disease variability complicate the situation. This review addresses the gap in existing literature by providing an in-depth examination of AI-driven techniques, ongoing developments, and practical solutions tailored to Lassa fever control and prevention. By extrapolating insights from Lassa fever, the study offers a strategic guide for stakeholders, scientists, healthcare professionals, and policymakers seeking to leverage AI to enhance public health resilience and epidemic management.

This study presents a comprehensive dataset designed to facilitate a rigorous evaluation of machine learning models for handwritten character recognition. By integrating our dataset with existing NIST databases, researchers can conduct in-depth analyses of models trained on these data, thereby enhancing the understanding of their performance. We provide an overview of prominent machine learning models that have been trained on the EMNIST-letters dataset, highlighting their strengths and limitations in achieving optimal accuracy on test sets constructed through cross-validation. Furthermore, we propose an additional evaluation framework that leverages newly constructed, independent data not affiliated with the NIST database authors, allowing for a more unbiased assessment of model performance. Our dataset and accompanying source codes are publicly available through the Gdansk Tech University repository, enabling the research community to replicate and build upon our findings. The proposed evaluation framework and dataset are expected to contribute to the advancement of handwritten character recognition models, ultimately improving their accuracy and reliability in real-world applications.

This study investigates the efficacy of artificial intelligence (AI) applied to electrocardiography (ECG) for mortality prediction across diverse populations. To address the heterogeneity of existing approaches and limited generalizability, we conducted a comprehensive evaluation of model design choices across three large medical center cohorts: the Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center in the United States, the Telehealth Network of Minas Gerais in Brazil, and Boston Children's Hospital in the United States. Our analysis compared the performance of various AI models, including horizon-based classification and deep survival methods, using different neural architectures such as convolutional neural networks and transformers. We also benchmarked these models against demographic-only and gradient boosting baselines. Our results show that top-performing models achieved good predictive accuracy, with median concordance indices ranging from 0.78 to 0.83 across the three datasets. Notably, incorporating age and sex as demographic covariates improved model performance across all datasets. However, we observed that classifier-based models were sensitive to horizon selection, with site-dependent variations in performance. External validation revealed a decline in concordance, and in some cases, demographic-only models outperformed externally trained AI-ECG models. Conversely, models trained on multi-site data outperformed site-specific models by margins of up to 22%. Our findings highlight the importance of model type, demographic features, and training data diversity in developing AI-ECG models that can be reliably applied across populations.

This study explores the potential of a single video question to predict self-reported depression, anxiety, and trauma through the analysis of both text and voice cues. With the growing demand for mental health screenings in clinical settings, the need for efficient multi-condition assessment methods has become increasingly pressing. To address this challenge, we developed a multimodal model that integrates a text analysis component, utilizing MPNet, with a voice prosody analysis component, leveraging HuBERT. The model was trained on a dataset comprising 2420 participants, yielding a significant reduction in assessment time (64.6% decrease from 221.7 seconds to 78.4 seconds) while simultaneously screening for all three conditions. Notably, only a small proportion of participants (1.4%) expressed reluctance to participate in video-based screening. Our results demonstrate the feasibility and efficacy of this approach, with strong performance observed across diverse demographic groups, including age, gender, and race/ethnicity. The findings suggest that brief video responses can be a valuable tool for efficient multi-condition screening in clinical settings, potentially reducing the burden on patients and healthcare providers alike.

This study addresses the pressing issue of cholangiocarcinoma (CCA), a prevalent and critical public health concern in Thailand, where its incidence far exceeds global averages. To tackle this challenge, we aimed to integrate and analyze diverse CCA data sources, including research datasets and electronic health records, utilizing the Cholangiocarcinoma Ontology (CCAO). Our methodology entailed annotating Thai datasets with CCAO and applying ontology-based term enrichment techniques to identify significant risk factors associated with suspected CCA and patients with CCA. These enriched terms were then visualized within the ontology hierarchy, facilitating the identification of key indicators. The outputs of the term enrichment analyses served as inputs for machine learning classification tasks. Our results revealed a set of demographic and lifestyle risk factors, including male gender, limited education, alcohol consumption, smoking, occupation as a farmer, and diabetes, as well as ultrasound indicators such as dilated bile ducts, periductal fibrosis, and hepatic mass. Furthermore, we employed a random forest classifier, seeded with the term enrichment results, to predict CCA patients with an average precision-recall curve score of 0.92 (0.023 standard deviation), highlighting age, dilated bile ducts, periductal fibrosis, suspected CCA, and hepatic mass as the top five crucial features. These findings have significant implications for targeted surveillance and intervention strategies to mitigate the morbidity and mortality associated with CCA in Thailand. Expanding the CCAO with molecular data and integrating ontology-driven term enrichment analysis and machine learning will facilitate the discovery of novel hypotheses to combat this disease.

This study investigates the application of machine learning (ML) in enhancing the predictive accuracy of iron-deficiency anaemia (IDA) diagnosis. IDA is a prevalent hematological disorder that significantly impairs physical capacity and cognitive development, necessitating early detection. Conventional diagnostic methods often have limitations, underscoring the need for more effective diagnostic tools. This study aims to develop and compare multiple ML algorithms for IDA prediction, focusing on the analysis of complex hematological parameters, including reticulocyte maturation indices. A retrospective cross-sectional study was conducted on anonymized data from 444 adults (20–59 years) collected at Shahid Mohammadi Hospital, Iran, between November 2024 and January 2025. Five supervised ML algorithms, including random forest, support vector machine, decision tree, naive Bayes, and CatBoost, were trained using a 70/30 train-test split. Stratified 10-fold cross-validation and the synthetic minority oversampling technique were applied for hyperparameter optimization and class-imbalance adjustment. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score, AUC-ROC, and the Matthews correlation coefficient. SHapley additive explanations were used to characterize feature importance. Results show that CatBoost achieved the highest AUC-ROC of 0.93 on an independent test set, outperforming other ML algorithms. SHAP analysis identified haemoglobin, haematocrit, and red blood cell count as the most influential predictors, highlighting the model's physiological interpretability. These findings suggest that ML models, particularly CatBoost, can accurately predict IDA using standard blood tests and reticulocyte indices, offering a promising future for more efficient and effective resource utilization in clinical settings.

The integration of artificial intelligence and visual art has revolutionized environmental design, enabling the creation of interactive and adaptive spaces that respond to changing environmental conditions and user emotions. Traditional artworks in public spaces often fail to adapt to the dynamic nature of their surroundings, resulting in a lack of engagement and emotional resonance. To address this limitation, our research focuses on the development of a novel machine learning framework, MVAEx-RL, which leverages reinforcement learning, multimodal data processing, and optimization techniques to create dynamic and responsive visual art installations. By combining deep learning algorithms with Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), MVAEx-RL enables the system to continuously collect data, evaluate visual art characteristics, and adapt to user preferences and environmental changes. The framework fuses multiple inputs, including images, audio, and sensor readings, to create a comprehensive understanding of the environment and inform the creation of aesthetically pleasing and contextually relevant art. Our study demonstrates that MVAEx-RL outperforms traditional static art and non-optimized reinforcement learning methods in terms of user experience, perceived visual aesthetics, and contextual relevance, making it a valuable tool for personalized and adaptive environmental design.

Cardiac patients equipped with Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillators (ICDs) are at heightened risk of experiencing anxiety, depression, or psychological distress. However, routine psychological screening in cardiology care remains limited, and identifying vulnerable individuals in settings transitioning to remote monitoring poses a significant challenge. Consequently, there is an urgent need for scalable, data-driven tools to facilitate timely detection and intervention. This study aimed to develop and evaluate Machine Learning (ML) models for predicting anxiety and psychological distress in ICD patients using patient-reported outcomes from a large dataset. We employed a comprehensive methodology, including data imputation, strategic feature selection, and hyperparameter tuning, to develop and compare the performance of nine ML algorithms and two ensemble approaches (stacking and voting). The best-performing models were further analyzed using SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) to enhance clinical interpretability. Our results show that the stacking classifier achieved high accuracy in detecting anxiety (F1-macro score of 0.93), while the light gradient boosting machine was effective in predicting psychological distress (F1-macro score of 0.93). Notably, feature importance analyses revealed that patient-reported mental health scores, loneliness scores, depression and anxiety scales, and quality-of-life indices were the most predictive features. Our findings demonstrate the potential of combining clinical insight, structured preprocessing, and ensemble learning to develop highly accurate and interpretable models for detecting psychological distress in ICD patients. The integration of SHAP facilitates clinical trust and paves the way for the development of scalable decision-support tools in cardiology, warranting further investigation into external validation and real-world deployment.

This study investigates the enhancement of machine learning model performance for complex problems through the integration of information from multiple views and the selection of the most critical features. Traditional multi-view feature selection methods focus on the distributional and informational differences between views, but often neglect the presence of noise in multi-view data. To address this limitation, we develop a novel noise-tolerant multi-view feature selection algorithm that incorporates view weights, sample weights, and feature weights into a unified framework. Our approach optimizes the objective function using a novel alternating iterative optimization algorithm, which assigns smaller weights to noise samples, thereby improving the algorithm's robustness to noise. We evaluate the performance of our algorithm on various classification and regression tasks and demonstrate its superiority over state-of-the-art methods, particularly in datasets contaminated with noise. The results indicate that our algorithm's ability to handle noise leads to improved model accuracy and generalizability, underscoring its potential for real-world applications in complex problem domains.

The advent of Beyond 5G (B5G) networks has created a robust infrastructure for supporting the diverse and dynamic requirements of Internet of Things (IoT) communication. The Session Initiation Protocol (SIP), originally designed for initiating, modifying, and terminating multimedia sessions over IP networks, has emerged as a promising protocol for facilitating communication and coordination in IoT environments. However, SIP is vulnerable to Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) threats, with INVITE flooding attacks posing a significant challenge. Traditional IoT Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) rely on machine learning models trained on local data, which may not detect attack patterns observed in other deployment contexts. To address this limitation, we propose a federated learning-based approach, where multiple IDS collaborate to achieve early detection of INVITE flooding attacks across different deployment contexts. Our framework leverages knowledge sharing among IDS to enhance the detection of SIP flooding attacks, demonstrating improved accuracy under various flow intensities and realistic SIP operational conditions. Performance evaluations under diverse scenarios reveal the robustness of the proposed framework, with federated learning enhancing accuracy from 47% to 99% through collaborative learning. The results highlight the effectiveness of a GRU architecture with a FedAvgM aggregation function in delivering the best performance in varied scenarios, underscoring the potential of federated learning in improving the resilience of IoT networks against DDoS attacks.

This study aims to investigate the effectiveness of recent deep learning models, specifically the Transformer architecture, in breast cancer survival prediction. The increasing reliance on machine learning models for clinical decision-making necessitates a thorough evaluation of their performance and potential for improvement. Despite the proliferation of machine learning models in this domain, existing studies have primarily focused on variations in data sampling and hyperparameter tuning, rather than exploring novel architectures. This research leverages a comprehensive breast cancer dataset collected from the University Malaya Medical Centre between 2006 and 2015, comprising 26 variables, including 24 categorical and two numerical features. A comparative analysis is conducted between Transformer-based models, including BERT, RoBERTa, and ALBERT, and traditional machine learning algorithms, such as random forest, decision tree, support vector machine, logistic regression, k-nearest neighbours, artificial neural network, and extreme gradient boosting. The results indicate that the Transformer models exhibit comparable performance to existing machine learning models, with the ALBERT model achieving an accuracy of 81.1%, and the logistic regression model achieving an accuracy of 83.4%. These findings suggest that the Transformer architecture can be a viable alternative for breast cancer survival prediction, offering a promising direction for future research in this field.

Arabic sentiment analysis on social media platforms is a complex task due to the language's morphological richness, dialectal diversity, and contextual ambiguity. To address these challenges, this study examines the interplay between annotation quality and model architecture on the accuracy of Arabic sentiment analysis. A novel dataset was created using an active annotation strategy that leverages both human expertise and AI-assisted labelling through advanced language models. The study investigates the impact of annotation quality on classification performance metrics, including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, across various machine learning and deep learning models. Experimental results show that annotation consistency has a significant influence on model performance, with classical machine learning models achieving best results with high-quality AI-generated annotations. In contrast, context-sensitive models performed better with human-labelled datasets, highlighting the importance of human semantic interpretation. Transformer-based architectures, such as AraBERTv2, CAMeL Lab BERT, and XLM-RoBERTa, demonstrated superior performance and robust contextual understanding, even in the presence of annotation noise. The findings suggest that integrating AI-assisted annotation with transformer-based deep learning offers a scalable and linguistically adaptive framework for Arabic sentiment analysis. This research contributes to both theoretical understanding and practical applications by proposing a balanced human-AI annotation paradigm that enhances the efficiency and interpretability of Arabic sentiment analysis models.

The advent of 6G networks underscores the pressing need for secure and high-performance data communication, particularly in dynamic distributed systems such as Human Activity Recognition (HAR). Centralized machine learning models often suffer from high latency, data breaches, and performance scalability issues, hindering their effectiveness in real-time applications. To address these limitations, this study proposes a novel framework that integrates Federated Learning (FL) with Edge Intelligence (EI) and Differential Privacy (DP) to enable secure and efficient activity recognition. By decentralizing model activation on edge devices, the proposed framework eliminates the need for raw data transfer to central servers, thereby ensuring user data privacy. The incorporation of Differential Privacy further enhances security by introducing controlled noise to gradient updates, protecting against data reconstruction attacks. Experimental results on the UCI-HAR dataset demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed framework, achieving an average accuracy of 99.25%, an F1-score of 0.9871, and a Kappa score of 0.9909. Notably, the framework significantly reduces latency and communication overhead compared to baseline models, such as SVM and CNN-BiLSTM, while maintaining high accuracy and privacy guarantees. The proposed framework has the potential to be applied in real-time smart cities, healthcare, and industrial IoT, making it a significant advancement towards secure and effective edge-based AI schemes in 6G systems.

The proliferation of digital transactions has underscored the need for robust fraud detection models in financial institutions. Traditional graph-based models have shown promise, but their reliance on limited labeled datasets raises concerns about their adaptability to real-world scenarios. These models often struggle to cope with distributional shifts and sophisticated adversarial tactics employed by fraudsters. To address these limitations, we introduce the concept of Multi-round Adversarial Fraud Detection, a novel framework that simulates real-life fraud detection by iteratively training and evaluating models on adversarially evolving graphs. The graph evolution is driven by generator functions rooted in common fraud behavior, reducing reliance on static datasets. However, existing models falter under this scenario, achieving F1 scores below 56 percent in subsequent rounds. To bridge this performance gap, we propose Temporally Pre-trained Node Embedder (TPNE), a self-supervised pre-training module that effectively separates and enhances temporal information across multiple rounds. TPNE is a model-agnostic and label-agnostic solution, yielding significant improvements over the best-performing baseline, with up to 4.6 percent increase in on-round F1 score and up to 32.9 percent boost in final recall. Our findings underscore the potential of TPNE in enhancing the resilience and effectiveness of graph-based fraud detection models in real-world applications.

This study aimed to develop and validate a machine learning model for predicting postoperative pain in patients following surgery. Postoperative pain management is a critical aspect of patient recovery, and accurate prediction can significantly improve the quality of care. To address this challenge, a machine learning model was developed using biosignals recorded in the post-anesthesia care unit (PACU), including electrocardiography (ECG) and photoplethysmography (PPG) signals from 24,211 adult patients undergoing surgery at two hospitals in South Korea between 2021 and 2022. Heart rate variability (HRV) and surgical pleth index (SPI) features were extracted from these signals, along with demographic variables, to train a range of machine learning models, including logistic regression, support vector machine, multilayer perceptron, random forest, and XGBoost. The model was internally validated using one dataset and externally validated using an independent dataset from a second hospital to assess its generalizability. The results showed that the best-performing model achieved high accuracy and area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) values in both internal and external validation, with feature importance analysis indicating that SPI and HRV features were the most influential predictors. Incorporating demographic features, such as age and sex, further improved prediction accuracy, suggesting that these variables can help mitigate variability in biosignal data and enhance the model's stability and generalizability. The study demonstrates the potential of biosignal-based approaches for objective pain assessment in clinical practice, highlighting the need for further research in this area.

The proliferation of fake news through digital platforms poses significant challenges, particularly in developing countries with limited resources. Misleading or fabricated content can have far-reaching consequences for individuals, organizations, and society as a whole. Existing detection methods often rely on complex algorithms and substantial computational resources, rendering them impractical for real-time applications or languages with limited digital infrastructure. To address this issue, we developed an efficient fake news detection system based on a stacked machine learning model that leverages TF-IDF for feature extraction. This approach transforms text into a numerical representation by emphasizing key terms, while the stacked ensemble model enhances classification accuracy by integrating multiple machine learning algorithms. We also explored various machine learning classifiers and feature vectorization techniques, including mBERT and Word2Vec. Our system was evaluated using three datasets: one English and two Bangla fake news detection datasets. The TF-IDF + Stacking model achieved high accuracy and F1-score on the English dataset, with notable improvements observed on the Bangla datasets after applying SMOTE and mBERT. The model demonstrated strong performance across languages, indicating its potential for real-world applications in multilingual and resource-constrained settings.

This study investigates the efficacy of the Transformer model and reinforcement learning optimization framework in evaluating and enhancing athletes' psychological quality. A comprehensive psychological quality assessment index system was developed, incorporating multimodal data from psychological measurements (e.g., emotional state, anxiety level), physiological signals (e.g., heart rate variability, skin conductance), and behavioral data (e.g., reaction time, action frequency). The proposed Transformer-based model effectively captures temporal dependencies and cross-modal interactions, enabling accurate psychological state assessments. Experimental results demonstrate that the Transformer model outperforms traditional methods in classification tasks, achieving significant improvements of 8.3%, 8.0%, 8.0%, and 8.0% in accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score, respectively. Furthermore, the integration of the reinforcement learning optimization framework and personalized training programs yields substantial improvements in athletes' psychological quality, with a mean enhancement of 12%. Notably, key indicators such as emotional stability, anxiety level, and concentration exhibit significant improvements. Long-term tracking analysis confirms the effectiveness and sustainability of the optimization training, providing robust evidence for future research and practical applications in the field of sports psychology.

Attribution of advanced persistent threats (APTs) is a critical component of cybersecurity defense, enabling the protection of sensitive assets and systems. Cyber threat intelligence (CTI) offers a wealth of information about APT groups, yet existing studies often focus on individual features, overlooking the potential of multi-level mining and combined features. This limitation hinders the depth and accuracy of attribution analysis, potentially leading to misinformed conclusions. To address these shortcomings, we introduce a novel multi-level feature Dempster-Shafer joint (MLDSJ) attribution approach for APT groups based on CTI. Our method involves extracting a range of features, including attack patterns, textual information, and graph topology from CTI reports, which are then classified using simple machine learning models. The Dempster-Shafer evidence theory is employed to integrate these features using the Dempster combination rule, resulting in a comprehensive attribution framework. Experimental results demonstrate the efficacy of our approach, achieving superior classification performance compared to baseline methods, with an accuracy of 89.9%, recall of 86.5%, and F1-score of 88.2%. These findings underscore the importance of multi-level feature fusion in enhancing APT attribution and provide valuable insights for the development of intelligence-driven defense strategies.

Parkinson's disease (PD) is a complex neurodegenerative disorder characterized by both motor and non-motor symptoms, with speech impairment often being one of the earliest and most prevalent manifestations. Early detection and monitoring of PD are crucial for effective management and treatment. Recent studies have demonstrated the potential of voice analysis as a non-invasive, accessible, and cost-effective approach for PD diagnosis. This study investigates the application of deep learning techniques to speech recordings for PD detection. We propose a novel framework that leverages acoustic features extracted from speech segments to identify PD patients. Utilizing the publicly available MDVR-KCL dataset, speech recordings are first preprocessed to isolate relevant segments, which are then divided into 2-second windows and subjected to feature extraction. A total of 43 acoustic features, including Mel-frequency cepstral coefficients, pitch, formants, spectral, and harmonic descriptors, are extracted and used as input to various recurrent neural network architectures, including long short-term memory (LSTM), gated recurrent unit, and bidirectional LSTM (BiLSTM), augmented with attention mechanisms to enhance temporal focus. Our results indicate that the BiLSTM-Attention model achieves the highest classification accuracy of 99.85% over 5-fold cross-validation, outperforming other models. Statistical analysis confirms the discriminative power of the extracted features, underscoring the potential of voice-based PD diagnostics supported by intelligent preprocessing, acoustic signal analysis, and attention-based deep learning.

The prevalence of poor posture, particularly during prolonged sitting, poses a significant health risk, contributing to musculoskeletal disorders, chronic pain, and reduced quality of life. Approximately 40% of children and numerous adults are affected, underscoring the need for accessible and user-friendly solutions to monitor and correct posture in real-time. Traditional posture monitoring systems often rely on expensive or uncomfortable wearable devices, hindering their practicality. This study presents ErgoGuard, a cost-effective, non-intrusive posture monitoring system designed to provide real-time feedback and promote healthier sitting habits. ErgoGuard utilizes Force Sensing Resistors (FSRs) and machine learning to classify sitting postures accurately. The system integrates ten FSRs on a chair's seat pan and backrest, connected to a Raspberry Pi 4 for data processing. Pressure distribution data was collected from five participants representing diverse age groups and body sizes in various sitting positions (upright, slouched, crossed-leg). The data was preprocessed, normalized, and analyzed using machine learning models, including Support Vector Machine (SVM), Random Forest, and Gradient Boosting Classifier. Results demonstrate that ErgoGuard achieves robust posture classification, with Random Forest exhibiting the highest macro-average F1 score of 0.87, indicating strong performance across multiple posture classes. The non-wearable design ensures user comfort, while machine learning enables scalable and accurate monitoring. ErgoGuard offers a practical, innovative solution for posture correction, addressing the limitations of existing systems. Its cost-effective design and real-time feedback make it suitable for ergonomics, healthcare, and workplace wellness applications. Future research will explore the integration of heat maps to enhance classification performance and provide deeper insights into posture transitions, paving the way for advanced, user-centric systems.

This study addresses the scarcity of accessible data in the insurance sector, particularly in health insurance, where confidentiality and competitive concerns often restrict data availability. To address this challenge, we present a novel dataset derived from a Spanish health insurance portfolio spanning 2017 to 2019, comprising over 70,000 unique insured individuals and over 225,000 data points. The dataset encompasses 42 variables, with 27 directly sourced from the insurer and the remaining 15 variables derived from publicly available sources to incorporate area-based contextual information. To ensure data privacy while maintaining analytical integrity, the dataset has been anonymized. This comprehensive dataset offers researchers a valuable resource for investigating health insurance dynamics, encompassing product design, contextual influences, and risk management strategies. Furthermore, the dataset provides a practical platform for academic applications, enabling students and educators to engage in real-world data analysis, statistical modeling, and machine learning exercises, thereby fostering hands-on learning experiences in data science and analytics.

This study presents the Trauma THOMPSON dataset, a comprehensive collection of high-resolution, egocentric video clips designed to enhance the development of artificial intelligence-driven decision support systems for life-saving interventions in emergency care, particularly in resource-constrained humanitarian settings.
