The dataset comprises 3,717 video clips of both standard and improvised procedures, where responders utilize makeshift tools to simulate real-world scenarios. Each clip is meticulously annotated by medical professionals using a verb-noun format, providing detailed information on actions such as "administer medication" and "apply tourniquet". The dataset also includes annotations for medical visual question answering, hand movements, and object detection, enabling the training of AI agents to provide real-time guidance to first-responders on the most effective course of action given the available resources. To facilitate the evaluation of AI models, we establish benchmarks for action recognition, anticipation, and medical visual question answering using cutting-edge machine learning algorithms. The Trauma THOMPSON dataset and associated benchmarks have the potential to revolutionize emergency care by empowering AI systems to provide critical support to responders in high-pressure situations.

This study provides an in-depth examination of the multiple sequence alignment (MSA) technique, a cornerstone of computational biology used to identify similarities in protein, DNA, or RNA sequences that reflect functional, structural, or evolutionary relationships. By systematically categorizing and analyzing various MSA algorithms based on their underlying approaches, this research aims to elucidate the strengths, limitations, and applications of these methods. The investigation encompasses seven primary categories of alignment techniques, including dynamic programming, progressive alignment, iterative refinement, Hidden Markov Model-based, consistency-based, structure-based, and machine learning-based approaches. Through a comprehensive review of recent benchmarks and literature, this study identifies key innovations, performance characteristics, and emerging trends in the field, shedding light on the evolution of MSA algorithms and their pivotal role in modern bioinformatics. The findings of this research offer a nuanced understanding of the diverse landscape of MSA methods, enabling informed decision-making and future advancements in computational biology and related fields.

The integration of machine learning techniques into clinical decision-making has significant potential in bioinformatics and medical imaging analysis, yet their application is often hindered by limited data availability, data quality issues, and the opaque nature of machine learning models. Consequently, there is a pressing need for reliable surrogate models that can effectively learn from small datasets to inform clinical diagnosis. To address this challenge, we conducted a retrospective observational study and developed a data-driven predictive model that estimates mean pulmonary artery pressure (mPAP) based on individual patient clinical characteristics. Our approach leverages readily accessible noninvasive physiological features to accurately predict mPAP, thereby facilitating the assessment of pulmonary hypertension. Moreover, we extended the disease evaluation framework by incorporating cardiac magnetic resonance (CMR)-related features, enabling a more comprehensive understanding of the disease. The proposed framework surpasses traditional invasive measurement methods by providing a reliable and noninvasive means of estimating mPAP, while also incorporating uncertainty quantification to extract meaningful patterns and support clinical diagnosis. This study demonstrates the potential of our approach to improve the accuracy and efficiency of clinical decision-making in pulmonary hypertension diagnosis.

This study addresses a critical limitation in the deployment of universal machine-learning interatomic potentials (uMLIPs) for atomistic simulations, which is the lack of reliable uncertainty estimates. These models have shown promise in achieving near-ab initio accuracy at a significantly reduced computational cost. To overcome this challenge, we introduce a novel, scalable uncertainty metric, U, derived from a diverse ensemble of existing pretrained uMLIPs. Our metric effectively captures true prediction errors and provides a robust ranking of configuration-level risk across various chemistries and structures. By leveraging U, we develop an uncertainty-aware distillation approach to train system-specific potentials with a substantial reduction in labeled data requirements. Our results demonstrate that for tungsten, a 4% reduction in DFT data is sufficient to match the accuracy of full DFT training, while for MoNbTaW, the distilled models outperform those trained on DFT data. Furthermore, the distilled models exhibit improved robustness to numerical label noise, in some cases surpassing the accuracy of the original MLIPs. This framework provides a practical tool for monitoring model reliability, guiding data selection, and fine-tuning, ultimately enabling the cost-efficient, accurate, and safe deployment of foundation models in atomistic simulations.

The development of high-performance single-crystal superalloys has traditionally relied on labor-intensive and costly trial-and-error experimentation. To address this challenge, we propose an innovative alloy design strategy that combines natural language processing (NLP) and machine learning (ML) techniques. A custom-built NLP model was developed to systematically extract γ′ solvus temperature data from a vast corpus of scientific literature, thereby creating a comprehensive and accurate database. Leveraging this database, ML models were trained to predict γ′ solvus temperature and creep life with high precision. Utilizing these predictive models, we conducted an extensive virtual screening of over 340,000 alloy compositions, ultimately identifying a novel low-cost alloy, CSU-S1. Experimental validation confirmed that CSU-S1 exhibits a γ′ solvus temperature of approximately 1300 °C and a creep life of 224.7 hours at 1100 °C/137 MPa, rivaling the performance of third-generation single-crystal superalloys while utilizing only 3.1 wt% Re and costing approximately 121 USD/kg. This research not only presents a high-performance, cost-effective superalloy but also demonstrates a generalizable "knowledge-to-innovation" design paradigm, offering a promising approach to accelerate the development of advanced engineering materials.

This study introduces an innovative approach to constructing training datasets for Machine-Learned Interatomic Potential (MLIP) models, enabling the prediction of phase properties under diverse conditions. Our framework leverages Nested Sampling (NS) to systematically explore the configuration space and generate thermodynamically relevant configurations, which are then evaluated using ab initio Density Functional Theory (DFT). The resulting database is used to train a model based on the Atomic Cluster Expansion (ACE) architecture. This novel framework offers several advantages, including automation, suitability for iterative learning, and independence from prior knowledge of stable phases, thereby mitigating bias towards pre-existing structural data. To demonstrate its effectiveness, we apply the framework to magnesium, developing a model capable of accurately describing its behavior across a wide range of pressures (0–600 GPa) and temperatures (0–8000 K). We evaluate the model's performance by calculating phonon spectra, elastic constants, and the pressure-temperature phase diagram within this region. The results demonstrate the potential of our framework to produce robust MLIPs with high transferability and generality, while significantly reducing computational costs.

The classification of ancient glass beads excavated across Japan is crucial for understanding the complex cultural exchanges that occurred across the Eurasian continent. However, traditional methods relying on expert inspection and chemical composition analysis are often time-consuming and impractical for large-scale investigations. To address this challenge, we have developed a machine learning framework that leverages image data to classify ancient glass beads based on their provenance. Our approach focuses on capturing both the intricate surface details and overall shapes of the artifacts, which are essential for accurate classification. We achieve this by integrating convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and vision transformers (ViTs) into a unified architecture, and employing a dual-view (top and side) image fusion technique to simulate expert observation. Furthermore, our framework incorporates bidirectional knowledge distillation between models and viewpoints to ensure feature consistency and efficient multiview fusion. Experimental results show that while our method may not consistently achieve the highest average accuracy, it exhibits the lowest performance variance across folds, indicating stable classification performance even for rare classes. This study demonstrates the feasibility of using image-based approaches for the provenance estimation of ancient glass beads, and highlights the potential of these methods for advancing archaeological research and analysis of cultural heritage materials.

This study investigates the impact of digital infrastructure development on urban green innovation in China, leveraging the Broadband China Pilot policy as a quasi-natural experiment. Employing double machine learning models and a panel dataset of 285 Chinese cities from 2010 to 2022, we examine the causal relationship between digital infrastructure investment and green innovation outcomes. Our analysis reveals a significant positive correlation between digital infrastructure development and both the quantity and quality of urban green innovation, as measured by patent applications and citations. These findings are robust to various sensitivity checks, including sample adjustments and model respecification. Further analysis suggests that digital infrastructure enhances innovators' access to relevant information, enabling more targeted and cost-effective green innovation. Notably, our results indicate that the policy effect is more pronounced in cities with different regional characteristics, such as eastern, western, and non-resource-dependent cities, which exhibit higher-quality green innovation outcomes. This study contributes to the understanding of how digital infrastructure can be leveraged to promote sustainable economic development through green innovation, providing valuable insights for policymakers and stakeholders.

This study provides a comprehensive examination of the cybersecurity landscape in 5G-enabled Internet of Things (IoT) networks, highlighting the intricate interplay between the technology's vast potential and its inherent vulnerabilities. As 5G technology continues to revolutionize IoT applications, it is essential to address the pressing need for robust security measures to mitigate potential threats and ensure the integrity of these networks. To achieve this goal, our research focuses on aligning theoretical frameworks with practical implementations by analyzing specific attack scenarios in IoT contexts, thereby enhancing our understanding of real-world vulnerabilities. We investigate the dynamic interplay between IoT devices and 5G infrastructure, identifying key areas of weakness that can be exploited by sophisticated threats. Our findings underscore the importance of ongoing research to stay abreast of the evolving 5G standard, particularly in relation to newly developed components and features. Furthermore, we emphasize the need for standardized security protocols and regulatory frameworks to ensure uniform security practices across various industries and geographical regions. Ultimately, our research aims to inform the development of more effective protection and mitigation strategies, thereby safeguarding the resilience and security of 5G-enabled IoT networks.

In the rapidly evolving landscape of financial markets, effective asset allocation and portfolio management are essential for achieving optimal returns and mitigating risk. To address this challenge, we propose a novel Reinforcement Learning-based Adaptive Portfolio Management (RL-APM) system that leverages the Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) approach to optimize real-time portfolio allocation. The RL-APM system generates a state vector incorporating market data, including daily OHLCV values and technical indicators such as Simple Moving Average (SMA), Relative Strength Index (RSI), Moving Average Convergence Divergence (MACD), and Bollinger Bands. This state is then utilized by the DDPG agent to dynamically adjust portfolio weights in response to market fluctuations. A key innovation of our approach is the introduction of a multi-objective reward function that simultaneously maximizes volatility, Sharpe ratio, cumulative return, and transaction costs, thereby enhancing the system's stability and realism. Our results, obtained using daily S&P 500 index data from 2015 to 2024, demonstrate that the RL-APM system outperforms traditional Mean-Variance Optimization (MVO) models, yielding average returns of 12.4% and a Sharpe ratio of 15.7%. These findings suggest that the RL-APM system has the potential to efficiently scale for real-world financial applications, including hedge funds, automated trading, and robo-advisory platforms, while providing flexible allocation plans and robust risk management capabilities.

Evaluating the risk of cardiac patients upon hospital entry is crucial for efficient triage and timely medical intervention. This study aims to predict a range of clinical outcomes by leveraging admission data from a cardiac care unit using an optimized machine learning approach. A novel hybrid architecture is proposed, integrating convolutional neural networks with advanced machine learning classifiers, including light gradient boosting machine and categorical boosting, and further enhanced through metaheuristic optimization techniques to maximize their performance. The two-tiered design organizes feature extraction and decision modeling into a coherent pipeline tailored for multi-class hospital admission classification. A comprehensive evaluation using a real-world hospital admission dataset demonstrates the framework's effectiveness, showcasing its utility for multi-class cardiac outcome prediction. Experimental results on publicly available datasets reveal that the best-performing models achieved a peak classification accuracy of 99.79%. Furthermore, the study employs explainable AI techniques to provide actionable insights into model predictions, guiding future data acquisition and enhancing the accurate classification of cardiac patients. The proposed framework has the potential to support informed decision-making in cardiac care, ultimately improving patient outcomes and healthcare services.

This study presents a novel machine learning-based approach to drug design, integrating large chemical language models (CLMs) with reinforcement learning (RL) to discover small molecule inhibitors of the epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR). By leveraging a multi-objective reward function that balances potency, drug-likeness, synthetic accessibility, and structural novelty, our framework enables the exploration of EGFR-relevant chemical space beyond known inhibitors. The model's output compounds demonstrate improved binding affinity compared to reference EGFR inhibitors and exhibit a high degree of novelty, with many featuring unique chemotypes not present in the training dataset. This research showcases the potential of combining CLMs with RL for accelerating target-focused de novo molecular design and provides a versatile framework for future applications in kinase inhibitor discovery. Furthermore, the approach's ability to generate diverse and chemically valid compounds highlights its potential for expanding the scope of existing drug discovery pipelines.

Recent advancements in machine learning have revitalized interest in computer-assisted chemical synthesis, a field that has been hindered by the complexity of chemical synthesis processes and the limited availability of relevant data. Despite the existence of computer-assisted synthesis for nearly a century, the scarcity of high-quality data has consistently hindered its widespread adoption. This study addresses the challenges associated with data quantity, quality, visibility, and accessibility by creating a comprehensive database, dubbed the Computer-Assisted Chemical Synthesis (CaCS) repository. The CaCS database serves as a centralized platform for storing, analyzing, and retrieving data, facilitating seamless integration with existing research projects. The database provides users with a programmatic interface to access data for tasks such as predicting synthesis outcomes, retrosynthetic analysis, synthesizability estimation, and chemical synthesis route planning and optimization. The database ensures reusability and traceability by archiving original data and storing processed data in an efficient manner. A case study demonstrates the practical application of the CaCS database within a contemporary computer-assisted chemical synthesis research project, highlighting its advantages and limitations. The code and documentation for the CaCS database are publicly available on GitHub under the MIT license, enabling researchers to leverage this resource for transparent processing and benchmarking purposes. This research contributes significantly to the field by providing a systematic overview of existing open-source data sources and an easily reproducible environment for data management and analysis.

Early and accurate diagnosis of breast cancer (BC) is crucial for effective treatment and improved patient outcomes. The integration of machine learning (ML) tools has significantly enhanced BC diagnosis, underscoring the need for innovative approaches to optimize classification models. This study proposes an enhanced BC classification model that leverages a hybrid optimization technique combining particle swarm optimization (PSO) and grey wolf optimizer (GWO) to improve the performance of stacked ML models. Utilizing a comprehensive dataset of 4024 patients with 16 attributes from the Kaggle repository, the study applies data preprocessing techniques to prepare the dataset for analysis. Feature selection is conducted using binary variants of PSO, GWO, and whale optimization algorithm (WOA), with BPSO demonstrating the best fit to the dataset. A suite of six ML models, including Random Forest (RF), Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD), Support Vector Machine (SVM), Gradient Boosting Classifier (GBC), k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN), and XGBoost Classifier (XGB), is employed to evaluate the proposed approach, with RF achieving the highest accuracy of 89.22%. A stacked model comprising RF, SGD, SVM, GBC, and k-NN as base learners, with XGB as the meta-learner, exhibits a significant improvement in classification accuracy of 93.91%. The hybrid PSO-GWO algorithm is then applied to optimize the stacked model, resulting in a substantial increase in accuracy to 99.49%. The study employs a range of evaluation metrics, including accuracy, precision, recall, F-score, and area under the curve (AUC), to assess the performance of the optimized model.

The accurate diagnosis of liver tumors is a complex task due to their inherent heterogeneity in etiology, pathology, and response to treatment. Traditional imaging modalities, such as multi-phase contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CT), provide valuable information for differentiation, but visual assessment alone is often hindered by overlapping features. To address this challenge, we introduce a comprehensive dataset, MCT-LTDiag, comprising 517 cases with four-phase contrast-enhanced CT scans and five distinct tumor subtypes: hepatocellular carcinoma, intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, colorectal liver metastasis, breast cancer liver metastasis, and hepatic hemangioma. This dataset features standardized preprocessing, rigorous quality control, and expert-annotated tumor masks, ensuring a high level of accuracy and reliability. Our preliminary experiments demonstrate the utility of MCT-LTDiag in radiomics-based machine learning and deep learning models, with the integration of multi-phase CT scans significantly enhancing diagnostic performance. The dataset serves as a benchmark for advancing automated liver tumor subtype classification and is made publicly available to facilitate future research and development in this critical area.

In the face of increasing healthcare demands, the management of patients with multimorbidity poses a significant challenge to healthcare systems worldwide. The identification of high-need patients with multiple chronic conditions is crucial to optimize care and alleviate system pressures. This study aims to uncover distinct patterns of multimorbidity and healthcare utilization among individuals with two or more chronic conditions. A weighted K-means clustering approach was employed on a dataset of 1,184,334 individuals, utilizing diagnostic algorithms based on ICD-10 and ATC codes to define chronic conditions. Sociodemographic characteristics were analyzed to describe the resulting clusters. The analysis revealed four distinct clusters based on chronic conditions and healthcare utilization patterns. Cluster 1 exhibited the highest healthcare utilization and a high burden of both somatic and mental health conditions, accompanied by lower socioeconomic status. In contrast, Cluster 2, predominantly comprising younger women with mental health conditions, demonstrated high utilization of psychological services and fewer somatic conditions. Cluster 3, the largest group, displayed low healthcare utilization and consisted of individuals with common, manageable conditions and relatively high socioeconomic status. Cluster 4 was characterized by older individuals with complex somatic conditions necessitating frequent interactions with general practitioners and specialists. The findings highlight the need for tailored care strategies, particularly for patients with mental health conditions, taking into account their socioeconomic status, to enhance care quality and optimize resource allocation.

The increasing application of artificial intelligence (AI) in clinical research has significantly improved diagnostic accuracy, prognostic modeling, and disease monitoring, particularly in complex and data-constrained areas such as rare diseases. Myasthenia Gravis (MG), a rare neuromuscular disorder, has seen a growing number of AI-driven studies aimed at addressing its diagnostic and clinical heterogeneity. However, the methodological quality and reporting standards of these studies remain largely unexamined. This study presents a longitudinal evaluation of AI research in MG using the CLARITY AI framework, a modular tool designed to assess both structural rigor and macro-topical completeness. An analysis of 20 peer-reviewed studies published between 2020 and 2024 revealed a significant improvement in the average total scores, from 235.0 in 2021 to 256.15 in 2024, with notable gains in evaluation metrics, data availability, and study type and objectives. A post hoc robustness check confirmed the stability of these temporal trends. Despite these improvements, critical deficiencies persist in usability testing, user engagement, and ethical reporting, where scores often remained below 1. These findings suggest that while technical sophistication is advancing, the translational readiness of AI applications for rare diseases is limited by the underreporting of human-centered and ethical dimensions. This work provides actionable insights and establishes a benchmark for enhancing transparency, methodological rigor, and clinical relevance in AI applications for rare diseases.

The increasing adoption of machine learning (ML) has led to significant advancements in various fields, but its indiscriminate application can result in resource waste, unforeseen complexities, and project failure. To address this issue, this study aims to provide a systematic approach to determining the optimal use of ML in problem-solving workflows. A thorough examination of existing decision-making frameworks and guidelines reveals their limitations in defining ML applicability, highlighting the need for a more structured approach. Through a comprehensive literature review and analysis, this research identifies explicit suitability criteria to assess when ML should be preferred over traditional engineering methods. Based on these findings, a conceptual decision-making framework is proposed, incorporating practical steps and checklists to guide engineers in method selection, model development, validation, and deployment. The framework aims to facilitate informed decision-making and ensure the effective integration of ML into problem-solving workflows, while also highlighting critical challenges and potential areas for future research.

Characterizing the transition state (TS) is crucial for reaction modeling, but conventional density functional theory (DFT) methods are computationally expensive. Machine-learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) have shown promise in achieving quantum-level accuracy at a lower cost, but their performance is often limited by the lack of large-scale Hessian data. To address this challenge, we have compiled the largest dataset of quantum-chemistry Hessian matrices for reactive systems, comprising 1.84 million matrices at the ωB97x/6-31G(d) level. To efficiently leverage this second-order information, we introduce a novel training approach, Hessian-informed training with stochastic row sampling, which controls the computational overhead of incorporating Hessians into the training process. Our results demonstrate that HORM yields significant improvements in TS optimization, with MLIPs trained with Hessian data achieving up to 63% lower Hessian mean absolute error and up to 200 times faster TS-search efficiency compared to their counterparts trained without Hessians. This breakthrough fills critical gaps in both data and methodology, enabling the development of more accurate and robust reactive MLIPs and facilitating the exploration of complex reaction networks at scale.

This study introduces a comprehensive dataset focused on the behavior of underwater umbilicals associated with Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs), specifically the BlueROV2. The dataset encompasses data from three distinct sources: a Motion Capture System (mo-cap system), onboard sensors of the BlueROV2, and a surface-mounted tension sensor. The mo-cap system tracks the ROV and its tether movements, while the tension sensor measures the force exerted by the cable tension on the surface side, near the tether drum. The dataset was collected for research purposes aimed at advancing machine learning and data-driven control methods for modeling, estimation, and control of underwater cables. The dataset can serve as a benchmark for developing and validating underwater tether models, enabling researchers to develop control methods that account for tether drag or entanglement, thereby enhancing navigation accuracy of ROVs and facilitating the development of automated tether management systems. This research details the experimental setup, data acquisition procedures, and post-processing techniques employed to create the dataset, accompanied by illustrative plots highlighting key features of the collected data.

This study addresses the critical challenge of predicting the health status of real-life turbofan engines to ensure flight safety and minimize maintenance costs. A significant body of evidence suggests that gradual performance degradation in these engines can have severe consequences if left undetected. To address this issue, we propose a novel approach based on a heterogeneous ensemble deep neural network (HEDNN) for multi-class health status prediction. Our methodology involves collecting a comprehensive dataset of 43,492 operational records from a dual-spool, variable geometry turbofan engine, spanning a 12-year period. We preprocess the raw sensor data using a robust z-score filter to remove outliers and a moving median imputation method to address missing temperature entries. The preprocessed dataset is then partitioned into training and testing sets, with 80% of the data used for training and 20% for testing. The data is labeled into three health classes based on performance margin and specific fuel consumption indices. We develop three base learners: a bidirectional LSTM network, a one-dimensional CNN, and a BiGRU network, each trained using the Adam optimizer with piecewise learning rate decay. The individual networks produce class probability vectors that are fused in a shallow fully connected ensemble. Our results show that the HEDNN outperforms standalone architectures, achieving a root mean squared error of 0.3421, mean absolute percentage error of 4.78%, and overall accuracy of 95.32%. These findings demonstrate the potential of our approach as a robust, data-driven prognostic tool for predictive maintenance of turbofan fleets.

The escalating rates of student dropout within academic institutions have prompted the exploration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) solutions to address this pressing issue. Traditionally, AI-based approaches rely heavily on administrative and academic data, often neglecting the importance of personal information about students. A previous study demonstrated the potential of machine learning models in extracting valuable insights from this data, yet the availability of labeled data remained a significant challenge. The distribution of data across various systems and the time-consuming process of collection, particularly for new degree programs, hinder the widespread adoption of AI in this context. Furthermore, institutions lacking labeled data are unable to fully leverage the capabilities of AI. To overcome this limitation, unsupervised clustering algorithms have been employed to uncover latent patterns within unlabeled data. However, the validation of resulting clusters is crucial, especially when dealing with datasets comprising both numerical and categorical attributes. This study presents a comprehensive comparison of various clustering algorithms to identify the most effective technique for uncovering the underlying factors contributing to university student attrition using unlabeled data. The proposed methodology integrates diverse data preprocessing techniques to streamline the selection of the optimal combination of algorithms and data transformations for harmonizing numerical and categorical information. A real-world case study utilizing academic data from a Spanish university provides empirical validation for the proposed approach, while an exploratory analysis sheds light on the factors driving cluster formation. The findings of this study can be applied to analogous scenarios where social or economic data are scarce and academic attributes dominate.

Parkinson's disease is a widespread neurodegenerative disorder characterized by the presence of dysarthria, a speech disorder resulting from neurological damage that impairs speech intelligibility. Conventional assessments of dysarthria severity rely on subjective auditory evaluations, which are time-consuming and require specialized expertise. To address this limitation, this study proposes an automated framework for objectively classifying the severity of speech disorders in Parkinson's disease patients using machine learning techniques. A dataset of speech recordings from 221 patients at the University Hospital of Kiel, Germany, was analyzed. The classification process involves two stages: the extraction of acoustic features using linear predictive coding, mel-frequency cepstral coefficients, and discrete wavelet packet decomposition, followed by feature matching using k-nearest neighbors, support vector machines, and neural networks. A novel voting strategy was developed, where voice records are segmented into short intervals and individually analyzed, with final subject-level predictions derived through majority voting across segments. This ensemble approach significantly enhances accuracy, with the combination of discrete wavelet packet decomposition and k-nearest neighbors achieving an accuracy of 89.7% and a macro-F1 score of 89.3% in a three-class system. The successful implementation of this methodology in clinical settings has the potential to improve the treatment of speech disorders in Parkinson's disease patients and enhance the effectiveness of speech therapies by providing an objective, efficient, and reliable assessment tool. This can facilitate more effective patient monitoring and faster, more consistent evaluations, particularly in therapy follow-ups and telemedicine applications.

This study addresses the challenges in Micro-Expression Recognition (MER) by developing a novel framework that integrates multiple machine learning techniques to enhance recognition accuracy and adaptability. The proposed framework leverages a 2D Convolutional Neural Network (2DCNN) for feature extraction, capturing the intricate spatial details of Facial Expressions (FEs). To address the issue of feature alignment, a Heteroscedastic Neural Network (HNN) is introduced to estimate predictive uncertainty. A two-stage learning process is employed, where Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL) optimization refines model parameters, and Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) ensures better alignment between micro- and macro-expressions. Furthermore, Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) and Meta-Reinforcement Learning (Meta-RL) are used to optimize feature learning and improve generalization. The proposed approach is evaluated on benchmark datasets, including SAMM and CASME II, achieving state-of-the-art results with 96.74% accuracy on SAMM and 98.84% accuracy on CASME II. The findings demonstrate the robustness, adaptability, and effectiveness of the proposed framework, making it well-suited for real-world micro-expression analysis applications. The integration of multiple machine learning techniques enables the model to learn from diverse data distributions and improve recognition accuracy, showcasing its potential for practical applications in affective computing and human-computer interaction.

This study provides a comprehensive review of Arabic Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD), a critical challenge in Natural Language Processing (NLP) due to the language's inherent complexities and limited availability of annotated datasets. Despite these challenges, recent advancements in contextualized methods, particularly deep learning and transformer models, have significantly improved WSD performance. This research systematically examines traditional knowledge-based methods, machine learning approaches, deep learning models, and hybrid techniques, with a focus on transformer models such as BERT, which have demonstrated exceptional performance in Arabic WSD. The evaluation criteria for model selection include performance on benchmark datasets and relevance to real-world applications, with a structured comparison of Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-score metrics across various studies. Notably, this study integrates multiple evaluation metrics from diverse studies, offering a more comprehensive analysis than existing surveys. The findings highlight the need for larger annotated datasets and improved contextual understanding, while also identifying potential avenues for future research, including domain adaptation and cross-dialectal WSD.

This study addresses the challenge of applying large language models (LLMs) to pharmaceutical contraindications in healthcare settings, where accurate information is crucial. To enhance the effectiveness of LLMs in this domain, we developed a Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline, integrating OpenAI's GPT-4o-mini as the base model and text-embedding-3-small for embeddings, orchestrated by LangChain. Our approach leverages Drug Utilization Review (DUR) data from public databases to create a hybrid retrieval system with re-ranking, focusing on contraindications for specific age groups, pregnancy, and concomitant drug use. The dataset comprises 300 question-answer pairs across three categories, with baseline model accuracy ranging from 0.49 to 0.57. Following the integration of the RAG pipeline, we observed a significant improvement in model accuracy, achieving rates of 0.94, 0.87, and 0.89 for contraindications related to age groups, pregnancy, and concomitant drug use, respectively. These results demonstrate the potential of augmenting LLMs with a RAG framework to provide more precise and reliable drug contraindication information, ultimately reducing uncertainty in prescription and drug intake decisions.

This study addresses the complex challenge of dimension reduction in datasets exhibiting nonlinear and time-dependent processes. Effective dimension reduction is crucial for retaining the intrinsic dynamics of the data while minimizing the number of features. To address this challenge, we introduce Average Volatility Dimensioning (AVD), a novel technique that transforms multiple dimensions into a single univariate signal, capturing the relationships between features. This work presents the underlying mechanisms of AVD and compares its performance with nine established dimension reduction methods. We evaluate the ability of AVD to preserve data information by applying the reduced dimensions to machine learning classification tasks and examining the resulting phase-space portraits. The validation is conducted on eight diverse datasets featuring nonlinear time series data from domains such as movement recognition, fault detection, and environmental monitoring. Our results demonstrate that AVD consistently outperforms other methods in retaining data dynamics, achieving superior performance in machine learning classification tasks while maintaining an interpretable phase-space trajectory and resisting noise. Furthermore, AVD exhibits computational efficiency, making it a promising approach for handling high-dimensional datasets.

In the realm of code auditing, the development of sophisticated models and methods has been a significant area of research. However, despite the advancements in this field, the interpretability of these models remains a pressing concern. Specifically, machine learning models designed to identify vulnerable code often lack transparency into their decision-making processes and the characteristics of code they deem vulnerable. To address this knowledge gap, this study introduces Program-PLATE, a novel method for evaluating the capability of machine learning models in extracting features relevant to code vulnerabilities. By creating a PLATE-dataset from a single vulnerable file, Program-PLATE enables a more objective assessment of model performance on this dataset. This study applies Program-PLATE to a range of machine learning models, conducts a thorough analysis of the results, and provides insights into the strengths and limitations of these models. Furthermore, the study offers recommendations for future research directions to enhance the interpretability and effectiveness of code auditing models.

The integration of explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) into customer analytics has become increasingly prominent due to the growing demand for transparent and trustworthy predictive models. This systematic review examines the application of XAI techniques, including SHAP, LIME, and other machine learning models, in the context of churn prediction, customer segmentation, and retention strategies. A comprehensive search of prominent databases yielded 80 studies published between 2015 and 2022, which were analyzed to categorize XAI approaches by industry application, interpretability outcomes, and methodology. The findings reveal that XAI not only fosters stakeholder trust but also enables more informed and targeted customer interventions. A comparative analysis of XAI techniques for churn prediction, customer segmentation, and retention strategies highlights their potential to improve model decision-making and transparency. However, the review also identifies limitations in real-time deployment, standardization, and evaluation metrics, underscoring the need for further research in XAI models, hybrid models, and domain-specific explainability contexts. The study provides valuable insights into the current state of XAI in customer analytics, shedding light on its potential to enhance model interpretability and decision-making in business applications.

In the era of big data, identifying significant variables from complex datasets has become increasingly challenging. Variable selection is a critical task in establishing relationships between predictors and response variables, while maintaining statistical power and controlling the False Discovery Rate (FDR). Recent advances in statistical methods have led to the development of innovative approaches, such as the knockoff filter, which generates artificial variables that mimic the correlation structure of the original variables, serving as negative controls for inference. This study extends the application of knockoffs to Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM), a fast and accurate machine learning technique. To enhance the interpretability of LightGBM, Shapely Additive Explanations (SHAP) values are employed. Through a comprehensive experimental evaluation, our proposed method demonstrates superior performance compared to traditional approaches, accurately identifying important variables for each class across multiple datasets. The results of an extensive simulation study validate the effectiveness of our approach, which integrates knockoffs into LightGBM to enhance performance and interpretability. Our research contributes to the advancement of variable selection methods, providing a valuable tool for identifying relevant variables in statistical modeling and machine learning applications.

This study leverages advancements in machine learning to address the challenges hindering organic solar cell (OSC) materials discovery. The scarcity of informatics-ready data and the complexity of numerical descriptors have long impeded comprehensive analysis of OSC performance. To bridge this gap, we develop a large language model (LLM)-driven framework that extracts structured data from literature and predicts device performance using natural language embeddings. Our framework is trained on a meticulously curated dataset of 422 OSC devices, showcasing strong predictive accuracy across key performance metrics: power conversion efficiency (PCE), short-circuit current (JSC), open-circuit voltage (VOC), and fill factor (FF). Notably, the model achieves high correlation coefficients of 0.87 for PCE, 0.82 for JSC, 0.89 for VOC, and 0.59 for FF. Building on this foundation, we employ the models to explore the vast space of 1.4 million OSC material combinations, experimental variables, and device architectures. The resulting analysis yields data-driven design guidelines, pinpointing optimal donor-acceptor pairs and processing conditions that consistently yield superior device performance. These findings have significant implications for the development of high-performance OSCs and highlight the potential of machine learning to accelerate materials discovery in the field.

This study explores the application of automated machine learning (AutoML) in predicting case durations and delays within the Indian judicial system. By leveraging a large dataset comprising 22.4 million criminal cases from lower courts across India, we develop a tree-based ensemble model integrated with explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) techniques to forecast case delay and duration while prioritizing interpretability. The analysis focuses on variables influencing case length and delays, such as case type, existing backlog, and jurisdiction type, which are systematically examined at the time of case filing. To enhance predictive accuracy, we employ two distinct models: a regression model for predicting on-time case durations and a separate regression model for delayed cases, resulting in a significant reduction in prediction error. The on-time case model achieves a lower RMSE of 4.90 months, while the delayed case model achieves a lower RMSE of 13.79 months. A classification model is also developed to augment the regression model, demonstrating 80% accuracy with a weighted F1 score of 84.1% for delay prediction. Furthermore, a hybrid classification-regression framework showcases potential for improved delay and duration prediction in end-to-end simulations. The application of XAI provides transparent explanations for predictions, enabling stakeholders to understand the reasoning behind predicted durations. The developed models can support case management, identify areas prone to delays, and inform policies to enhance the efficiency, timeliness, and transparency of the Indian judiciary.

This study investigates the challenges of managing patients with unstable pelvic fractures accompanied by traumatic brain injury (TBI), where fluid resuscitation and cerebral perfusion pressure often conflict. A comprehensive analysis was conducted on 204 patients with this complex condition between 2010 and 2025. A novel machine learning approach was employed to develop a predictive model that integrates multiple risk factors. The model identified shock, abnormal systolic blood pressure, non-surgical management, and prolonged coagulation time as key indicators of poor prognosis. A predictive nomogram was constructed using systolic blood pressure, shock, pelvic fracture surgery, and prothrombin time, demonstrating excellent performance with an area under the curve of 0.801 and a concordance index of 0.7747. The results reveal a U-shaped correlation between systolic blood pressure and prognosis, with the lowest risk observed at a systolic blood pressure range of 110-122 mmHg. The developed models provide a valuable tool for personalized blood pressure management, coagulation monitoring, and surgical decision-making, ultimately improving patient outcomes in this high-risk population.

This study introduces an explainable machine learning approach to classify traditional Korean ceramics, encompassing celadon, buncheong, and white porcelain, based on X-ray fluorescence chemical composition data. A comprehensive dataset of 624 samples was analyzed using six machine learning algorithms, including principal component analysis-linear discriminant analysis, decision tree, random forest, extreme gradient boosting, k-nearest neighbors, and support vector machine. Among these models, tree-based approaches, specifically random forest and extreme gradient boosting, yielded the highest classification accuracy of 95.8%. Notably, white porcelain was consistently identified across all models, whereas celadon and buncheong exhibited partial misclassification due to overlapping chemical characteristics. To enhance model interpretability, Shapley additive explanations were employed, revealing Fe2O3 and TiO2 as the most influential components for type differentiation, aligning with established ceramic coloration mechanisms. These findings demonstrate the efficacy of explainable machine learning in chemical-based ceramic classification and provide a quantitative framework that complements traditional typological approaches in cultural heritage research, offering a valuable tool for the analysis and preservation of cultural artifacts.

This study addresses the complex challenge of tailoring the rheological properties of polymers to achieve precise shear-rate-dependent viscosity profiles. The intricate interplay of molecular chemistry, polymer architecture, and intermolecular interactions hinders the design of polymers with desired rheological responses. To overcome this challenge, we developed a computational framework that integrates topology-aware generative machine learning, Gaussian process modeling, and multiparticle collision dynamics. This framework enables the design of polymers with prescribed viscosity profiles by systematically modifying their topology and solvophobicity, while keeping other variables constant. Our results show that the framework successfully identifies polymers that meet low- and medium-difficulty rheological targets by optimizing topology and solvophobicity. Notably, we observed design degeneracy in these regimes, where distinct polymers produce nearly identical rheological profiles. However, achieving high-difficulty targets requires extrapolating beyond the initial design space, which is facilitated by physical scaling theories. Our integrated framework offers a data-driven approach to rational polymer design, providing a valuable tool for the development of materials with tailored rheological properties.

The proliferation of cloud-based Business Intelligence (BI) outsourcing has heightened concerns regarding sensitive data protection. As organizations increasingly rely on cloud computing for data analysis, safeguarding sensitive information while preserving its analytical value becomes a pressing challenge. This study proposes an artificial intelligence-driven automated solution to address this issue. By integrating machine learning algorithms with data anonymization techniques such as pseudonymization and data masking, our solution effectively conceals sensitive data while maintaining its integrity for analytical purposes. A comprehensive evaluation of our approach demonstrates that the anonymized data retains its analytical utility with negligible impact on performance and accuracy, thereby providing a robust foundation for secure and efficient BI outsourcing over cloud environments. The results of this research underscore the potential of AI-driven data anonymization in mitigating the risks associated with sensitive data exposure, paving the way for widespread adoption of cloud-based BI solutions.

Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH) significantly elevates the risk of liver-related mortality, underscoring the pressing need for reliable predictive biomarkers. This study aimed to develop a non-invasive diagnostic tool for MASH, leveraging machine learning techniques to integrate routine clinical and biochemical data. Employing a machine learning framework, we derived the MASHRisk score from a dataset comprising 218 participants and validated its performance across multiple cohorts, including 93, 96, and 26,256 individuals. The MASHRisk score demonstrated exceptional diagnostic accuracy, with area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) values of 0.791, 0.793, 0.806, and 0.796 across training, validation, and test sets, respectively. Notably, the score emerged as an independent predictor of MASH and outperformed existing indices, including Fibrosis-4, aspartate aminotransferase to Platelet Ratio Index, aspartate aminotransferase to alanine aminotransferase Ratio, and Non-Alcoholic Fatty Liver Disease Fibrosis Score. Furthermore, in a large prognostic cohort of 390,574 individuals, high-risk participants exhibited significantly elevated hazard ratios for liver-related mortality, MASH, cirrhosis, hepatocellular carcinoma, atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease-related mortality, and all-cause mortality, underscoring the MASHRisk score's potential as a valuable tool for early detection, risk stratification, and outcome prediction in MASH.

Liver disease is a significant public health concern, often characterized by asymptomatic progression and potentially fatal outcomes. The application of machine learning (ML) techniques has shown promise in enhancing the understanding and prediction of liver disease. However, the performance of ML models is heavily reliant on data preprocessing, which involves data cleaning, standardization, and handling of missing values, feature scaling, and categorical encoding. Despite its critical role, the impact of different preprocessing strategies on liver disease prediction models remains poorly understood. This study aims to systematically investigate the effect of normalization, imputation methods, and feature encoding on the performance of multiple ML classifiers for liver disease prediction. A novel preprocessing sensitivity index (PSI) is introduced to quantify the variance in model performance resulting from preprocessing changes. Through a comprehensive evaluation of five common ML classifiers, this study reveals that specific preprocessing pipelines consistently outperform others in liver disease datasets, yielding significant improvements in prediction accuracy. Notably, a substantial increase in accuracy of 8-12% is observed following data preprocessing. The findings highlight the crucial role of preprocessing in mitigating data inconsistencies and noise, as well as enhancing the model's ability to detect subtle patterns within the data, ultimately leading to improved liver disease prediction.

Accurate localization of perfusion deficits in diffusion-weighted MRI (DWI) scans is a crucial aspect of acute ischemic stroke management, as it directly impacts treatment decisions and patient outcomes. Current deep learning methods often rely on binary segmentation, which oversimplifies the complex nature of ischemic injury and discards valuable information within the lesion. To address this limitation, we introduce a novel framework, StrokeFlow, that represents the ischemic region as a continuous field. By leveraging a coordinate-based network architecture, StrokeFlow outputs a smooth ischemic density field, quantifying voxel-level infarction probability. Furthermore, we incorporate a vector flow component, trained to learn a directional vector field that aligns with the negative gradient of the Apparent Diffusion Coefficient (ADC) map. This allows StrokeFlow to model the directionality of perfusion deficits, providing a more nuanced understanding of ischemic injury. In our evaluation on the ISLES 2022 dataset, StrokeFlow demonstrated superior lesion boundary accuracy, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines in the 95% Hausdorff Distance metric. Additionally, the model exhibited enhanced sensitivity in detecting small and multifocal lesions, underscoring its potential as a valuable tool for clinical assessment of ischemic stroke. By shifting the paradigm from discrete segmentation to continuous, functionally-aware fields, StrokeFlow offers a more biologically plausible and interpretable approach to ischemic stroke diagnosis.

The proliferation of smart devices and connected vehicles has led to an exponential surge in mobile data traffic, placing immense pressure on wireless networks to maintain quality of service and efficiently manage handover (HO) processes. As a crucial component of next-generation intelligent transportation systems, vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V) communication is pivotal in ensuring seamless mobility, particularly in high-speed vehicular environments where frequent HO events occur. This study undertakes a comprehensive examination of HO decision techniques specifically tailored to V2V communications in 6G networks, focusing on the core challenges of mobility management, security, interference, and service continuity. A thorough evaluation of state-of-the-art HO algorithms, encompassing Bayesian regression, fuzzy logic, machine learning, and software-defined networking approaches, is conducted to identify their strengths and limitations. Furthermore, the unique opportunities offered by 6G technologies, including ultra-reliable low-latency communication, terahertz spectrum, and AI-native networking, are explored to enhance the stability, efficiency, and reliability of V2V HO management. The research highlights the need for advanced HO mechanisms to support the requirements of autonomous driving and intelligent transportation systems, underscoring the significance of continued innovation in this field.

The gastrointestinal system's inherent biological variability poses significant challenges in predicting the effects of food on drug absorption, thereby impacting the efficacy and safety of administered medications. Traditional methods, including in vitro and in vivo models, have limitations in capturing the full scope of food-drug interactions. This study investigates the predictive capabilities and accuracy of machine learning (ML) algorithms in forecasting food effects (FE) in comparison to conventional approaches. By leveraging food dataset information, ML models can enhance the formulation and dosing of drugs, thereby optimizing their bioavailability. Recent advancements in FE prediction, its underlying mechanisms, and the impact on drug pharmacokinetics are examined. The study analyzes the potential of supervised and unsupervised learning, as well as reinforcement learning, in forecasting absorption, distribution, metabolism, and elimination (ADME) processes and drug development. While ML shows promise in addressing the limitations of traditional methods, concerns regarding data quality, model generalizability, and integration into the drug development process must be addressed. Furthermore, the integration of ML with emerging technologies, such as physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) modeling, is explored to enhance its prospects in the field of drug development. The study also examines the potential of deep learning, explainable artificial intelligence, and the ethical and legal considerations of applying ML in pharmacokinetics, highlighting the need for interdisciplinary approaches to improve patient care outcomes.

The aviation industry relies heavily on effective aircraft maintenance to guarantee safety, reliability, and cost-efficiency.  Advancements in artificial intelligence (AI) offer promising opportunities to revolutionize various facets of aircraft maintenance, including predictive maintenance, fault diagnosis, and overall aircraft health management. This study conducts a comprehensive review of AI technologies, specifically Automated Reasoning and Deep Learning, as applied to aircraft maintenance.  A thorough literature search encompassing publications from 1984 to 2024 yielded a dataset of 696 articles focusing on AI applications in airworthiness management, aircraft health monitoring, and maintenance, repair, and overhaul operations.  Analysis of these publications revealed key AI technologies and their corresponding applications in aircraft maintenance processes, highlighting emerging trends, prominent research areas, and potential gaps in knowledge. The review concludes by offering insights into the adoption of AI in aircraft maintenance and its potential impact on researchers, industry professionals, educators, and other stakeholders.

The integration of artificial intelligence (AI) into online education is rapidly expanding, prompting a need to understand its impact on autonomous learners, particularly within discussion forums. This scoping review aimed to synthesize existing research on AI applications in discussion forums and characterize the resulting learning experience. Utilizing the SALSA framework and PRISMA flow diagram, a comprehensive search of Scopus yielded twelve relevant articles. These articles explored six key applications of AI: intelligent tutoring systems, AI-assisted applications, chatbots, automatic formative assessment, AI teaching assistants, and AI-assisted lifelong learning assistants.  

The review identified three primary models of human-AI interaction in discussion forums: student-AI partnerships, student-AI-student partnerships, and student-AI-teacher partnerships.  Analysis revealed that cognitive presence, particularly in relation to individual learning outcomes, was more prominent than social presence. Three distinct perspectives emerged regarding AI's role in learning: AI as a virtual teacher supplementing human instruction, AI as a facilitator of information access through interactions, and AI as an automated evaluator providing feedback and adapting the learning process.  

While the focus often centered on individual student support, the reviewed articles demonstrated a diverse range of applications across various disciplines. This suggests that AI in discussion forums operates within a complex interplay of technology, pedagogy, and the learning environment, highlighting the concept of entangled pedagogies. In conclusion, this review underscores the evolving role of AI in online discussion forums, with chatbots and generative AI playing prominent roles.  Emerging applications include virtual teaching, peer/message matching, and automated evaluation, ultimately aiming to enhance student learning and communication within the learning environment.

Traditional reactive maintenance practices in infrastructure and facilities management often lead to unexpected downtime and escalating costs. This study investigates the potential of machine learning to transition towards proactive, data-driven predictive maintenance strategies.  To achieve this, several machine learning algorithms, including K-Nearest Neighbors, Support Vector Classifier, Random Forest Classifier, and Extreme Gradient Boosting, were applied to a comprehensive dataset containing operational metrics such as temperature, torque, rotational speed, and tool wear, encompassing various failure modes. 

The results demonstrate that Extreme Gradient Boosting achieved the highest predictive accuracy (98.9%) for multiclass failure prediction, with an AUC score close to 1.0 and F1 scores exceeding 0.98 in both validation and test sets. Random Forest Classifier and Support Vector Classifier followed closely, achieving precision and recall rates above 95%.  While K-Nearest Neighbors exhibited slightly lower accuracy (~96.6%), its rapid inference capabilities make it suitable for real-time applications.  

The implementation of preprocessing techniques, including feature scaling, label encoding, and synthetic minority oversampling, enhanced the robustness of the models in addressing class imbalances within the dataset.  Torque and tool wear emerged as critical predictive features, aligning with established mechanical failure patterns.  This research highlights the transformative potential of AI in optimizing maintenance practices by enabling granular failure diagnostics and timely interventions, ultimately minimizing operational costs and preventing catastrophic infrastructure failures.  By integrating interpretability, cross-model benchmarking, and scalability considerations, this study advances the field of AI-driven predictive maintenance, positioning it as a key component of modern infrastructure resilience and sustainability.

This study investigates the effectiveness of machine learning algorithms for identifying depressive symptoms in Arabic tweets, aiming to address the lack of mental health resources for Arabic-speaking populations.  To facilitate this research, a novel dataset, ArabMindGuard (AMG), was created, containing 3083 tweets annotated by experts and sourced from Saudi Arabia.  The study systematically compares the performance of nine machine learning classifiers across five diverse datasets representing various Arabic dialects, including Saudi, Jordanian, and Modern Standard Arabic.  A comprehensive evaluation framework, utilizing metrics such as precision, recall, F1-score, and statistical significance testing, was employed.  Advanced natural language processing techniques specific to Arabic, including TF-IDF, N-gram analysis, and CAMeL tools, were integrated into the analysis.  The results demonstrate that ensemble methods and Support Vector Machines consistently achieved the highest F1-scores, ranging from 0.82 to 0.95, across all datasets.  These findings underscore the significant influence of dialectal variations on depression detection and establish benchmark performance metrics for future deep learning applications in this domain.  This research contributes to the development of culturally sensitive digital mental health interventions for the Arab world and provides a methodological foundation for Arabic mental health text analysis.

Ensuring the integrity and trustworthiness of electronic voting systems is paramount for maintaining public confidence in democratic processes. Existing security measures, such as centralized authentication and traditional cryptography, often face challenges related to scalability and single points of failure. Blockchain-based solutions, while promising, grapple with issues like limited throughput, high energy consumption, and vulnerability to emerging quantum threats.  This study proposes PQSHAC, a novel blockchain-based voting mechanism designed to enhance privacy and trust in electronic voting. PQSHAC leverages a hybrid consensus approach and a novel sharding protocol to optimize data distribution and mitigate skewness within shards, thereby improving system performance.  A unique token generation protocol is implemented to prevent fraudulent voting and safeguard voter anonymity.  Experimental results demonstrate that PQSHAC achieves a throughput of approximately 106 blocks per second with a block generation time of 28 seconds for 3000 blocks using a five-shard configuration. Furthermore, the proposed modular ledger architecture significantly reduces storage requirements by around 60% compared to traditional approaches, while maintaining robust security against quantum attacks through the integration of Dilithium-3 cryptography. These findings highlight the potential of PQSHAC as a secure and efficient solution for modern electronic voting systems.

Transverse cracking poses a significant challenge to the durability and performance of Continuously Reinforced Concrete Pavements (CRCP), impacting ride quality and necessitating costly maintenance interventions.  This study proposes a novel hybrid predictive model to enhance the accuracy of transverse crack prediction in CRCP. The model integrates Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) with Gradient Boosting Machine (GBM) algorithms.  A dataset of 395 records from 33 pavement sections, sourced from the Long-Term Pavement Performance (LTPP) program, was utilized. This dataset encompassed a range of factors influencing pavement performance, including structural, environmental, traffic, and performance-related parameters. PSO was employed to optimize critical hyperparameters of the GBM model, specifically the number of iterations, learning rate, and tree depth.  The resulting PSO-GBM model demonstrated superior predictive performance compared to conventional machine learning models such as Random Forest, Support Vector Regression, Artificial Neural Networks, and Linear Regression.  The model achieved an average Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) of 1.62 and an R-squared value of 0.99 under 5-fold cross-validation.  Sensitivity analysis identified L3 thickness, L4 thickness, and Annual Average Daily Traffic (AADT) as the most influential factors in crack development, aligning with established engineering understanding.  Residual distribution and equality line plots confirmed the robustness and stability of the proposed approach across varying crack severity levels.

The proliferation of fake news in the digital age poses a significant challenge to the dissemination of accurate information. To address this issue, this study investigates the effectiveness of an improved convolutional neural network (CNN) model for news authenticity recognition.  The proposed model leverages deep learning techniques and undergoes optimization to enhance its performance in distinguishing genuine news from fabricated content.  Experimental results demonstrate that the improved CNN model achieves superior accuracy compared to traditional methods and models employing random deactivation techniques.  

Under identical training conditions, the optimized CNN model achieved an accuracy of 96.97% after 50 iterations, surpassing the 89.68% accuracy of the random deactivation-based model.  Furthermore, the model exhibited strong performance across diverse datasets.  On the FakeNewsNet dataset, it achieved a normalized mutual information of 84.82%, outperforming traditional methods by 5.05% and random deactivation-based methods by 10.25%.  Similarly, on the LIAR dataset, the model attained an adjusted Rand index of 87.32%.  This research highlights the potential of advanced CNN models in combating fake news and underscores their significance for safeguarding information security, upholding public access to truthful information, and promoting the dissemination of accurate news.

This study presents a novel hybrid machine learning approach for classifying weld bead defects in robotic Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW) processes.  This research contributes to the advancement of intelligent manufacturing and quality control within industrial automation.  The objective is to accurately categorize six distinct weld bead defect types: lack of fusion, burn-through, misalignment, lack of penetration, contamination, and good weld.  To achieve this, a hybrid model is developed that integrates Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Vision Transformers (ViTs).  This combination leverages the strengths of CNNs in extracting localized features and ViTs in capturing global contextual information, aiming to surpass the performance of traditional architectures like ResNet and standard CNN models.  Evaluation of the proposed hybrid model demonstrates its effectiveness and reliability across various operational conditions.  The results suggest that incorporating hybrid deep learning models into industrial automation systems holds significant potential for enhancing weld bead defect detection, ultimately reducing operational inefficiencies and ensuring consistent weld quality in manufacturing.

Sentiment analysis in low-resource languages like Greek often faces challenges due to limited labeled data. This study proposes a hybrid framework that addresses this issue by integrating a novel lexicon construction method with an Active Learning (AL) strategy.  The lexicon is built using two unique metrics: Score<inf>w</inf>, which quantifies word-level sentiment polarity, and Weight<inf>w</inf>, which considers both emotional and statistical significance. These metrics enable the transformation of coarse-grained tweet-level annotations into more nuanced word-level representations.  

The framework's performance was evaluated using four classification models: Naïve Bayes, Logistic Regression, Support Vector Machines, and Decision Trees.  Results demonstrate that incorporating lexicon-derived features significantly improves accuracy and F1-score, particularly for Logistic Regression and Decision Trees.  

To further enhance efficiency, a dynamic AL approach was implemented, where the lexicon is iteratively updated using the progressively labeled dataset. This strategy achieved comparable accuracy and Macro F1-score to a fully supervised approach while requiring only 30% of the original labeled data.  The findings highlight the effectiveness of combining lexicon refinement with uncertainty-based sampling, offering a cost-effective and scalable solution for sentiment analysis in resource-constrained languages.

The potential of using static word embeddings derived from scientific literature for materials design has been explored.  These embeddings, representing atoms as vectors, are hypothesized to encapsulate physicochemical information. However, the impact of variability in these embeddings, potentially caused by the heterogeneity of training data from diverse scientific domains, remains unclear. This study investigates the variability in atomic embeddings and its influence on the transfer of domain-specific knowledge.  

The research analyzed the extent of variability in embeddings associated with atoms and its relationship to the encoded physicochemical information.  A regression model was employed to predict the formation energy of compounds, utilizing joined atomic embeddings as input features.  The results demonstrate significant variability in individual atomic embeddings, which is strongly correlated with the specific vocabulary used in the language modeling process.  

Despite this variability, the composite embeddings of compounds effectively mapped to physicochemical properties, enabling accurate predictions of compound stability.  Furthermore, dimensional reduction techniques applied to the compound vectors did not compromise the encoded information or the model's predictive performance. This study concludes that, despite inherent variability, joined atomic embeddings derived from scientific literature can serve as robust predictors in materials design applications.

The development of effective representations of atomic configurations for machine learning models has resulted in a variety of descriptor sets. However, many existing sets exhibit limitations such as incompleteness or functional dependence. Incomplete sets fail to accurately capture the complexities of atomic environments, while highly dependent sets contain redundant descriptors that do not enhance the discrimination between different atomic configurations. This study addresses these challenges by utilizing pattern recognition techniques to identify and eliminate functionally dependent descriptors, aiming to create the most concise descriptor sets that maintain completeness.  This approach is applied to two distinct scenarios: refining an existing descriptor, the atomic cluster expansion, and augmenting an incomplete descriptor set to develop a novel message-passing neural network architecture capable of recognizing five-body patterns. The resulting architecture demonstrates high accuracy on established benchmarks while preserving computational efficiency. These findings highlight the effectiveness of this strategy for optimizing descriptor sets across diverse descriptor types and application datasets.

Understanding cesium (Cs) diffusion within high energy grain boundaries (HEGBs) of silicon carbide (SiC) is hindered by the absence of computationally efficient interatomic potentials suitable for long-duration simulations.  Developing machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) for these complex HEGB structures is difficult due to their size exceeding the capabilities of traditional ab initio methods.  To address this challenge, a novel workflow utilizing moment tensor potentials (MTPs) was established. This approach enables the extraction of unknown regions within HEGBs, facilitating efficient active training of the MLIPs.  Employing the developed MTPs, simulations of Cs diffusion in both amorphous SiC and HEGBs were conducted.  The results demonstrate that Cs diffusion occurs through a cage-breaking mechanism.  Further analysis, including radial distribution functions, Voronoi analysis, and electronic structure calculations, provides insights into the local atomic environments and weak interactions that influence Cs mobility. This work presents a generalizable methodology for training MLIPs on complex structures and offers valuable atomic-level understanding for modeling the behavior of fission products.

The ionospheric Spread-F (SF) phenomenon is crucial for understanding ionospheric electrodynamics and improving radio-based technologies. However, current SF classification methods rely on manual interpretation of ionograms by experts, which is time-consuming and prone to subjectivity.  This study addresses the lack of publicly available, large-scale, and comprehensively classified ionogram datasets.  We present the first open-access ionogram dataset comprising 150,000 ionograms, with 30,000 examples per class, including FSF, RSF, MSF, SSF, and a non-SF group. This dataset spans 14 years (2002-2016), encompassing a wide range of solar and geomagnetic conditions.  To demonstrate the dataset's utility, we developed a classification model, SA-ResNet50, trained on this data. This model can be applied to further ionogram data, facilitating automated and efficient SF classification.

Extra-nodal natural killer/T-cell lymphoma (ENKTL) survival prediction models currently exhibit limited accuracy. This study aimed to develop and validate a novel machine learning (ML) algorithm for improved survival prediction in ENKTL patients. A dataset comprising clinical and pathological covariates from 977 ENKTL patients across four cohorts was utilized.  Performance of the algorithm was assessed using established metrics including Harrell's c-index, receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves, calibration curves, and decision curve analysis (DCA).  Among 16 tested ML algorithms, the Gradient Boosting Machine (GBM) demonstrated superior performance, leading to the development of the ENKTL-ML score.  The ENKTL-ML score achieved c-indexes of 0.82 and 0.84 in the evaluation and external validation cohorts, respectively, indicating robust predictive capability.  Furthermore, the model effectively categorized patients into three risk groups with distinct survival outcomes.  The ENKTL-ML score outperformed existing prognostic models such as IPI, KPI, PINK-E, and NRI (all p < 0.001), highlighting its clinical utility.  An online calculator for the ENKTL-ML score is accessible to facilitate its implementation in clinical practice. This study demonstrates the potential of the ENKTL-ML score to enhance survival prediction accuracy in ENKTL patients, aiding clinicians in personalized treatment decisions.

The transition towards sustainable transportation has led to a surge in electric vehicle adoption, particularly electric cargo vehicles, where accurately predicting remaining driving range is crucial. This study proposes a machine learning-based approach to estimate the remaining driving range of electric cargo vehicles. Real-world operational data was collected from a Musoshi Pop-Up Mini electric cargo vehicle operating on a 2 km campus route under varying load and speed conditions. This high-resolution dataset allowed for detailed modeling of energy consumption patterns.  

The data underwent preprocessing, including feature engineering and segment-wise aggregation, before being used to train seven regression algorithms: ElasticNet, Support Vector Regression, Random Forest, LightGBM, XGBoost, CatBoost, and ExtraTrees.  A Bayesian hyperparameter optimization technique, Optuna, was employed to refine the performance of each model.  The models were rigorously evaluated based on root mean squared error (RMSE), mean absolute error (MAE), and R-squared (R²) metrics.

The Support Vector Regression (SVR) model demonstrated the highest accuracy, achieving an RMSE of 2.37, an MAE of 1.75, and an R² of 0.892, outperforming other ensemble and gradient boosting models. These findings highlight the effectiveness of data-driven models in accurately assessing energy consumption and predicting the remaining driving range of electric cargo vehicles. This contributes to the safe and reliable implementation of electric mobility systems.

The increasing dominance of e-commerce necessitates accurate prediction of consumer buying intentions for effective digital marketing. This study investigates the combined power of machine learning (ML) and structural equation modeling (SEM) to forecast consumer purchase decisions. Leveraging a dataset comprising consumer reviews and behavioral data from prominent e-commerce platforms, sentiment analysis, TF-IDF text vectorization, and various predictive models, including Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, and Neural Networks, are employed.
