These findings contribute to the development of targeted therapies and broad-spectrum antivirals for mitigating severe respiratory viral diseases.

This study investigates the challenges of accurately estimating 3D human pose and energy expenditure, particularly in complex activities and across diverse datasets.  The objective is to develop a unified framework that improves both tasks simultaneously while enhancing generalization capabilities.  To achieve this, Pose2Met, an end-to-end system, is proposed. At its core lies STAPFormer, a Transformer model designed with a SpatioTemporal Aggregated Pose (STAP) representation to effectively capture motion patterns.  Pose2Met leverages this representation to implement a novel pose-metabolism learning strategy, jointly optimizing both pose dynamics and metabolic patterns within a single training process. This unified approach enables direct prediction of both 3D pose and energy expenditure from 2D pose inputs, achieving performance comparable to traditional multi-stage pipelines while offering improved computational efficiency and robustness.  

Experiments demonstrate that STAPFormer achieves a Mean Per-Joint Position Error (MPJPE) of 38.2 mm on the Human3.6M dataset, surpassing existing methods like MixSTE and STCFormer.  For energy expenditure estimation on the Vid2Burn-ADL dataset, STAPFormer achieves a Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 22.1 kcal using 2D pose input, rivaling video-based methods.  Importantly, the unified learning framework significantly improves the accuracy of energy expenditure prediction using 2D pose input, approaching the performance of 3D pose-based methods.  These findings underscore the significance of robust motion representations for both 3D pose estimation and energy expenditure prediction.  Pose2Met holds promise for applications in intelligent fitness and healthcare, paving the way for more accurate and efficient joint estimation of pose and energy expenditure.

Sentiment analysis of short, noisy social media messages presents unique challenges due to the prevalence of slang, emojis, and misspellings, which can hinder predictive accuracy and obscure the reasoning behind model outputs. This study proposes a novel hybrid model designed to address these challenges. The model integrates a convolutional block, a bidirectional long short-term memory network (BiLSTM), and a single multi-head self-attention layer, augmented by a noise-invariant contrastive head (NICH) that normalizes noisy tokens before encoding.  The model's training involves two phases: initial training with frozen word embeddings followed by fine-tuning.  Performance is evaluated on two benchmark datasets: Sentiment140, comprising 1.6 million tweets, and IMDb, containing 50,000 movie reviews.  

Results demonstrate that the proposed model outperforms traditional machine learning methods such as logistic regression, support vector machines, and random forests, as well as an Attention-BiLSTM baseline.  Furthermore, it achieves comparable performance to a compact Transformer-based model (DistilBERT). Notably, the model achieves 90.63% accuracy and 0.9079 F1-score on Sentiment140 and 91.90% accuracy and 0.9203 F1-score on IMDb.  Analysis of attention scores and a perturbation-based metric reveals that the model consistently focuses on sentiment-bearing tokens, providing interpretable token-level explanations that are valuable for real-time monitoring of customer feedback and brand perception in noisy social media environments.

The high failure rate of fintech startups necessitates robust predictive models to inform investment strategies and policy decisions. This research proposes a novel deep learning approach utilizing 1D Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to classify fintech startup outcomes into four distinct categories: Initial Public Offering (IPO), Merger & Acquisition (M&A), Closure, and Unicorn status.  The model leverages global Crunchbase data to analyze structured attributes such as team composition, funding patterns, investor demographics, and company age, extracting both local and hierarchical patterns. To mitigate the challenge of class imbalance, the ADASYN oversampling technique is employed.  Performance evaluation reveals that while XGBoost, a traditional tree-based model, achieves higher accuracy and macro F1-score (92.9% and 91.9% respectively), the CNN model demonstrates competitive performance (90.8% accuracy and 89.6% macro F1-score), particularly in identifying rare outcomes like IPOs and Unicorns.  These findings confirm the effectiveness of tree-based models for structured data analysis, while also highlighting the potential of CNNs as a viable deep learning alternative for multiclass startup outcome prediction.

Smart agricultural Internet of Things (IoT) systems encounter difficulties in allocating resources effectively due to the diverse nature of devices and fluctuating environmental conditions. This study presents a novel framework that integrates 6G edge computing with reinforcement learning to optimize resource management within these systems. The framework leverages the characteristics of 6G, namely its low latency and high bandwidth, to facilitate real-time communication and efficient task offloading.  Reinforcement learning algorithms are employed to dynamically adjust the resource allocation strategy in response to evolving environmental conditions and task demands. A reward function is meticulously designed to strike a balance between minimizing latency, maximizing resource utilization, and enhancing energy efficiency.  Evaluation results indicate that the proposed framework achieves a 35% reduction in latency and a 25% improvement in resource utilization compared to conventional methods.  Furthermore, the framework demonstrates scalability under scenarios with increased device density and task complexity. This research offers a scalable and adaptable solution for smart agriculture, with potential applications in domains such as precision irrigation, autonomous farming, and drone-based surveillance.

The increasing availability of big data and advancements in artificial intelligence have led to the growing application of large language models (LLMs) in diverse fields, including healthcare.  This study investigates the potential of LLMs in analyzing both structured and unstructured medical data for tasks such as disease prediction and risk assessment. To evaluate this potential, a novel framework, the HLLM-Potential Framework, was developed. This framework employs publicly available cardiovascular datasets encompassing both structured and unstructured data to compare the performance of LLMs with traditional machine learning and deep learning models.  

The study trained and configured existing LLMs on medical data for disease prediction and health-risk assessment tasks. Results demonstrate that LLMs can effectively process both structured and unstructured medical data to achieve high accuracy and recall rates in cardiovascular disease prediction, exceeding 80% in structured data tasks.  Furthermore, in the analysis of unstructured ECG images, a multimodal LLM achieved an overall accuracy rate of 85%. 

The findings indicate that LLMs exhibit superior interactivity, generalization capabilities, and reduced reliance on data quality and feature engineering compared to traditional models.  These advantages, coupled with their high efficiency and flexibility, suggest that LLMs hold significant promise for a wide range of applications in the healthcare domain.

Sparse signal recovery is a fundamental problem with applications in diverse fields.  While significant progress has been made in general sparse recovery techniques, and there is a growing body of work on non-negative sparse recovery, a unified theoretical and algorithmic framework encompassing both scenarios has been lacking. This study introduces a novel feasible sequential quadratic programming (SQP) framework that addresses this gap. This framework treats both general and non-negative sparse recovery as special cases of a single constrained optimization model, differentiated solely by the geometry of the trust region used to ensure feasibility. The framework leverages the Smoothed-ℓ0 (SL0) approximation, a smooth, non-convex surrogate for the combinatorial ℓ0 norm, enabling the derivation of accurate second-order search directions and achieving superlinear convergence, surpassing the performance of prevalent first-order methods. For non-negative sparse recovery, the SQP subproblem incorporates interior-point ideas, utilizing an ellipsoidal trust region to maintain iterates strictly within the non-negative orthant. This approach avoids the convergence disruptions and numerical instability often associated with post hoc projections or hard thresholding.  The non-negative method is initialized with a simple, robust all-ones vector, mitigating potential instabilities linked to pseudo-inverse-based initializations.  Global convergence of the unified framework is established under the standard Mangasarian–Fromovitz Constraint Qualification (MFCQ).  Extensive numerical experiments on both synthetic data and real-world tasks, including face recognition, demonstrate that the proposed approach significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art algorithms in terms of recovery accuracy, phase transition behavior, and robustness to noise.

Heterogeneous treatment effects (HTE) estimation in observational studies with confounding variables is a challenging task. Bayesian causal forests (BCF) offer a promising approach, but their accuracy relies heavily on accurate propensity score (PS) estimation.  Inaccurate PS can introduce bias into treatment effect estimates.  This study proposes BCF with covariate balancing propensity scores (BCF-CBPS) to mitigate this issue. BCF-CBPS integrates covariate balancing directly into the PS estimation process, aiming to improve the balance of covariates between treatment and control groups.  This enhanced balance allows BCF to produce more precise estimates of the true treatment effect heterogeneity.  

Through extensive Monte Carlo simulations and real-world applications, BCF-CBPS demonstrated superior precision and robustness compared to standard BCF and other machine learning methods for HTE estimation.  The findings underscore the effectiveness of incorporating covariate balancing within the BCF framework for reliable treatment effect estimation in complex observational studies.

This research proposes a machine learning framework designed to analyze and group modern and contemporary Korean paintings using image data.  A pre-trained vision-language model, coupled with a multi-layered analysis process, was employed to effectively extract detailed visual features from the paintings. These features encompass color information from various color spaces and quantified texture characteristics. The extracted feature vectors were then clustered, and the performance of the clustering was evaluated using a majority-label assignment strategy.  The resulting accuracy rate of 82.4% surpassed the performance of models relying on single feature types, such as RGB, HSV, histograms, Local Binary Patterns (LBP), and Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrices (GLCM). Notably, the proposed method demonstrated superior per-artist precision, enhanced discrimination of borderline cases, and greater robustness when dealing with categories represented by limited samples. Representative images from the artist clusters effectively captured the unique color and texture profiles of each artist.  Further analysis was conducted using automatic image captioning and zero-shot style assignment techniques, leveraging image and text embeddings for matching. The findings highlight the effectiveness of machine learning-based image analysis as a powerful and objective approach for identifying and differentiating the visual characteristics of modern and contemporary Korean paintings, offering a quantitative lens for art historical interpretation.

Accurate landslide risk identification is crucial for effective disaster response and sustainable land management. Traditional landslide detection methods often struggle due to their reliance on manual interpretation, subjectivity, and limitations in handling the vast and complex datasets involved. This study presents a novel, explainable machine learning approach to address these challenges. The proposed method utilizes a multistage fusion architecture to enhance the identification of diverse landslide types by incorporating knowledge of key landslide-inducing factors. Initially, a feature selection process based on information gain is employed to identify the most influential features. Subsequently, these selected features are used to train a range of linear and ensemble classification models. The ensemble model, resulting from this multi-stage process, demonstrates superior performance in landslide prediction tasks compared to other machine learning models. Furthermore, the study incorporates visualization techniques to provide transparent and interpretable explanations for the model's predictions, enhancing its usability and trust.

Sentiment analysis (SA) is a crucial tool for understanding public opinion, particularly in the context of vast textual datasets generated on social media platforms.  Despite the abundance of data available, acquiring high-quality, representative datasets for SA research is hindered by platform-specific data access policies, privacy concerns, and usage limitations. This study addresses these challenges by introducing TSAPM-BERT, a novel framework built upon the BERT architecture.  TSAPM-BERT incorporates a weighted attention mechanism to enhance the model's focus on relevant textual information, integrates sentiment lexicon knowledge, and optimizes the learning rate for improved aspect-level sentiment classification.  The proposed framework was evaluated on a standard emotion dataset, where its performance was compared against established machine learning and deep learning methods.  Experimental results demonstrate that TSAPM-BERT achieves a high level of accuracy, surpassing existing approaches with a training accuracy of 99.37% and a testing accuracy of 98.52%. This work offers a robust and reproducible framework that effectively addresses the challenges of accurate sentiment classification in the context of real-world social media data usage.

Surface defects in waterborne painted wood products can significantly impact product quality and necessitate effective detection methods. To address this challenge, a novel image dataset was created specifically for surface defect detection in this domain. The dataset comprises over 13,000 high-resolution images capturing four common defect types: scratches, cracks, bubbles, and holes.  A balanced distribution of images ensures representation of each defect class, with over 3,000 images per category. Images were acquired in a real-world production setting using industrial cameras, providing a realistic representation of practical scenarios.  Expert annotators meticulously labeled each image to guarantee high accuracy and reliability. This comprehensive dataset serves as a valuable resource for researchers developing and evaluating deep learning models for automated defect detection in industrial environments. By leveraging this dataset, machine learning algorithms can be trained to identify defects with high precision, enabling timely remediation and ultimately enhancing the quality and consistency of finished wood products.

Concept drift, a phenomenon where the underlying data distribution changes over time, poses a significant challenge to the performance of machine learning models in operational settings.  To address this, effective drift detection and adaptation mechanisms are crucial for maintaining the reliability of predictions.  Recent research has explored the use of deep learning, particularly autoencoders, for drift detection. Autoencoders learn the data distribution and measure deviations in incoming data through reconstruction loss.  While standard autoencoders are widely used, variations such as denoising and variational autoencoders offer potential for improved performance.  

This study proposes a framework to optimize autoencoder-based drift detection.  A comparative analysis of different autoencoder types, including standard, denoising, and variational autoencoders, is conducted both theoretically and empirically.  Furthermore, the framework outlines a methodology for selecting optimal hyperparameters using grid search and drift detection-specific parameters using both grid search and Bayesian optimization.  

The proposed framework is applied to the AEDDM (autoencoder-based drift detection method), resulting in Opt-AEDDM, an optimized version.  Experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the framework in identifying the best-performing autoencoder and optimizing its parameters, leading to enhanced drift detection accuracy.

The utilization of social media data for diverse applications, including urban and regional studies and public health surveillance, often hinges on accurate location information. However, a significant challenge arises from the scarcity of geo-annotations in Twitter data, with only a minuscule fraction of tweets explicitly providing location details. This study proposes a novel classification framework designed to infer tweet locations with high accuracy, leveraging solely the textual and contextual cues embedded within the tweets themselves. This approach boasts global coverage and dynamically adjusts its spatial resolution based on the volume of tweets originating from a particular area, enabling country-level predictions in less active regions and state-level predictions in densely populated zones.  A comprehensive evaluation of the framework's performance across diverse geographical regions is conducted, exploring the influence of various factors on its accuracy.  Furthermore, the analysis reveals that removing a small percentage of tweets exhibiting limited location-related information substantially enhances the overall model performance.

The scarcity and high cost of data pose a significant challenge in data-driven machine learning, particularly in domains like predictive maintenance where fault occurrences are infrequent.  Synthetic data generation emerges as a valuable solution in such scenarios. While synthetic data generation has been extensively explored in areas like computer vision and natural language processing, its application in time series analysis remains relatively underexplored. This study investigates the potential of diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs), a recent advancement in image processing, for generating synthetic time series data. The proposed D3A-TS methodology leverages DPMs to augment time series data for both classification and regression tasks.  Furthermore, the study explores the use of meta-attributes to guide the data augmentation process.  

Experiments conducted on six diverse datasets, encompassing various input sizes and output types, demonstrate the effectiveness of the D3A-TS methodology in generating synthetic data for training machine learning models.  An in-depth ablation study further validates the findings and provides insights into the impact of different methodological components. The results highlight the promising potential of DPMs for enhancing the performance of machine learning models in time series analysis by addressing the limitations posed by data scarcity.

The integration of artificial intelligence into the creative process of film and television scriptwriting is an emerging field with significant potential. This study explores the application of machine learning and natural language processing techniques to address the challenges and opportunities associated with script generation, plot construction, and character development. A deep neural network-based script generation model was developed and trained on a substantial dataset of film and television scripts. This training process aimed to enhance the model's capabilities in script structure, language expression, and emotional conveyance.  The resulting model demonstrated proficiency in generating short scripts that exhibit fluency and accuracy in language and structure. However, limitations were observed in the model's ability to generate longer scripts, handle intricate plotlines, and effectively express nuanced emotional complexities.  Future research endeavors will concentrate on addressing these shortcomings by improving the model's capacity for complex plot processing and emotional expression, as well as optimizing its computational efficiency and generation quality. This research contributes valuable empirical evidence to the application of artificial intelligence in creative industries and paves the way for innovative technological advancements in script creation.

Accurate and comprehensive soil moisture data is crucial for understanding Earth's hydrological and energy cycles, yet existing datasets often lack depth resolution and long-term coverage. This study presents a novel global soil moisture dataset (SWSM) spanning 2002 to 2021, generated using a machine learning algorithm (XGBoost). The SWSM dataset offers daily estimates of soil moisture at a 0.05° spatial resolution for three distinct depth horizons: 0–10 cm, 10–30 cm, and 30–60 cm.  Validation against ground-based observations revealed high accuracy, with Pearson correlation coefficients consistently above 0.90 and root mean square errors below 0.05 across all depths.  Furthermore, an analysis of feature importance within the model confirmed the dataset's physical consistency, exhibiting depth-specific patterns consistent with established hydrological principles. The SWSM dataset, characterized by its extensive temporal coverage, fine spatial resolution, and multi-layer structure, provides a valuable resource for advancing research in hydrology, agriculture, and climate change studies.

Global assessments of total water storage anomalies (TWSA) are crucial for understanding long-term climate and hydrological processes. While satellite missions like GRACE and GRACE Follow-On have provided valuable TWSA observations, their relatively short record limits the scope of such assessments. To address this, we introduce ML-TWiX, a novel dataset reconstructing monthly TWSA anomalies from 1980 to 2012 at a 0.5° × 0.5° global resolution.  ML-TWiX leverages the power of machine learning by training an ensemble of three distinct models – Random Forest, XGBoost, and Gaussian Process Regression – on global hydrological and land surface model simulations. Each model independently reconstructs TWSA, and their outputs are combined through ensemble averaging to generate a unified product with spatially explicit uncertainty estimates. The accuracy of ML-TWiX is rigorously validated against independent datasets, including satellite laser ranging measurements, storage derived from water mass balance closure, and global mean sea level budget estimates. This continuous reconstruction of global TWSA facilitated by ML-TWiX opens new avenues for research in hydrology, climate science, and water resource management.

The widening disparity between educational curricula and industry skill requirements poses a significant challenge in today's dynamic technological landscape. This research proposes a machine learning framework designed to dynamically bridge this gap by matching industry skill demands with educational offerings.  The framework leverages data from various sources, including job postings, industry reports, and labor market analyses, to identify current and emerging skill trends across diverse sectors. A novel Dynamic Termite Life Cycle Optimizer-driven Euclidean-Support Vector Machine (DTLC-Euclidean SVM) model is employed to predict future skill requirements.  By analyzing the alignment between graduate skillsets and employer expectations, the system pinpoints skill gaps and recommends tailored course adjustments.  A dynamic matching algorithm, incorporating similarity metrics and k-Means Clustering, connects industry needs with existing course content, while an automated course generation module proposes new or updated courses to address identified shortages.  Evaluation of the framework demonstrates high accuracy in skill prediction and course recommendation, achieving a performance accuracy of 96.8%, precision of 97.6%, recall of 96.2%, and an f1-score of 97.3%. This data-driven approach enables continuous curriculum adaptation, fostering a stronger alignment between academia and industry. Ultimately, the framework empowers educators and policymakers to develop responsive educational programs that equip students with the skills necessary to thrive in the evolving job market.

Effective Physical Education (PE) teaching is crucial for student development, engagement, and long-term health. Traditional assessment methods often lack objectivity and fail to capture the multifaceted nature of PE instruction. This study proposes a novel framework for evaluating PE teaching effectiveness using Multimodal Machine Learning (MML).  The framework analyzes a comprehensive dataset encompassing instructional delivery (video and audio), student movement patterns (wearable sensors and motion capture), and student perceptions.  To ensure data quality, noise reduction techniques and z-score normalization are applied to sensor signals and across modalities, respectively.  The Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG) is employed for feature extraction from video data, capturing fine-grained movement and posture characteristics.  The core of the assessment framework is an Enhanced Marine Predators-driven Explainable Boosting Machine (EMP-EBM) model. This hybrid system combines the global optimization capabilities of the Enhanced Marine Predators algorithm with the transparency and interpretability of the Explainable Boosting Machine.  The EMP-EBM model achieved high accuracy (98.07%), precision (97.85%), recall (98.31%), and F1-score (98.07%) in identifying effective PE teaching sessions. This demonstrates the model's ability to leverage multimodal inputs for comprehensive assessment. By balancing precision and explainability, the EMP-EBM model advances the application of AI in education, providing a more objective, adaptive, and holistic approach to evaluating PE teaching effectiveness.

Continuous, non-contact monitoring of vital signs for multiple patients presents a substantial challenge in healthcare settings. This study introduces a novel radar-based dataset specifically designed to evaluate methods for simultaneous multi-patient vital sign monitoring.  Collaborating with Ostrava University Training Hospital, the dataset was created using advanced medical simulation mannequins, simulating a diverse range of physiological conditions, from normal resting states to critical emergencies such as apnea, cardiac arrest, and severe respiratory distress.  The dataset encompasses a wide variety of sensor configurations, including different positions (top, frontal, lateral) and distances (1-4 meters) from the mannequins, as well as variations in radar parameters such as ADC samples, chirp loops, ramp times, frame rates, and gains. This resulted in over 100 distinct recording sessions.  Initial processing using beamforming techniques yielded mean heart rate and breathing rate errors that approach clinical thresholds (approximately 6.6 bpm for heart rate and 1.47 bpm for respiratory rate). These findings demonstrate the dataset's potential for developing advanced signal processing and machine learning algorithms for non-invasive multi-patient monitoring systems.  Adhering to the FAIR principles, the dataset is fully documented and publicly available, promoting transparency and reproducibility in research endeavors aimed at advancing this technology.

Intraoperative acquired pressure injury (IAPI) poses a significant risk for elderly cardiac surgery patients due to their compromised skin integrity and circulation. Accurate risk prediction is crucial for effective prevention and individualized patient care. This study developed a machine learning framework for IAPI risk prediction in elderly cardiac surgery patients using data from the MIMIC-IV database and an external dataset from Nanjing First Hospital.  

A stacking ensemble model was constructed using 24 clinically relevant features and seven different classifiers. The model's performance was rigorously evaluated using stratified fivefold cross-validation with multiple imputations to address missing data and bootstrapped confidence intervals for uncertainty quantification. External validation was performed on a separate dataset, employing resampling techniques to mitigate class imbalance.  

The ensemble model demonstrated strong predictive accuracy in both internal and external validation sets, with an area under the curve (AUC) of 0.857 and 0.783, respectively. Calibration analysis confirmed the model's reliability in aligning predicted risks with observed outcomes.  Furthermore, SHAP analysis revealed key predictors such as atrial fibrillation, lymphocyte count, and operative time, which are clinically meaningful and robustly identified by the model. 

This study presents a robust and interpretable machine learning framework for IAPI risk prediction in elderly cardiac surgery patients. The incorporation of multiple imputation, bootstrapped calibration, and SHAP analysis enhances methodological transparency and provides a clinically actionable tool for perioperative risk stratification and prevention strategies.

Diabetes is a major global health concern, and accurate prediction of its onset is crucial for effective management and prevention of complications. Regression-based machine learning has emerged as a powerful tool for diabetes prediction, leveraging its ability to model complex relationships between risk factors and disease progression.  This study examines the current landscape of regression-based machine learning techniques for diabetes prediction.  The research investigates the performance and evolution of these algorithms, analyzing how advancements in feature selection, regularization, and ensemble methods have contributed to improved prediction accuracy.  A key objective is to establish a standardized framework for evaluating and benchmarking diabetes prediction models, facilitating fair and reproducible comparisons across different approaches.  The study also evaluates the effectiveness of leading regression algorithms in this domain, considering the balance between interpretability and predictive power for real-world healthcare applications.  Finally, the research identifies future research directions, emphasizing the need for more robust, explainable, and patient-centered prediction models that can be seamlessly integrated into clinical practice.

Early diagnosis of Alzheimer's Disease (AD) is crucial for effective intervention. This study presents a novel hybrid framework for AD detection using magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans. The framework combines the efficiency of SqueezeNet, a lightweight convolutional neural network, with ensemble stacking of multiple machine learning models.  SqueezeNet's unique architecture, featuring a fire module, allows for efficient feature extraction while minimizing computational demands, making it suitable for resource-constrained clinical settings.  

The ensemble stacking method integrates predictions from various machine learning algorithms, including Multi-Layer Perceptrons of different sizes, XGBoost, CatBoost, and AdaBoost. This ensemble approach aims to enhance the robustness and accuracy of AD classification.  The proposed framework was evaluated on a comprehensive dataset of 3,382 MRI scans from the Kaggle platform, encompassing four AD severity classes.  

The results demonstrate that the hybrid framework achieves state-of-the-art performance, surpassing the performance of individual models and effectively addressing class imbalance.  The model achieved an AUC of 94.9% and an accuracy of 89%, outperforming existing methods such as XGBoost, which achieved 83.2% accuracy.  This study highlights the potential of the proposed framework as a scalable, reliable, and cost-effective tool for real-time AD detection and classification in clinical practice.

The high dimensionality of feature spaces derived from malware opcodes presents a considerable obstacle to effective machine learning classification. This study investigates the performance of four dimensionality reduction techniques: Principal Component Analysis (PCA), Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection (UMAP), and autoencoder-based reduction, applied to opcode-frequency representations of malware.  A dataset comprising 82,569 malware samples and 1796 opcodes was utilized to evaluate the impact of each reduction method across various target dimensions and two classifier architectures: Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) and a three-layer Multilayer Perceptron (MLP).  The results demonstrate that LDA effectively separates malware classes at lower dimensions, while PCA excels at higher dimensions where variance preservation is crucial. Autoencoder-based reduction consistently achieves high accuracy with compact representations. In contrast, UMAP shows limited benefits for tabular opcode data. These findings reveal the trade-offs inherent in linear versus non-linear dimensionality reduction strategies and offer valuable insights for selecting appropriate feature compression methods in large-scale malware analysis.

Accurate determination of twin zygosity is crucial in twin research, but traditional genetic testing can be costly. This study explores the potential of machine learning to predict zygosity based on physical similarity features, offering a more accessible alternative.  A dataset of 5,077 Iranian twin pairs was collected and preprocessed to address class imbalance through the Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE). Eight machine learning algorithms, including K-nearest neighbors, support vector machine, logistic regression, random forest, decision tree, and boosting techniques, were trained and evaluated on the dataset.  Performance was assessed using various metrics, and hyperparameter optimization was conducted using GridSearchCV.  The XGBoost algorithm demonstrated the highest predictive accuracy, achieving 85.57% accuracy and an F1-score of 86.72%.  Further analysis using the SHAP algorithm revealed the relative importance of different physical traits in zygosity prediction. This research highlights the potential of XGBoost, combined with SMOTE oversampling, as a robust framework for predicting twin zygosity based on physical characteristics. Future work will focus on validating the model against genetic testing data to establish its reliability and clinical applicability.

Recognizing epileptic seizures from electroencephalogram (EEG) data is crucial for timely diagnosis and clinical guidance. However, traditional centralized machine learning approaches for this task raise privacy concerns due to the sensitive nature of patient data. This study investigates a privacy-preserving framework for epileptic seizure recognition using federated and explainable machine learning techniques.  

A publicly available EEG dataset, originally containing five seizure classes, was transformed into a binary classification problem distinguishing seizure and non-seizure events.  Data was partitioned at the patient level to prevent information leakage, with separate training and testing sets. A simulated federated learning environment with five clients was established, incorporating both independent and non-independent data distributions.  The Federated Averaging strategy was employed over fifty communication rounds.  

Several machine learning models, including Support Vector Machine, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, and K Nearest Neighbors, were evaluated using standardized hyperparameter configurations. Model performance was assessed using accuracy, area under the receiver operating characteristic curve, confusion matrices, and learning curves.  Explainability was achieved through SHAP and LIME techniques to provide insights into the decision-making process of the models. 

The results demonstrate that federated learning can achieve competitive performance in epileptic seizure recognition while effectively preserving patient data privacy.  Furthermore, the integration of explainable machine learning methods offers valuable transparency into the classification decisions made by the models. This study serves as a proof-of-concept evaluation using an EEG dataset, highlighting the methodological feasibility of this approach for future clinical applications.

The increasing digitization of information has led to a surge in the use of tables as a primary format for structured data representation across diverse fields.  Table recognition technology, which automates the conversion of table content from images to machine-readable formats, is crucial for enhancing data processing efficiency and accuracy. While convolutional neural networks and Transformer-based approaches have made significant strides in this area, challenges remain in handling complex table layouts, diverse formats, and computational costs.  This study proposes SPPLTR, a novel table recognition model that integrates spatial pyramid pooling with a causal linear Transformer framework. SPPLTR leverages a spatial pyramid context fusion module and a causal linear Transformer to effectively capture both structural and textual information within table images.  Furthermore, the model incorporates a multi-head causal linear attention mechanism with automatic mixed precision support, enabling efficient and stable inference.  Evaluations on the PubTabNet dataset demonstrate that SPPLTR achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming most existing methods across various evaluation metrics. This research presents a promising solution for table recognition tasks and paves the way for advancements in structured document understanding.

The reliability of wind turbine systems in cold climates hinges on accurate icing detection, as undetected icing can lead to structural damage. This study presents an intelligent framework for icing detection that combines structural vibration data with environmental information. The framework utilizes Operational Modal Analysis for automated feature extraction and incorporates temperature compensation to account for environmental influences. Five distinct classification models—Optimized Threshold, Random Forest, XGBoost, Support Vector Machine, and Gated Recurrent Unit—were rigorously evaluated using time-series cross-validation on comprehensive experimental data obtained from a large-scale climate chamber.  Given the critical nature of the application, the framework prioritizes recall to minimize false negative errors and employs non-parametric statistical validation to assess model stability.  The results demonstrate a "simplicity paradox" where the physics-informed Optimized Threshold model achieved the highest operational reliability, surpassing more complex ensemble and deep learning methods in both safety and statistical stability. This research highlights the effectiveness of meticulous feature-level fusion and domain-specific engineering in enhancing system resilience. The proposed methodology offers a scalable foundation for multi-sensor fusion and adaptive predictive maintenance strategies in renewable energy infrastructure.

The proliferation of machine-generated text, particularly deepfakes, poses a significant threat to the integrity of social media platforms.  This research investigates the development of a robust framework for identifying machine-generated textual content, specifically focusing on short-form posts like tweets.  The framework leverages the DeBERTaV3 model, trained on the TweepFake and PHEME datasets, to analyze the linguistic characteristics of tweets and distinguish between human-authored and bot-generated content.  

To assess the effectiveness of this approach, the proposed framework is compared against several established deep learning models, including BERT, RoBERTa, SVM-RBF, random forest, SVM, CNN, and LSTM.  Results demonstrate that the DeBERTaV3-based framework achieves a high accuracy rate of 97.12% in detecting fake news, outperforming the other models.  This superior performance is attributed to the DeBERTa architecture's design, coupled with data preprocessing techniques and embedding methods that effectively capture the nuances of tweet language.  Furthermore, the study evaluates the scalability and computational efficiency of the proposed framework in comparison to alternative models, highlighting its potential for real-world deployment.

This study investigates the performance of artificial intelligence (AI) credit-scoring models in comparison to traditional statistical models. Using a real-world dataset of 1000 loan applications, the research aims to determine if AI methods offer superior predictive accuracy, interpretability, and stability during economic downturns. Four models were developed: logistic regression and decision trees representing traditional approaches, and XGBoost and a multilayer perceptron neural network as AI-based alternatives. Model training and evaluation were conducted in R, employing a 70:30 train-test split, inner cross-validation, and a spatial search algorithm for hyperparameter optimization.  

The results demonstrate that the neural network achieved the highest balanced accuracy, followed by XGBoost, while logistic regression exhibited the lowest performance.  SHAP analysis revealed that credit amount, loan duration, and age were key predictors across all models.  Furthermore, under simulated economic stress, XGBoost demonstrated greater prediction stability compared to logistic regression, which experienced a significant decline in accuracy.  These findings suggest that advanced AI tools possess enhanced predictive capabilities and offer robust interpretability, positioning them as promising alternatives to conventional credit-scoring systems used in financial institutions.

Accurate assignment of imaging protocols is crucial for efficient clinical radiology workflows. This study investigates the performance and interpretability of GPT-4 in classifying head MRI protocols based on physician-entered clinical indications.  The study compared GPT-4 with a fine-tuned BERT model and a fine-tuned LLaMA-3 model, evaluating their accuracy against expert-validated ground truth labels.  While GPT-4 achieved an F1 score of 0.83, outperformed by BERT (0.91) and LLaMA-3 (0.93), it consistently generated the most human-like and interpretable explanations.  A novel approach, LLaMA-3 assisted prompting, was also explored, where GPT-4 received the LLaMA-3 model's prediction as context for potential revision and explanation. This method significantly improved GPT-4's accuracy. The findings suggest that GPT-4, despite its lower initial accuracy, offers valuable insights into clinical reasoning through its interpretable outputs.  Furthermore, combining GPT-4 with specialized models like LLaMA-3 demonstrates the potential for enhanced performance and transparency in clinical decision support systems.

Clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC) is a highly aggressive cancer often linked to specific genetic mutations. While these mutations are well-studied, the impact of structural variations (SVs) and the three-dimensional organization of the genome in ccRCC development remains unclear. This study presents a comprehensive molecular analysis of ccRCC, utilizing multi-omics approaches to identify novel oncogenic targets associated with SVs and uncover how the three-dimensional genome structure changes during tumor progression.  

The research demonstrates a complex relationship between SVs and chromatin architecture, revealing how structural rearrangements contribute to oncogenic dysregulation through alterations in three-dimensional genome organization. A previously unrecognized mechanism, enhancer hijacking, was discovered and experimentally confirmed, leading to the persistent activation of the SEMA5B proto-oncogene.  

Building on these findings, a machine learning-based prognostic model was developed, utilizing signatures of enhancer hijacking events. This work provides a valuable resource for ccRCC research by elucidating the combined role of SVs and three-dimensional genome reorganization in driving cancer development and translates these insights into a clinically applicable tool for predicting patient outcomes.

Anemia, affecting over a billion individuals worldwide, poses a significant global health challenge due to its diverse causes, including iron or vitamin deficiencies, blood loss, and genetic disorders.  Accurate diagnosis is crucial for effective treatment, but traditional methods like blood tests are often inaccessible in resource-limited settings.  This study investigates the potential of artificial intelligence (AI) to revolutionize anemia diagnosis.  We systematically analyze various AI techniques, encompassing machine learning, deep learning, optimization algorithms, and hybrid approaches, for the detection of three prevalent anemia types: iron deficiency, sickle cell disease, and thalassemia.  The analysis focuses on evaluating the performance of these techniques in terms of accuracy, key diagnostic features, and limitations.  Furthermore, we examine the relationship between dataset size and model accuracy, exploring the impact of data availability on diagnostic performance.  Our findings highlight the promise of AI in providing a non-invasive, rapid, and cost-effective solution for anemia diagnosis, particularly in underserved areas.  While traditional invasive methods remain the gold standard, AI integration can significantly enhance diagnostic efficiency and support healthcare professionals in confirming diagnoses.

The Iraqi building sector plays a vital role in the nation's economic development, despite facing various challenges. This study investigates the application of machine learning for predicting the productivity of housing projects in Iraq, leveraging project design and implementation characteristics.  Three machine learning algorithms, artificial neural networks (ANN), extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost), and support vector regression (SVR), were employed to develop predictive models.  A dataset comprising 67 housing projects, featuring seven input variables and one output variable representing implementation productivity, was utilized for model training and evaluation.  The XGBoost model demonstrated the highest predictive accuracy, achieving a determination coefficient (R2) of 0.998 and a mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of 0.8%.  The ANN model also exhibited strong performance with an R2 of 0.92 and a MAPE of 4.1%.  In contrast, the SVR model yielded lower accuracy, with an R2 of 0.813 and a MAPE of 6.6%.  Feature importance analysis revealed that built-up area and the quantities of brick and concrete works were the most significant factors influencing productivity.  These findings suggest that machine learning, particularly XGBoost and ANN, offer valuable tools for supporting informed decision-making in the early stages of housing project planning by enabling accurate predictions of project execution performance based on project-related data.

The accelerating pace of materials discovery through machine learning and computational methods hinges on the availability of comprehensive input data and effective structure models. High-entropy materials, a promising class of materials with potential to surpass limitations of conventional materials, present a unique opportunity in this context.  This review explores the intersection of quantum computing and the design of high-entropy materials, particularly focusing on high-entropy MXenes.  A key challenge lies in encoding and decoding the complex crystal structures of these materials to facilitate the creation of robust databases.  The review further examines the application of deep learning algorithms in predicting material properties prior to physical synthesis, specifically targeting the identification of high-entropy materials suitable for use in batteries and supercapacitors. Finally, the review highlights the potential of integrating quantum computing and artificial intelligence to advance our understanding of high-entropy material structures and their applications in energy storage.

This study proposes an integrated AI-driven framework designed to enhance Management Information Systems (MIS) capabilities in the context of sustainable supply chain optimization and environmental impact analysis. The framework addresses three key tasks: predicting greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, detecting anomalies in emission patterns, and providing interpretable insights for data-driven decision making.  A variety of machine learning models are employed, including classical regression models such as Linear Regression, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost, and Support Vector Regression, as well as a deep learning-based Gated TabTransformer (GTT) for GHG emission prediction. Unsupervised anomaly detection is achieved through a Variational Graph Autoencoder (VGAE), which leverages both topological and feature-level deviations.  Explainability is incorporated via a hybrid model combining Explainable Boosting Machines (EBM) and SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP).  

The results demonstrate that classical regression models, particularly Gradient Boosting, achieve superior accuracy and robustness compared to existing methods. The GTT model exhibits strong generalization capabilities. The VGAE effectively identifies anomalies in emission patterns.  Furthermore, the EBM-SHAP combination enables transparent and interpretable local and global insights, facilitating data-driven decision making. This comprehensive framework bridges the domains of sustainability, artificial intelligence, and MIS, advancing the state-of-the-art in supply chain sustainability analytics.

Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) are increasingly deployed in both military and civilian applications, but their open and unsecured nature makes them vulnerable to various routing attacks. These attacks exploit the inherent vulnerabilities of sensor nodes and the unreliability of wireless communication, posing significant security threats.  This study proposes a novel Intrusion Detection System (IDS) tailored for cluster-based WSNs.  The system leverages a dual-level node authentication model, incorporating Node Behaviour Analysis (NBA), to enhance intrusion detection capabilities.  At the core of the model is a machine learning algorithm designed to accurately identify malicious activity within the network.  Evaluation results demonstrate that the proposed DLNA-NBA model achieves a 98.9% accuracy rate in dual-level node authentication and a 99.2% accuracy rate in intrusion detection.  These performance metrics surpass those of traditional intrusion detection methods.  The improved node verification, optimized feature utilization, and efficient behavioral pattern analysis contribute to secure communication and a reduction in false alarms within WSN environments.

Accurate prediction of sports-related injuries is crucial for enhancing athlete well-being and performance. This study investigated the efficacy of machine learning models in predicting injury risk among professional football players. Data from 300 male athletes aged 18 to 28 years, spanning two competitive seasons, was analyzed. Injuries were defined as musculoskeletal conditions resulting in at least one missed training session or match, confirmed by ICD-10 diagnoses. A comprehensive dataset encompassing daily training workload, recovery metrics, wellness indicators, heart-rate variability, cumulative playing time, and injury history was collected.  

The objective was to develop a predictive model capable of identifying athletes at a higher risk of injury.  Data preprocessing techniques, including normalization, one-hot encoding, and feature selection using LASSO regression and recursive feature elimination, were employed. Missing data was addressed through multiple imputation by chained equations, and class imbalance was mitigated using SMOTE and weighting.  

The study evaluated the performance of logistic regression, decision tree, and random forest models using 10-fold cross-validation.  Random forest models demonstrated superior performance compared to other algorithms, achieving high accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC.  Furthermore, explainable AI techniques, such as SHAP and LIME, were utilized to identify the key predictors of injury risk.  

The results revealed that prior injury history, training intensity, and recovery time were the most influential factors in predicting injury risk.  These findings highlight the potential of ensemble machine learning methods for providing robust, interpretable, and actionable insights into injury prevention.  The insights gained can inform data-driven strategies to optimize training regimens and mitigate the incidence of sports-related injuries.  Future research should focus on validating these findings across diverse sports and incorporating additional physiological and genetic factors to enhance predictive accuracy and generalizability.

The integration of artificial intelligence into precision medicine (AI-PM) holds significant promise for healthcare advancements. This study aimed to evaluate the cost-effectiveness of AI-PM by systematically reviewing existing economic evaluations.  A comprehensive search of five major databases yielded 48 economic evaluations, 31 of which focused on cost-utility analyses.  Risk-of-bias assessment, using the ECOBIAS checklist, revealed potential for systematic optimism in the included studies. Despite this, the majority (89%) of base-case analyses demonstrated that AI-PM was either cost-saving or cost-effective, with incremental cost-effectiveness ratios ranging from dominant to $129,174 per quality-adjusted life-year (QALY).  Further analysis revealed substantial heterogeneity in the estimated net monetary benefits (NMBs) of AI-PM, influenced by factors such as modeling choices and system-level considerations.  These findings suggest that while AI-PM offers potential for cost-effective healthcare improvements, uncertainties remain regarding its adaptability and the comprehensive consideration of key value factors.  This highlights the need for further research to address these limitations and inform informed decision-making regarding AI-PM adoption.

Urosepsis following upper urinary tract stone surgery poses a significant risk to patient outcomes. This study aimed to develop a reliable and interpretable machine learning model for predicting postoperative sepsis based on readily available laboratory parameters.  A dataset comprising 7,464 patients and 155 pre- and postoperative features was analyzed.  Feature selection was performed using LASSO regression and correlation analysis, identifying eight key variables for model development.  Eight different machine learning algorithms were evaluated using 10-fold cross-validation, with the Light Gradient Boosting Machine demonstrating the highest predictive accuracy.  The model achieved excellent performance metrics, including AUCs of 1.0, 0.90, and 0.88 for the training, validation, and test cohorts, respectively.  Furthermore, the model exhibited good calibration and clinical utility as confirmed by the learning curve and decision curve analyses.  The SHAP algorithm was employed to elucidate the relative importance of each input variable, revealing postoperative PCT/ALB, neutrophil count, IL-6, albumin, and prothrombin time as key predictors of urosepsis.  The developed model was made publicly accessible as a web application to facilitate its use in clinical practice.  This study demonstrates the potential of machine learning to accurately predict postoperative urosepsis, enabling early risk stratification and personalized interventions to improve patient outcomes.

The increasing mandatory use of artificial intelligence (AI) in higher education has raised concerns about its impact on student well-being and academic performance. Existing technology adoption models struggle to capture the complexities of compulsory digital environments, where poorly designed AI systems can contribute to cognitive overload and student resistance. This study investigates the influence of AI system design on student experiences and its subsequent effects on techno-resistance and academic outcomes. A quantitative approach was adopted, utilizing data from 229 undergraduate students across Malaysian universities.  

The study examined the relationships between system reliability, task routinization, information richness, negative technological experiences, techno-resistance, and academic performance.  Results indicate that system reliability and information richness significantly mitigate negative student experiences, while task routinization does not. Notably, negative technological experiences were found to be a key mediator, intensifying techno-resistance and negatively impacting academic performance. 

These findings highlight that the implementation of AI technologies in education, even when mandated, does not automatically lead to improved learning outcomes.  The study proposes a reframing of techno-resistance as a response to the cognitive and emotional strain caused by poorly designed AI systems in compulsory educational settings. By integrating perspectives from digital dissonance, sociotechnical systems, and cognitive load theory, this research contributes to a deeper understanding of the complex interplay between AI system design, student experiences, and academic success. The findings offer practical guidance for the development of more student-centered AI educational technologies.

Quantum computers hold the potential to revolutionize the simulation of quantum many-body systems. However, recent research has demonstrated the capability of classical machine learning algorithms to predict various properties of quantum systems using classical data. While theoretical learning tasks with proven quantum advantages have been proposed, they primarily focus on cryptographic functions and lack relevance to real-world laboratory experiments. This study investigates the practical applicability of quantum advantage in learning quantum observables from classical measurement data. We analyze two types of observables: linear combinations of Pauli strings and unitarily parametrized observables.  Our findings establish clear boundaries distinguishing tasks efficiently solvable by classical methods from those requiring quantum computation for data analysis.  Unlike previous work, our classical hardness results are based on the weaker assumption that problems in the complexity class BQP cannot be efficiently simulated by classical circuits. Furthermore, we present a novel quantum learning algorithm for these tasks.  These results provide valuable insights into the utility of quantum resources for learning problems in quantum many-body physics and highlight potential areas where quantum learning algorithms can offer practical advantages.

Generative adversarial networks (GANs) have revolutionized deep learning by enabling the creation of high-fidelity synthetic data.  This has led to widespread adoption across diverse fields such as image synthesis, speech generation, and medical data reconstruction. Despite significant progress, GAN training remains challenging, with issues like instability, mode collapse, and hyperparameter sensitivity hindering optimal performance.  

This study comprehensively analyzes 45 distinct GAN architectures, categorizing them based on learning paradigms, applications, network designs, datasets, and remaining challenges. The analysis aims to provide a structured understanding of GAN evolution and highlight current trends.  By examining the strengths and weaknesses of various architectures, this survey identifies key areas for future research. 

The findings offer valuable insights for both researchers and practitioners seeking to navigate the evolving landscape of GANs and leverage their potential for innovative applications.  The exploration of novel architectures, training methodologies, and evaluation metrics will be crucial in addressing existing challenges and unlocking the full capabilities of GANs.

The increasing integration of pervasive sensing, data platforms, and artificial intelligence in urban environments is driving the development of recommender systems as crucial tools for enhancing urban services. This study provides a comprehensive analysis of existing research on recommender systems in smart cities, encompassing domains such as mobility, healthcare, energy, tourism, retail, and e-governance.  The research categorizes various recommender system techniques, including collaborative filtering, content-based methods, hybrid approaches, deep learning, and context-aware strategies, in relation to their applications in urban decision-making.  

The analysis reveals a concentration of empirical studies in mobility and tourism, while the application of deep and graph-based techniques remains relatively limited in governance and energy sectors.  The study explores how diverse data sources, such as IoT data, geospatial information, environmental indicators, and demographic data, are integrated to enable personalized recommendations while addressing challenges related to latency, reliability, and privacy.  

Furthermore, the research highlights advancements in addressing data sparsity and the "cold start" problem through the use of graph neural models, sequence modeling, and transfer learning.  It also examines operational considerations such as edge inference and streaming architectures for real-time recommendations.  The study critically assesses the risks and governance aspects associated with recommender systems in smart cities, including privacy preservation, fairness, equitable exposure across neighborhoods, explainability, and mechanisms for audit and oversight.  

Finally, the research identifies promising future directions, such as pollution management, citizen education, and participatory platforms, that can foster greater civic engagement.  It also explores the potential impact of emerging technologies, including digital twins, immersive interfaces, generative models, and quantum algorithms, on personalization and oversight in urban settings.  The study concludes by emphasizing the need for field evaluations and standardized benchmarks that comprehensively evaluate the accuracy, latency, robustness, and equity of recommender systems in real-world urban environments.

Alzheimer's disease (AD) has been linked to immune and metabolic pathways through genome-wide association studies, but the precise cellular mechanisms remain elusive. This study utilized bidirectional two-sample Mendelian randomization to uncover single nucleotide polymorphism-mediated, AD-associated immunometabolic signatures, revealing both positive and negative correlations between immune cell types and metabolic pathways and AD.  

Further investigation through integrated single-cell omics analysis identified distinct astrocyte subpopulations in patient brains. One subpopulation, enriched in glutamate-glutamine uptake and metabolism, showed a positive association with AD, while another, characterized by amino acid metabolism and transport, exhibited a negative association.  In peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs), AD-negative metabolic signatures were observed, accompanied by downregulated immune responses. 

Based on these findings, a blood transcriptome-based AD prediction model was developed and optimized using multiple machine learning algorithms. This model, trained on a gene set derived from blood immune cells negatively associated with AD, demonstrated applicability to both European and Asian populations. Notably, it enabled pre-symptomatic detection for familial AD, effectively differentiated AD from other neurodegenerative disorders, and is readily adaptable for clinical implementation.  This research provides novel insights into the crucial role of immunometabolism in AD pathogenesis and offers a practical predictive tool for large-scale, routine population screening.

Surgical outcomes for horizontal non-paretic strabismus can exhibit variability. This study investigated the potential of machine learning to predict surgical dosage based on preoperative patient features, aiming to enhance surgical precision and consistency. A retrospective analysis of 767 patients who underwent strabismus surgery between 2003 and 2022 was conducted. A multilayer perceptron model was developed using a deep learning framework to predict surgical dosage based on various preoperative factors.  Explainable artificial intelligence techniques were employed to assess the model's feature attributions and ensure their clinical plausibility.  The Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno algorithm yielded the most accurate predictions, achieving a root mean square error of 0.54 mm and 0.71 mm for the primary and secondary labels, respectively.  The model successfully predicted dosages within an acceptable range for 69% and 55% of the data for the primary and secondary labels, respectively. Feature importance analysis, using techniques like feature permutation and integrated gradients, identified the preoperative angle of deviation as the most influential factor, followed by near angle of deviation, simultaneous cover test results, Hirschberg test angle, and refractive error. This study demonstrates the potential of machine learning to assist surgeons in predicting surgical dosages for strabismus correction, ultimately contributing to more precise and consistent surgical outcomes.  Further refinement of the model is anticipated with the inclusion of a larger dataset.

The proliferation of fake news on social media platforms poses a significant threat to societal trust and safety, with emotionally charged content often driving its rapid dissemination. This research investigates the identification of anomalous propagation subgraphs indicative of coordinated misinformation campaigns. To address this challenge, we introduce MAPLE, a novel framework for emotion-aware anomaly detection in social media propagation. MAPLE integrates sentiment features derived from users' past posts with network structural information, leveraging a multi-Graph Neural Network architecture. This architecture comprises Graph Convolutional Networks, Graph Attention Networks, and GraphSAGE, fused through an attention-based mechanism. Subgraph embeddings generated by this framework are then analyzed using a One-Class Support Vector Machine for anomaly detection. Theoretical analysis demonstrates the effectiveness of MAPLE by establishing guarantees on mutual information preservation, variance reduction, anomaly margin amplification, and entropy maximization.  Experimental evaluation on the PolitiFact and GossipCop datasets reveals that MAPLE consistently surpasses existing methods, achieving significant improvements in F1-scores. Notably, MAPLE demonstrates robustness across diverse datasets, outperforming baselines by 43% and 2.17% respectively.  Unlike previous approaches that isolate structural or sentiment cues, MAPLE presents a unique multi-Graph Neural Network framework that integrates emotional context and offers theoretical justifications for subgraph anomaly detection.

Feature selection is a crucial preprocessing step in machine learning, especially when handling high-dimensional datasets often characterized by redundant or irrelevant features.  Inefficient feature handling can lead to increased computational complexity and hinder the generalization ability of classifiers.  Existing binary metaheuristics often rely on static control parameters, which can result in premature convergence and limited adaptability.  This study proposes a novel nature-inspired binary optimizer called the Dynamic Binary Swordfish Movement Optimization Algorithm (DBSMOA) to address these challenges. DBSMOA is inspired by the foraging behavior of swordfish and extends the original Swordfish Movement Optimization Algorithm (SMOA) by incorporating dynamic behavioral adaptation mechanisms. These mechanisms allow for a self-adjusting balance between exploration and exploitation throughout the optimization process.  The algorithm utilizes a sigmoid-based probabilistic binary mapping and dynamically reassigns agent roles based on performance, promoting diversity and stability during the search.  Extensive experiments on 52 benchmark datasets demonstrate that DBSMOA achieves superior performance compared to twelve state-of-the-art binary optimizers, including Binary Particle Swarm Optimization, Binary Genetic Algorithm, and Binary Grey Wolf Optimizer.  DBSMOA consistently yields the lowest average classification error on a significant number of datasets and produces the smallest average subset size, showcasing its effectiveness in feature selection and its potential for other binary optimization tasks.

High-grade cervical intraepithelial neoplasia (CIN2/3) poses a significant risk for cervical cancer, but existing screening methods often lack accessibility or invasiveness. This study aimed to develop a non-invasive, machine learning model utilizing routine blood biomarkers to predict the risk of high-grade CIN and potentially guide triage before colposcopy.  A dataset comprising 128 high-grade CIN patients and 120 low-grade CIN patients was analyzed, incorporating 29 clinical characteristics and blood test measurements.  Four feature selection algorithms were employed to identify the most relevant predictors, followed by training and evaluation of 11 machine learning algorithms.  The Support Vector Machine (SVM) algorithm demonstrated the highest predictive accuracy, achieving an area under the curve (AUC) of 0.75 and a Brier score of 0.21.  The SHapley Additive exPlanation (SHAP) method was used to determine the contribution of each feature to the model's predictions.  Key features included creatinine, red blood cell count, neutrophil ratio, direct bilirubin, and monocyte count.  A web-based calculator was developed to facilitate clinical implementation and risk stratification.  These findings suggest that the developed machine learning model, with its strong predictive performance and user-friendly interface, holds promise as a potential tool for improving cervical cancer screening and management.

The development of functional materials often necessitates the simultaneous optimization of multiple interconnected properties.  A prime example is boron-doped graphene (BDG), where both band gap and work function are crucial for its performance in electronics and catalysis. However, existing machine learning methods often concentrate on predicting single properties and rely on manually engineered features, hindering their broader applicability. This study introduces an adaptive edge-aware graph convolutional neural network with multi-task learning (AEGCNN-MTL) designed to accurately predict multiple material properties concurrently.  

Evaluated on a dataset of 2613 DFT-computed BDG structures, AEGCNN-MTL demonstrated high predictive accuracy for both band gap (R² = 0.9905) and work function (R² = 0.9778).  Furthermore, when compared to representative single-task graph neural network models trained with the same resources, AEGCNN-MTL exhibited superior performance.  The framework's ability to generalize was further assessed on the QM9 benchmark, where it achieved competitive results across twelve diverse quantum chemical properties. These findings underscore the potential of AEGCNN-MTL as a powerful tool for high-throughput, multi-property screening and the data-driven discovery of materials with tailored functionalities.

This study introduces a novel method for generating material fingerprints that aim to be both computationally efficient and interpretable by humans. This approach is designed for use in both machine learning applications and for understanding the relationships between a material's structure and its properties. The proposed material encoding consists of two parts: one representing the crystal structure and the other characterizing the chemical composition, termed the Pettifor embedding.  The Pettifor embedding for chemical composition utilizes a non-orthogonal space where each axis corresponds to a chemical element, and the angle between axes reflects the similarity between elements.  The chemical composition is then represented as a point on the unit sphere within this non-orthogonal space.  

Experiments demonstrate that the Pettifor embeddings consistently outperform other widely used elemental embeddings in compositional machine learning models.  By leveraging the Pettifor embeddings to define a distance metric and applying dimensionality reduction techniques, a two-dimensional map of thermodynamically stable crystalline compounds is constructed.  Despite their simplicity, these maps effectively group materials based on fundamental physical properties.

Terrestrial ecosystems play a crucial role in climate regulation by absorbing a significant portion of human-caused carbon dioxide emissions.  Accurate monitoring of carbon, water, and energy fluxes within these ecosystems is essential for understanding their responses to climate change. However, existing flux datasets often lack the necessary spatial resolution and consistency to effectively analyze fragmented landscapes like agricultural areas. This study introduces the Unified FLUXes (UFLUX) ensemble, a novel dataset designed to address this gap. UFLUX integrates eddy covariance data, satellite observations, and machine learning algorithms to generate a globally consistent representation of gross primary productivity, evapotranspiration, and sensible heat fluxes. The dataset encompasses approximately 60 ensemble members, spanning multiple spatial and temporal scales, including global monthly data at 0.25° resolution, European daily data at 0.25° resolution, and UK daily data at 100m resolution.  Validation against eddy covariance measurements demonstrates that UFLUX effectively captures over 80% of the observed flux variability, exhibiting low mean absolute errors. The dataset accurately reproduces known climate responses and interannual patterns consistent with previous research, although uncertainties remain in the estimation of net carbon flux. UFLUX offers a valuable resource for supporting cross-scale climate policy development and land management strategies aimed at achieving carbon neutrality.

The proliferation of multiplayer gaming has fueled a persistent conflict between anti-cheat systems and the developers of increasingly sophisticated cheats.  Traditional anti-cheat methods, such as signature-based detection and memory scanning, have proven insufficient in addressing the evolving nature of these threats.  This study examines the limitations of these conventional approaches and explores the emergence of AI-driven solutions for combating cheats.  Modern techniques, including computer vision for aimbot detection and deep learning models for behavioral analysis, offer proactive and adaptive capabilities.  However, a significant gap exists in the current literature regarding the integration of these disparate approaches.  This research proposes a novel hybrid security framework that combines the effectiveness of traditional methods against known exploits with the dynamic detection power of AI for zero-day threats.  This framework incorporates cryptographic technologies like Trusted Execution Environments for process isolation and Hierarchical Identity-Based Encryption for secure authentication, creating a robust, multi-layered defense.  Future research directions include the integration of post-quantum cryptography for enhanced asset protection and the development of lightweight frameworks for mobile and virtual reality gaming platforms. This study provides a technical foundation and architectural blueprint for the development of next-generation secure and cheat-resistant online gaming systems.

The optimization of sintered neodymium-iron-boron (NdFeB) magnets for high-performance applications is hindered by the intricate relationship between their structure and properties, compounded by the scarcity of available data. This study addresses this challenge by establishing a comprehensive database encompassing 1994 industrial and academic samples of NdFeB magnets.  The objective is to investigate the efficacy of active learning (AL) strategies in conjunction with both classical and quantum-enhanced regression models.  A comparative analysis reveals distinct design heuristics employed in industrial and academic datasets, highlighting the importance of domain-specific considerations.  Furthermore, this research proposes a novel methodology for integrating quantum kernel regression into an AL framework, incorporating a bootstrapped ensemble for robust uncertainty estimation.  Crucially, the findings demonstrate a strong dependence of AL effectiveness on the chosen regression model. While AL significantly accelerates training for models such as Random Forest and Support Vector Regression (SVR), it proves less beneficial or even detrimental for models like XGBoost, particularly when coupled with quantum enhancements in the SVR case.  These results provide valuable insights into the strategic deployment of machine learning techniques for accelerating materials discovery.

Energetic Intelligence, a recently validated construct encompassing emotional, spiritual, and physical dimensions of human intelligence, presents a comprehensive understanding of human potential. This study investigates the feasibility of predicting Energetic Intelligence using machine learning techniques.  Data collected through organizational surveys were analyzed to develop predictive models employing various supervised learning algorithms. Model performance was assessed using standard metrics, with optimized and simplified models achieving an R-squared value of 0.73, indicating a promising level of accuracy for predicting this psychologically grounded construct.  Key predictors of Energetic Intelligence included variables such as Flow, Flourishing, and Emotional Vitality, consistently demonstrating their significance in model training.  These findings suggest the potential of machine learning to advance psychological research and provide practical tools for assessing and fostering Energetic Intelligence in real-world applications.  This research highlights the benefits of integrating artificial intelligence methodologies with psychological theory to gain data-driven insights into human well-being and potential.

Cyber-physical systems, which integrate sensors, communication, and computing powered by integrated circuits (ICs), face a growing threat from malicious hardware attacks, particularly hardware Trojans. These stealthy implants, introduced during design or fabrication, remain undetected until triggered, potentially causing functional disruptions or data breaches.
