This study investigates the obstacle of hyperparameter tuning in the implementation of unsupervised anomaly detection systems within heterogeneous smart home environments, which typically necessitates costly, site-specific adjustments. The paper aims to bridge this gap by exploring the transferability of hyperparameters, thereby enabling the development of zero-tune, easily deployable security solutions. Five unsupervised machine learning models, including Elliptic Envelope (EE), Isolation Forest (IF), Local Outlier Factor (LoF), One-Class SVM (oSVM), and an Autoencoder (AE), are systematically evaluated across five prominent Internet of Things (IoT) datasets. A thorough, dataset-specific hyperparameter optimization approach is employed to compare the performance of transferred configurations against both dataset-specific and default settings. The results demonstrate a performance hierarchy, with dataset-specific tuning remaining the optimal choice, yet an intelligent transfer strategy significantly surpassing default configurations. Remarkably, the IoTID20 dataset emerges as the most effective source, as indicated by a quantitative topological analysis. This analysis reveals that the high feature space complexity and cluster overlap in IoTID20 (as shown by low Silhouette scores) create a challenging training environment that yields robust, transferable hyperparameters. Moreover, the analysis reveals a strategic trade-off: Autoencoders and LoF deliver the highest absolute performance, while IF offers the most significant improvement over default settings. This research offers a quantitative framework for dataset-driven initialization, contributing to the development of resilient, low-maintenance intrusion detection systems. © The Author(s) 2026.

In the ongoing pursuit of early and accessible detection methods for Alzheimer's disease (AD), this study presents a novel machine learning-based model, the Lactylation-Derived Score (LDS), constructed from lactylation-related genes across nine distinct AD cohorts. The development of LDS was facilitated through a standardized pipeline, incorporating z-score normalization, random forest-based feature screening, plsRglm modeling, and 10-fold cross-validation. The efficacy of LDS was validated externally in seven independent brain transcriptomic datasets and clinically in an independent plasma cohort (n = 540). To further enhance diagnostic performance, logistic regression was employed to integrate LDS with plasma phosphorylated tau 181 (p-tau181) and p-tau217. The three-marker model (LDS + p-tau181 + p-tau217) demonstrated the highest diagnostic accuracy (AUC 0.859, 95% CI 0.824–0.893). LDS proved capable of identifying AD-positive individuals effectively (AUC 0.861, 95% CI 0.827–0.897), and a five-gene classifier derived from LDS genes successfully stratified amnestic mild cognitive impairment with an AUC of 0.809 (95% CI 0.714–0.836). Notably, LDS-high individuals displayed neuroinflammatory activation and metabolic stress signatures, suggesting that this scalable and interpretable transcriptomic model serves as a valuable complement to plasma p-tau biomarkers, thereby advancing precision digital medicine in AD. © The Author(s) 2026.

In the rapidly evolving landscape of healthcare, artificial intelligence (AI) is making significant strides, particularly in the field of dentistry. This study focuses on the development of an AI-driven tool, named HistorAI, designed to streamline and optimize the evaluation of dental patient histories. The tool utilizes machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) algorithms, emulating human cognitive processes, to aid in diagnostics, treatment planning, and patient care.

The evaluation process involves the use of a structured 22-item anamnesis questionnaire, addressing both oral and systemic health concerns. Two AI models, GPT-4 and Gemini, were employed to analyze the collected data. GPT-4 was integrated via an Application Programming Interface (API) to provide treatment suggestions, generate prescriptions, recommend referrals, and assess patient complaints.

A comparative analysis was conducted between the AI-assisted and conventional methods, using descriptive statistics, independent-samples t-tests, and Cohen’s d for effect size calculation. The results demonstrated that HistorAI significantly reduced the time required for dental history assessments compared to traditional methods (p < 0.01), with a Cohen’s d of 2.599, indicating a substantial increase in efficiency for the AI-assisted group.

In conclusion, HistorAI shows promise in enhancing efficiency and improving clinical decision-making in dental practice while maintaining clinician oversight. However, further clinical validation and careful consideration of ethical implications are crucial to ensure the safe and responsible integration of AI into dental workflows. Future research should focus on refining the tool and addressing any potential ethical concerns to ensure its optimal and responsible implementation in dental care.

In the realm of drug discovery, the ability to accurately predict compound-protein interactions (CPIs) is crucial. The effectiveness of machine learning (ML) models for CPI prediction hinges on the availability and diversity of CPI datasets, particularly those maintained by industries. To address privacy concerns and maximize data utility, federated learning (FL), a method that integrates data from multiple sites while preserving privacy, has emerged as a promising approach. However, its performance in dealing with heterogeneous data from various molecular domains, a common real-world scenario, is yet to be fully established, hindering its broader adoption. This study investigates the application of FL for CPI prediction using datasets across multiple chemical and protein domains. The results reveal that the FL model enhances out-of-domain prediction performance, but under data heterogeneity, it is outperformed by local models for in-domain data. Based on these findings, a novel strategy, the similarity-guided ensemble (SGE), was developed. This strategy combines the global FL model with fine-tuned models based on each client's local data, achieving robust performance for both in-domain and out-of-domain tasks. The efficacy of this approach was demonstrated using both public datasets and real-world industry data from 13 pharmaceutical companies. In conclusion, this study provides insights into the performance trade-offs caused by heterogeneous data distributions in FL for CPI prediction and offers a practical solution through the integration of local fine-tuning and the SGE, ensuring robust accuracy for both in-domain and out-of-domain predictions in contemporary drug discovery processes.

This study endeavors to construct and validate a machine learning (ML) model for predicting the risk of gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) using 45 dietary nutrients and baseline demographic data. A retrospective analysis was performed on 3,649 pregnant women's data from the NHANES database (2007-2018), encompassing demographic factors (age, race/ethnicity, BMI, etc.) and dietary nutrient intake. To address class imbalance, the Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) was employed post-train-test split. Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) and the Boruta algorithm were utilized for feature selection to reduce multicollinearity and identify essential predictors, respectively. Six ML models, namely XGBoost, LightGBM, Random Forest (RF), Support Vector Machine (SVM), Gaussian Naive Bayes (GNB), and K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), were trained. Model performance was assessed using metrics such as Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC), accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F-Beta score (β = 2), and Precision-Recall Area Under the Curve (PR-AUC). SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) analysis was employed to clarify feature importance.

The core predictors identified were race/ethnicity, BMI, protein, dietary fiber, α-carotene, β-carotene, lutein/zeaxanthin, folate (DFE), calcium, phosphorus, zinc, potassium, alcohol intake, educational level, and smoking status. In the validation set, XGBoost demonstrated superior performance (accuracy: 93.1%, F-Beta: 0.943, AUC: 0.966, sensitivity: 97.5%, specificity: 86.7%,

In the realm of single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq), the exploration of intricate tissue transcriptional landscapes has led to the discovery of novel cell types and functions. Nevertheless, the task of identifying and categorizing cells from scRNA-seq datasets remains a significant challenge. To alleviate this issue, we have developed a novel computational tool, named CIA (Cluster Independent Annotation). This tool offers an accurate, user-friendly, and practical approach to cell-type and functional annotation of single cells, without the need for a fully annotated reference dataset or intricate machine learning processes. The CIA framework is compatible with various single-cell analysis frameworks, as it is implemented in both Python and R programming languages. The tool is available under the MIT license, with comprehensive documentation accessible at the following links: Python package - <https://pypi.org/project/cia-python/>, Python tutorial - <https://cia-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tutorial/Cluster_Independent_Annotation.html>, and R package and tutorial - <https://github.com/ingmbioinfo/CIA_R>. Our findings indicate that CIA's classification performances are on par with contemporary state-of-the-art methods, while offering a substantially reduced computational running time. In essence, CIA streamlines the process of generating reproducible signature-based cell assignments, which can be effortlessly interpreted through graphical summaries. This tool equips researchers with a potent instrument to delve into the complex transcriptional landscape of single cells. © The Author(s) 2025.

In this research, we present MatterVial, a groundbreaking hybrid framework for feature-based machine learning in materials science, which leverages latent representations from a diverse ensemble of pre-trained graph-neural network (GNN) models. The models encompass structure-based (MEGNet), composition-based (ROOST), and equivariant (ORB) graph networks, along with computationally efficient GNN-approximated descriptors and novel features derived from symbolic regression. By merging the chemical interpretability of traditional feature-based models with the predictive prowess of deep learning architectures, MatterVial offers a unique blend of transparency and accuracy.

When applied to Matbench tasks in conjunction with the existing feature-based model MODNet, our method demonstrates substantial error reductions, significantly enhancing its performance. In some cases, it even surpasses state-of-the-art end-to-end GNNs, with accuracy improvements exceeding 40% for various tasks. An integrated interpretability module, utilizing surrogate models and symbolic regression, translates the latent GNN-derived descriptors into explicit, physically significant formulas, thereby fostering a deeper understanding of the materials under investigation.

This unified framework represents a significant advancement in materials informatics, providing a high-performance, transparent tool that adheres to the principles of explainable AI. This tool is expected to facilitate more targeted and autonomous materials discovery, paving the way for a more comprehensive understanding of materials properties and behavior. © The Author(s) 2026.

In the realm of Alzheimer's Disease (AD) diagnostics and prognostics, the fusion of multimodal data has demonstrated significant potential in enhancing the precision and interpretability of artificial intelligence (AI) models. This systematic review offers an in-depth exploration of the latest advancements in AI-driven multimodal fusion strategies for AD prediction. The study provides a thorough examination of popular datasets, delineating their modalities, preprocessing procedures, and accessibility, to promote reproducibility and methodological transparency.

The analysis encompasses a variety of data harmonization and preprocessing techniques applied across neuroimaging modalities (e.g., fMRI, sMRI, PET), electrophysiological (EEG), and genomic data, underscoring domain-specific practices and challenges. The fusion strategies are systematically categorized into data-level, feature-level, decision-level, and temporal (early, intermediate, and late) paradigms, offering valuable insights into their implementation and diagnostic impact.

The review further investigates the utilization of Explainable AI (XAI) techniques across studies and identifies a notable scarcity of works that concurrently emphasize multimodality, explainability, and methodological rigor. Adhering to both PRISMA and Kitchenham’s guidelines, this review ensures transparency and replicability in evidence synthesis.

Distinguishing itself from existing reviews, this work uniquely focuses on the intersection of multimodal integration and explainability within a systematically validated framework. The review concludes with recommendations for future research aimed at developing robust, interpretable, and clinically relevant AI models for AD, paving the way for advancements in the field. © The Author(s) 2026.

In the escalating quest for innovative materials, machine learning-driven inverse design techniques encounter substantial hurdles in harmonizing the vast, high-dimensional materials composition space with scant experimental data. Traditional approaches encounter two primary shortcomings: (1) machine learning models exhibit unreliability in high-dimensional spaces, resulting in prediction biases during the design process; (2) these models fail to adequately incorporate domain expert knowledge, thereby limiting their potential to support knowledge-guided inverse design. To surmount these obstacles, we propose AIMATDESIGN, a reinforcement learning framework that amplifies experimental data through difference-based sampling to establish a trustworthy experience repository. To bolster model reliability, an automated refinement strategy, guided by large language models, rectifies prediction discrepancies, thereby ensuring a more accurate alignment between reward signals and value functions. Furthermore, a knowledge-based reward capitalizes on expert rules to augment stability and efficiency. Empirical studies indicate that AIMATDESIGN surpasses conventional machine learning and reinforcement learning benchmarks in terms of discovery efficiency, convergence speed, and success rates. Validation of Zr-based alloys through experimentation led to a bulk metallic glass exhibiting a yield strength of 1.7 GPa and an elongation of 10.2%, closely approximating predictions. This framework exhibits consistent performance in capturing property trends and offers a general roadmap for closed-loop, data-efficient materials discovery. © The Author(s) 2026.

In the realm of machine learning applications, particularly within electroencephalography (EEG) analysis for neurolinguistics and second language acquisition, the importance of feature selection cannot be overstated. By elucidating neural activity during language learning, valuable insights into cognitive and linguistic processing are gained. Despite significant progress in EEG-based studies, the challenge of identifying optimal feature selection techniques to boost classification performance and interpretability persists. This research aims to systematically assess and contrast various feature selection methods to enhance the precision and efficiency of EEG-based language learning classification, focusing on the acquisition of Arabic and Hindi vocabulary. EEG signals were collected from twenty Indian and Yemeni participants during their language learning process. The data underwent preprocessing, feature extraction, and classification using Random Forest (RF), Support Vector Machine (SVM), Decision Tree (DT), XGBoost, CatBoost, and K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) algorithms. Five feature selection techniques, including Minimum Redundancy Maximum Relevance (mRMR), refined selection, ReliefF, ensemble selection, and Genetic Algorithm (GA), were employed and compared. The results demonstrated that feature selection substantially improved classification accuracy, with ensemble and ReliefF methods achieving the highest accuracies of 97.13% and 97.15%, respectively, notably with RF, SVM, and XGBoost classifiers. These methods effectively reduced dimensionality, minimized overfitting, and augmented model interpretability. In conclusion, this study offers a comprehensive evaluation of feature selection strategies in EEG-based neurolinguistic research, providing insights into the neural mechanisms of language learning and supporting advancements in brain–computer interfaces, cognitive neuroscience, and machine learning applications. © The Author(s) 2025.

In the realm of polar reaction mechanisms, proton transfers play a pivotal role. This study compiles an extensive dataset of approximately 51 million kinetically feasible proton transfer events between heteroatoms, derived from approximately 8,000 acids and their conjugate bases, encompassing a broad spectrum of aqueous pK<sub>a</sub> values ranging from -15 to +37. The rate constants were estimated at 25°C using a streamlined Eigen equation, incorporating pK<sub>a</sub> values but excluding statistical factors. Proton transfer steps with rate constants exceeding 10<sup>3</sup> M<sup>-1</sup> s<sup>-1</sup> were retained for the final dataset. Furthermore, an additional 5,043 proton transfer events from carbon acids to heteroatom bases were estimated using the Eigen-Bernasconi equation, based on reported intrinsic rate constants and Brønsted β values, with rate constants surpassing 10<sup>3</sup> M<sup>-1</sup> s<sup>-1</sup> added to the dataset. Each entry was encoded in SMIRKS format, ensuring machine learning compatibility, with a focus on structural diversity over diversity of conditions. The calculated rate constants are anticipated to exhibit accuracy in aqueous environments. This methodology and dataset are expected to be instrumental in training models capable of predicting stepwise mechanistic pathways. The work is slated for publication in 2026.

In the realm of molecular datasets, the understanding of metabolites' associations with biochemical pathways is crucial for interpreting findings in biological and biomedical research. However, pathway annotations are often sparse, hindering the interpretation at the pathway level. To overcome this limitation, previous studies have developed machine learning models to predict the pathway association of small biomolecules (metabolites and xenobiotics) using data from the Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG). This study expands upon this research by training and evaluating multilayer perceptron models using compound entries and pathway annotations from MetaCyc, an alternative knowledgebase with a larger number of compound entries and pathway definitions than KEGG.

The models trained on the MetaCyc dataset exhibited a mean Matthews correlation coefficient (MCC) of 0.845, with a standard deviation of 0.0101, compared to a mean MCC of 0.847 and a standard deviation of 0.0098 for the KEGG dataset. Notably, MetaCyc's 184 metabolic-only pathway predictions (out of 502 total pathways) showed a mean MCC of 0.800, representing a 5.6% improvement in metabolic pathway prediction performance compared to KEGG. Given that MetaCyc pathways are metabolically focused, these results suggest that the 4055 MetaCyc pathways can be effectively predicted at a level of performance comparable to the current state-of-the-art. This study underscores the potential of using MetaCyc for pathway-level interpretation in molecular datasets.

In the realm of machine learning, hypergraphs, distinct from conventional networks, offer a robust representation of intricate relationships among nodes, making them indispensable for various real-world applications, such as analyzing social interactions. Transforming network structures into simplified spaces through learning embedded node representations allows the application of machine learning techniques designed for vector data to network data. However, existing methods tend to disregard the structural nuances inherent in hypergraphs. This study presents HyperS2V, a novel node embedding approach that prioritizes structural similarity within hypergraphs. The approach incorporates hyper-degrees to encapsulate nodes' structural properties, develops functions to assess structural similarity, and employs a multi-scale random walk framework for generating embeddings. The efficacy of HyperS2V is validated through experiments on both synthetic and real-world networks, demonstrating its superiority in terms of interpretability and applicability to subsequent tasks, thereby offering a significant advancement in the field. © The Author(s) 2026.

In the realm of computer vision and image processing, Single Image Super-Resolution (SISR) plays a pivotal role, focusing on enhancing the sharpness and visual quality of low-resolution images. Applications of SISR span across various domains, including facial recognition, video surveillance, medical imaging, remote sensing, and scene text recognition. Existing SISR techniques, both conventional and machine learning-based, have been extensively studied. However, conventional methods often struggle with recreating fine details, while machine learning-based techniques, particularly those utilizing residual networks, demonstrate remarkable performance in image-enhancing tasks. Despite their effectiveness, these networks tend to accumulate unnecessary complexity, leading to increased model weight scale, execution time, and memory consumption.

To address these issues, we propose the development of Multi-Scale Deep Residual Networks (MSDRNs), incorporating a novel mean-std normalization mechanism. This mechanism uniformly scales all residual branches, ensuring stability and generalization in residual learning. Additionally, we introduce a Bayesian hyperparameter optimization approach to adaptively tune critical parameters, promoting robust convergence. The evaluation of the reconstructed images is conducted using a combination of full-reference metrics (PSNR, SSIM) and the no-reference NIQE index, ensuring a balance between quantitative accuracy and human-perceived visual fidelity.

Comparative analysis with linear, residual, recursive, dense, and attention-based approaches reveals that the proposed MSDRNs outperform in terms of image quality, achieving higher SSIM (0.94) and lower NIQE (6.73) scores compared to contemporary methods such as SwinIR and EDSR. The proposed method leverages high resolution for accuracy maximization, demonstrating its potential as a promising solution for SISR tasks. © The Author(s) 2026.

This study introduces a collection of expert-labeled image datasets, specifically designed for the automated analysis of human sperm in the field of male fertility diagnostics. The dataset comprises three subsets: a primary collection of over 400 high-resolution, stained bright-field images featuring multiple sperm cells; a binary classification set, with image patches cropped and labeled as either sperm or non-sperm; and a multiclass dataset, categorizing individual sperm cells based on DNA fragmentation level, determined by halo size (large, medium, small, or no halo). Five experienced embryologists independently performed annotations, with final labels determined by majority agreement. These datasets serve as a basis for training and evaluating machine learning models, focusing on sperm detection, classification, and chromatin integrity assessment. The data's open availability aims to promote reproducibility and advance research in reproductive health. © The Author(s) 2025.

This study presents a comprehensive dataset of multimodal biomechanical recordings during over-ground walking, ramps, and stairs, encompassing electromyographic (EMG), inertial (IMU), and plantar pressure data. The dataset was generated from 45 participants, including 15 individuals with unilateral transtibial amputation and 30 without, who completed five standardized locomotor tasks. These tasks consisted of level walking, ramp ascent/descent, and stair ascent/descent, with each participant performing 50 supervised trials. Wireless EMG and IMU sensors (Delsys Trigno Avanti) were utilized to measure muscle activation and kinematics, while intelligent insoles (XSENSOR) captured plantar pressure distribution. The raw data were saved in .hpf (EMG/IMU) and .XSN (pressure) formats, with processed outputs in .csv files. The dataset is organized by task and sensor type, including complete participant metadata. Significant dataset outputs include time-normalized EMG amplitudes, segment kinematics, and pressure maps across terrains and populations. The dataset underwent technical and experimental validation during acquisition. This resource offers a valuable platform for quantitative analysis of gait adaptation and supports machine learning applications in locomotion classification. The data are provided in accessible formats, promoting reuse in biomechanics, rehabilitation engineering, robotics, and clinical gait research. © The Author(s) 2025.

In the realm of machine learning, quantum computers present a promising potential for surpassing classical computers in performance, particularly on complex tasks. However, quantum learning systems are susceptible to catastrophic forgetting, a phenomenon that hinders the achievement of continual learning. This study experimentally demonstrates quantum continual learning on a superconducting processor, using a sequential training of a quantum classifier with three tasks: two involving real-life image identification and one focusing on classifying quantum states. The research reveals the classifier's susceptibility to catastrophic forgetting. To mitigate this issue, an elastic weight consolidation strategy is employed, enabling the quantum classifier to incrementally retain knowledge across the three tasks, achieving an average accuracy of 92.3%. Furthermore, for sequential tasks involving quantum-engineered data, the quantum classifier outperforms a classical classifier with a comparable number of parameters. This research provides a viable strategy for enhancing the adaptability of quantum learning systems to sequential tasks, paving the way for their practical application. © The Author(s) 2026.

In the realm of machine learning, the encoding of categorical data is a pivotal preprocessing step, as models predominantly operate on numerical inputs. This study investigates the performance and efficiency of 26 distinct categorical encoding techniques, applied across 13 real-world datasets and seven diverse machine learning algorithms. The evaluation is structured according to the predictive task, model performance, and computational efficiency, thereby offering a taxonomy for the selection of encoders. Furthermore, this research demonstrates the utility of Safe AI metrics in encoding pipelines, revealing their potential in enhancing model robustness and fairness. As a practical contribution, we introduce EncodeHero, a Python tool designed to facilitate further exploration by incorporating user-defined data and aiding in the selection of the most suitable encoding methodology, taking into account specific data and technical constraints. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Nature Switzerland AG 2026.

In an endeavor to discover novel diagnostic biomarkers for acute pancreatitis (AP) and to predict the progression to severe AP (SAP) early on, this study investigates the serum metabolomic profiles of patients across various disease stages. By integrating metabolomics with artificial intelligence, this research constructs bile acid-based predictive models. The study was registered with the Chinese Clinical Trial Registry (ChiCTR2000034117) on June 24, 2020. The comparative metabolomic analysis of patients revealed significant alterations in 303 metabolites and 461 lipid species in AP. Subsequent weighted gene coexpression network analysis showed robust correlations between clinical parameters and specific metabolic clusters, notably bile acids (BAs) and lipid species. Further targeted quantification of 63 BAs was conducted within a multicentre validation cohort (n = 948). Machine learning algorithms were applied to these data, resulting in the derivation of two distinct BA panels. The first panel, consisting of nine BAs, demonstrated high diagnostic accuracy for AP, even in individuals with negative conventional enzymatic biomarkers, and effectively distinguished AP from acute cholangitis, as demonstrated by increased area under the curve (AUC) values. A second panel, comprising 13 BAs, reliably identified patients at increased risk for SAP progression. Collectively, these findings validate the translational potential of machine learning-driven metabolic biomarkers for the precision management of acute abdominal conditions, emphasize the clinical utility of BAs as promising diagnostic and prognostic biomarkers in acute pancreatitis, and propose a new paradigm for the development of dynamic risk early-warning systems. It is important to note that this study is an observational study registered in ChiCTR, and does not fall under the ICMJE definition of a clinical trial requiring CONSORT compliance. © The Author(s) 2

In the realm of prostate cancer (PCa), the clinical diversity persists as a significant challenge. This study integrates single-cell and spatial transcriptomics with an explainable machine learning approach to elucidate a lethal tumor axis and develop a prognostic model with interpretable insights. From a comprehensive analysis of 141,986 high-quality single cells spanning localized, hormone-sensitive, and castration-resistant PCa, a malignant C4 epithelial subpopulation was identified. This subpopulation exhibits characteristics of high chromosomal instability, androgen receptor and cell-cycle activation, and stemness potential. Spatial mapping revealed immune-enriched yet suppressive niches, where fibroblasts, myeloid cells, and exhausted lymphocytes coexist, reflecting a functional immune imbalance. Among 101 machine learning pipelines, a Lasso plus PLS-Cox model demonstrated robust performance across independent cohorts. The C4-based risk score, when adjusted for age, Gleason score, and T stage, independently predicted recurrence-free survival. A nomogram combining this score with clinical variables showed good discrimination. SHAP interpretation pinpointed MT1M, PCSK1N, and ACSL3 as significant risk factors. PCSK1N was found to be progressively upregulated from normal prostate to castration-resistant disease and promoted proliferation, clonogenicity, migration, and enzalutamide resistance. Conversely, PCSK1N inhibition sensitized organoids and xenografts to androgen receptor-targeted therapy. This research delineates a C4-centered lethal tumor axis and offers an explainable, experimentally validated framework for prognostic stratification in PCa.

In the realm of energy strategy optimization and energy security, precise forecasting of global natural gas trade is essential. Traditional forecasting methods, relying on expert knowledge and statistical analysis, struggle to deliver accurate predictions due to the intricate and volatile factors influencing global natural gas trade. To address this challenge, machine learning (ML) approaches have been increasingly employed to model these complex and unpredictable factors using historical data related to global natural gas trade. However, global natural gas trade exhibits three unique characteristics: (1) network structure, characterized by the interconnected trade relationships between multiple countries, (2) dynamic nature, as these relationships evolve over time, and (3) complexity, due to the influence of numerous intricate factors. Existing ML models have yet to effectively capture these characteristics for forecasting purposes. This paper introduces a novel hybrid ML model, GAT-GT, which integrates Graph Attention Networks (GAT), Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU), and Transformer. The GAT-GT model leverages GAT to learn the latent features of the annual trade network among various countries, GRU to model the dynamic trade relationships, and Transformer to combine various complex factors with the learned latent features for forecasting. By combining the strengths of GAT, GRU, and Transformer, GAT-GT is capable of comprehensively capturing these three characteristics of global natural gas trade. Experimental results, using global natural gas trade data from 2000 to 2023, show that GAT-GT significantly outperforms eight advanced ML competitors in forecasting global natural gas trade. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Nature Switzerland AG 2026.

In the realm of materials science, the precise and interpretable modeling of crystalline materials is crucial for elucidating structure-property relationships, thereby accelerating materials discovery. While graph neural networks (GNNs) have demonstrated high predictive accuracy, they often lack physical interpretability and fail to explicitly model the hierarchical and symmetrical nature of crystals. To address this, we propose Capsule Graph Networks with E(3)-Equivariance (CGN-e3), a novel deep learning framework that combines equivariant message passing with capsule networks to capture both geometric symmetries and hierarchical motif structures. CGN-e3 utilizes E(3)-equivariant message passing to learn physically consistent features and organize them into capsule representations, enabling the disentanglement of local motifs, such as polyhedral environments, and their connection to global properties. The effectiveness of CGN-e3 is demonstrated through bandgap and formation energy prediction, as well as material classification using Materials Project and Matbench datasets. The model achieves a Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 0.054 eV/atom and 0.379 eV on formation energy and bandgap prediction, respectively, surpassing CGCNN and matching the performance of MEGNet on the same dataset. Moreover, CGN-e3 offers insightful interpretations of the learned capsule representations, providing a pathway for unsupervised motif discovery. The dynamic routing-by-agreement mechanism identifies and aggregates structurally significant local environments, offering human-interpretable insights by explicitly quantifying the contribution of specific structural motifs to target material properties. We validate our framework on key property prediction tasks and provide capsule-level interpretations of the results. This work represents the first integration of E(3)-equivariant GNNs with capsule networks for modeling crystalline materials, capturing both the fundamental physical symmetries of 3D space and the intrinsic hierarchical part-whole relationships found in crystal structures.

The study investigates the role of inhibitory control, a critical component of cognitive control function, in neurological disorders such as schizophrenia and obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD). These disorders are characterized by abnormalities in frontal-subcortical circuits, which are also associated with intrusive saccades during active visual fixation. This research employs eye movement studies and machine learning (ML) techniques to classify individuals into three groups: healthy controls, patients with OCD, and schizophrenia patients. The study analyzes data from three visual fixation tasks, using statistical tests to identify relevant saccade features for classification. A shallow Artificial Neural Network (ANN) is implemented for both binary and three-class classification. The binary classification achieved 87% accuracy and 93% specificity in distinguishing controls from schizophrenia patients, 84% accuracy and 90% sensitivity in differentiating between controls and medicated OCD patients not on antipsychotics, while the differentiation between schizophrenia and medicated OCD patients reached 77% accuracy and 82% specificity. The results suggest that selected saccadic features can differentiate OCD and schizophrenia patients from healthy controls using shallow ANNs. However, distinguishing between OCD and schizophrenia patients remains more complex. Notably, the findings indicate that group differences may be more related to intrinsic saccadic generation properties than to fixation or inhibitory mechanisms, particularly in relation to unwanted, intrusive saccades during fixation tasks. This research provides valuable insights into the neurophysiological mechanisms underlying these disorders.

In the realm of mental health assessment, identifying depression through speech or conversation has emerged as a significant approach. However, the current methods face limitations, including reduced effectiveness due to inadequate feature selection and extraction, interpretability issues, and the challenge of detecting depression across various languages. This study proposes an innovative model aimed at enhancing accuracy and efficiency in depression detection. The model employs Adaptive Threshold-based pre-processing (AdaT) to eliminate redundant and insignificant information, and the Twinned Savitzky-Golay filter (TSaG) to minimize noise in the dataset. The Synchro-Squeezed Adaptive Wavelet Transform Algorithm (SSawT) is utilized to convert the signal into an image. The Singular Empirical Decomposition and Sparse Autoencoder (SiFE) model is employed for feature extraction, combining the input's deep, linear, and statistical properties using the Weighted Soft Attention-based Fusion (WSAttF) model. The Chaotic Mud Ring Optimization algorithm (ChMR) is then used to select the most relevant features from the fused data. A Dilated Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based Bidirectional-Long Short Term Memory-Bi-LSTM (DiCBiL) network is employed for detecting various stages of depression, thereby reducing error rates and increasing detection accuracy. On the DAIC-WOZ original test set, the proposed method achieved an F1-score of 93.22%, precision of 93.11%, recall of 93.12%, and accuracy of 93.31%. The model's performance was further validated on the AVEC 2019 and MELD datasets, yielding accuracies of 93.91% and 85.34% respectively. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Nature B.V. 2026.

The field of Camouflaged Object Detection (COD) has experienced significant growth due to its diverse practical applications. This study presents a human-machine collaboration framework for COD, capitalizing on the synergistic capabilities of computer vision (CV) models and noninvasive brain-computer interfaces (BCIs). The proposed approach integrates a multiview backbone to quantify CV prediction uncertainty, employs this uncertainty during training to optimize efficiency, and defers low-confidence cases to human evaluation via Rapid Serial Visual Presentation (RSVP)-based BCIs during testing, enhancing overall decision-making reliability. The framework's performance was assessed on the CAMO dataset, yielding state-of-the-art results with an average enhancement of 4.56% in balanced accuracy (BA) and 3.66% in the F1 score. For the top-performing participants, improvements reached 7.6% in BA and 6.66% in the F1 score. Analysis of the training process revealed a robust correlation between confidence and precision, while ablation studies affirmed the efficacy of our training policy and human-machine collaboration strategy. This research reduces cognitive burden on humans, boosts system dependability, and lays the groundwork for future advancements in real-world COD applications and human-computer interaction. The project's code and data can be accessed at: https://github.com/ziyuey/Uncertainty-aware-human-machine-collaboration-in-camouflaged-object-identification. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Nature B.V. 2025.

Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) is a rapidly progressing hematologic malignancy characterized by intricate genetic and epigenetic alterations. Circular RNAs (circRNAs), known for their covalently closed structures and remarkable stability, have garnered attention as potential diagnostic biomarkers. However, current circRNA-based predictive models primarily focus on differential expression, disregarding the potential influence of higher-order chromatin organization on circRNA formation and functionality. This study introduces a machine learning framework that incorporates three-dimensional (3D) genome architecture to refine the selection of circRNAs for AML prediction. By mapping 9,565 circRNAs onto a 3D chromatin model derived from Hi-C data, we examined their spatial clustering and biological pathway enrichment. Notably, 18 pathways displayed significant 3D aggregation of circRNAs, facilitating nuclear localization-based stratification. Five circRNA panels were developed using a combination of expression, pathway, and spatial features. Cross-validation and external validation across six machine learning algorithms revealed that the panel derived from the fifth radial layer (Panel-3DG-Radius5) delivered the most robust and consistent performance (ROC-AUC > 0.99). The integration of 3D genomic context reduced feature collinearity while enhancing biological interpretability. In conclusion, our research establishes a 3D genome-informed paradigm for circRNA biomarker discovery, demonstrating that spatial genome organization can significantly improve the precision and robustness of AML predictive modeling.

This study presents an innovative approach to enhance the performance of motor imagery-based brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) for brain-controlled wheelchair (BCW) applications. The proposed method, termed EHT-CSP, combines Ensemble Empirical Mode Decomposition Hilbert-Huang Transform (EEMD-HHT) with Time-Frequency Common Spatial Pattern (TFCSP) to extract and analyze features from electroencephalography (EEG) signals. The EHT-CSP algorithm calculates marginal spectrum entropy and energy spectrum entropy using EEMD-HHT, and combines these features with TFCSP-derived feature vectors to improve discrimination between different classes. The Light Gradient Boosting Machine is then utilized for classification. The efficacy of the proposed system is evaluated through both offline analysis and real-world BCW obstacle avoidance experiments, demonstrating an average classification accuracy of 78.45% across all participants, enabling successful navigation of the BCW. Comparative analysis with other algorithms reveals that the EHT-CSP model outperforms existing models, offering a promising solution for reliable multi-class classification in BCW systems. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Nature B.V. 2026.

In the realm of the Internet of Medical Things (IoMT), the necessity for lightweight yet efficient intrusion detection systems (IDS) capable of handling highly skewed, burst-like traffic generated by medical devices and real-time monitoring nodes is paramount. The conventional Gaussian Naïve Bayes classifier often falls short in this context due to its assumption of symmetric feature distributions. This study presents NBGamma, an enhanced Naïve Bayes classifier, which replaces the Gaussian likelihood with a Gamma-based likelihood formulation, thereby providing a more accurate representation of positively skewed traffic characteristics prevalent in IoMT networks. Unlike previous Naïve Bayes extensions, the proposed method integrates the Gamma distribution into the likelihood computation and formulates the IDS task as a security-driven optimization problem. Experiments conducted on the CSE-CIC-IDS2018 dataset reveal significant enhancements in precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC compared to Gaussian NB, both under unbalanced and SMOTE-balanced settings. These findings underscore NBGamma's capacity to more effectively identify malicious traffic patterns, thereby advancing lightweight IDS modeling for resource-constrained IoMT environments. This research contributes to the strengthening of cyberattack detection in IoMT healthcare settings, delivering more stable and reliable protection compared to traditional intrusion detection approaches.

In the realm of neurodegenerative diseases, Alzheimer's disease poses a significant threat to cognitive function, impacting neurotransmitters, neurons, and brain tissue, thereby affecting sensory perception, memory, and behavior. Despite the lack of a definitive cure, existing medications can help slow down the progression of the disease. Early detection of Alzheimer's disease is crucial for effective treatment and further research, yet it is hindered by the scarcity of trained samples and the proliferation of feature descriptions using various classification algorithms. Moreover, reliable AI-based solutions for detecting this disease are absent. This study introduces a system that employs supervised machine-learning models and deploys a web application for the preliminary identification of Alzheimer's disease. The Random Forest, Support Vector Machine, Decision Tree, Logistic Regression, and K-Nearest Neighbors classifiers were utilized for predictive purposes. The dataset, sourced from Kaggle, comprised 35 features and 2149 data points, which were balanced to enhance accuracy. Additionally, the importance of features was assessed to optimize model performance. Among the classification models, the Random Forest model demonstrated the highest accuracy of 91.19%. This research contributes to the development of AI-based solutions for the early detection of Alzheimer's disease. © The Author(s) 2025.

This study investigates the efficiency of a novel, compact, and lightweight dual-band switched-beam antenna, designed for 5G sub-6 GHz communications, operating at 0.7 GHz and 2.6 GHz. The antenna design incorporates grooves and parasitic elements to enhance bandwidth performance. Three machine learning models, specifically decision tree, random forest, and XGBoost, are employed to optimize seven crucial dimensional parameters for broadband operation. The models are trained on 244 samples derived from 305 simulated datasets generated by CST Microwave Studio, encompassing a wide range of conditions to target resonance frequencies and corresponding bandwidths. Beam steering is accomplished through the inclusion of a short-circuit mechanism, enabling the antenna to direct its main beam in two directions across both frequency bands. The proposed design achieves bandwidths of 21.65 MHz at 0.63 GHz and 192.89 MHz at 2.48 GHz, representing improvements of 11.52 MHz (113.72%) and 128.72 MHz (200.59%) compared to a conventional reference antenna. The design's performance in beam switching and wideband dual-band functionality was validated through simulation and experimental measurements, demonstrating strong agreement and confirming its effectiveness. Its streamlined structure, compact dimensions, and machine learning optimization make it a promising candidate for future wireless systems. © The Author(s) 2025.

In the realm of healthcare, predicting Patient Length of Stay (LOS) is of paramount importance, as it significantly influences hospital efficiency, resource allocation, cost management, and quality of care. However, predicting LOS in resource-limited settings poses challenges due to data skewness, outlier sensitivity, and imbalanced datasets. This study aims to address this gap by developing and evaluating Machine Learning (ML) models for predicting prolonged LOS (≥ 6 days) at Debre Markos Comprehensive Specialized Hospital, Ethiopia, using Electronic Health Records (EHRs) to optimize operational planning.

A retrospective analysis of 27,268 patient records from four hospital wards spanning 2017-2024 was conducted. Data preprocessing techniques, including feature engineering, one-hot encoding, and the Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) to address class imbalance (20,768 "Not Prolonged" vs. 6,500 "Prolonged"), were employed. Eight ML models (Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, Multi-Layer Perceptron, Support Vector Machine, K-Nearest Neighbors, and Naïve Bayes) were trained and evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC-ROC). The importance of features was assessed via permutation analysis.

The analysis revealed key demographic trends, such as a predominance of rural patients (62.3%), a peak age range of 29-49 years (26.8%), and a sharp hospitalization decline post-2021 due to regional conflict. The application of SMOTE significantly improved model performance, particularly in terms of recall and F1-scores. Among the models, Gradient Boosting demonstrated the best performance (AUC: 0.70, Accuracy:

In the realm of high-entropy alloys (HEAs), solid solution strengthening plays a pivotal role in enhancing their mechanical strength. Traditional strengthening theories often fall short in accounting for the intricate environments within HEAs. This study introduces a data-driven framework to examine the composition-dependent inherent strength of Face-Centered Cubic (FCC) HEAs. Employing large-scale molecular dynamics simulations, we quantify dislocation mobility across diverse temperatures and compositions, uncovering jerky and wavy glide patterns resulting from transient local pinning. The critical resolved shear stress (CRSS) at absolute zero is derived from these findings, and a linear relationship is established between CRSS and the standard deviation of atomic pinning strength. Subsequently, we identify atomic features that describe local structural and compositional fluctuations and construct a symbolic model to forecast the variability in atomic pinning strength based on these features, utilizing the Sure Independence Screening and Sparsifying Operator method. This framework offers valuable mechanistic insights and predictive capabilities for the development of robust, compositionally complex alloys, potentially revolutionizing the design of materials with enhanced strength properties. © The Author(s) 2026.

In the realm of English education, automated essay grading holds significant potential for enhancing efficiency by minimizing human bias and scaling assessment processes. This study proposes a novel hybrid scoring model, integrating Bi-directional Long Short-Term Memory (Bi-LSTM), Transformer encoder, and Light Gradient Boosting Machine (Light GBM) to surmount the limitations of traditional rule-based and machine learning methods. The research employs the Deep Essays Dataset, a diverse collection of student essays with human-assigned scores, procured from Kaggle. The methodology encompasses deep text preprocessing, shallow and semantic feature extraction, and the fusion of context representation, facilitated by Bi-LSTM and Transformer layers. These features serve as input for a Light GBM regressor, enabling accurate end score prediction. Extensive experiments on extensive datasets demonstrate that the proposed model surpasses existing methods substantially in terms of Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), Mean Absolute Error (MAE), and Pearson correlation metrics. Furthermore, the model's evaluation metrics, including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, consistently exceed 99%, underscoring its stability. Graphical analysis via Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) and Precision-Recall (PR) plots further attest to the system's robustness. The results underscore the efficacy of combining deep neural networks and gradient boosting to identify linguistic and semantic patterns in essays. This innovation in automated scoring technology offers a promising avenue for fair, reliable, and scalable assessment in English education. © The Author(s) 2026.

In the context of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM), early detection is crucial for effective prevention and targeted screening. This study aimed to develop and assess an interpretable Extra Trees Classifier (ETC) for the early identification of T2DM within the PERSIAN Dena Cohort, focusing on probability calibration, fairness, and clinical interpretability.

The study analyzed data from 3,203 adults aged 35-70 years, considering 79 variables, and retaining 15 informative predictors following preprocessing and feature elimination. The ETC was optimized through a randomized hyperparameter search and evaluated using ten-fold cross-validation, along with an 80/20 internal-external split. Isotonic regression was employed for calibrating probability estimates. Model transparency and feature influence were investigated using SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) and Morris sensitivity analysis.

The cross-validated performance demonstrated a mean accuracy of 0.69 ± 0.03 and Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.69 ± 0.04, suggesting moderate discrimination and stable internal consistency. On the 20% hold-out set, the uncalibrated model achieved an AUC of 0.67 and F1 score of 0.66. After isotonic calibration, the AUC decreased to 0.64, and the Brier score increased to 0.48 (slope 0.09; intercept −1.50), indicating under-confident probability estimates. Excluding fasting blood sugar (FBS) improved performance (AUC 0.77), while categorizing FBS into deciles reduced AUC to 0.57. Across sex and age subgroups, AUCs ranged from 0.63 to 0.70 without systematic bias. SHAP and Morris analyses identified FBS, fatty-liver status, age, kidney-stone history, and tr

In the era of burgeoning digital information, the significance of Recommender Systems (RSs) has surged as indispensable tools for sifting through data and providing personalized recommendations to users in the e-commerce domain. These systems leverage user preferences, historical reviews, and product ratings to streamline decision-making processes in the online retail sector. This review offers an in-depth analysis of numerous research papers published between 2020 and 2025, sourced from diverse journals and conferences, encompassing a broad spectrum of recommender system dimensions. The study underscores the development of innovative techniques designed to enhance system efficiency, with a focus on machine learning, deep learning, large language models, and conversational RSs, which are instrumental in the ongoing evolution of these systems. This review extends beyond conventional approaches, highlighting unaddressed research gaps and pinpointing critical factors shaping RS platforms. The objective of this study is to provide a holistic understanding of the current status and future trajectory of RSs within the e-commerce industry, thereby offering valuable insights for future research and development. © The Author(s) 2026.

In the burgeoning realm of medical diagnostics, artificial intelligence (AI) is increasingly significant. This study focuses on the efficacy of various machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) algorithms in the classification of pulmonary diseases, utilizing a standardized dataset of spirometry features from the Global Lung Initiative 2012. The research encompassed demographic variables and multiple spirometry parameters, addressing both a conventional four-class and an extended five-class classification problem. The study employed logistic regression, support vector machine, k-nearest neighbor, random forest, XGBoost, deep neural networks, and convolutional neural networks. To enhance model accuracy, preprocessing and standardization techniques were applied to the dataset. Evaluation metrics, including accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score, F1-macro, and the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve, were utilized to analyze model performance. The results indicated that tree-based models, particularly XGBoost and random forest, demonstrated superior generalization performance, with XGBoost achieving a success rate of 99.24 ± 0.59% and random forest at 97.23 ± 2.04%. Deep learning models, specifically deep neural networks and convolutional neural networks, recorded 81.61 ± 5.83% and 77.57 ± 5.55% accuracy, respectively, on the same conventional feature set. This research underscores the potential of ML in early pulmonary disease diagnosis. However, the need for further optimization to augment model performance is highlighted. The findings of this study offer valuable insights for the integration of AI-supported spirometry analysis into clinical practice. Nevertheless, validation on larger patient groups is crucial for the incorporation of these technologies into the healthcare system.

In the intersection of machine learning and chemistry, Molecular Representation Learning (MRL) serves a pivotal role in predicting molecular properties and complex tasks, such as drug discovery, by encoding molecules as numerical vectors. Despite the impressive performance of existing MRL methods on in-distribution data, their generalization ability often falters when confronted with distribution shifts, particularly in dynamic and uncertain real-world molecular environments. To tackle this challenge, this study introduces a novel framework, termed EISG (Environmental Inference and Subgraph Generation), for enhancing MRL's performance on out-of-distribution (OOD) data. The framework aims to capture the invariance of molecular graphs in various environments by integrating an unsupervised environmental classification model to identify latent variables generated by different distributions and a subgraph extractor based on information bottleneck theory, which extracts invariant representations from molecular graphs that are closely related to the prediction labels. By integrating new learning objectives, the environmental classifier and the subgraph extractor collaborate to help the model identify invariant graph representations in diverse environments, thereby improving OOD generalization robustness. The experimental results indicate that our model demonstrates robust generalization capabilities across various OOD scenarios. The code for this study is accessible on GitHub. © The Author(s) 2025.

In the realm of Artificial Intelligence (AI), algorithms are typically trained on extensive datasets to discern intricate patterns, enabling informed decision-making. The performance of these algorithms is contingent upon the quality of the training data, with data-intensive nature being a critical factor. However, stringent privacy regulations often restrict access to comprehensive medical datasets, resulting in reduced dataset sizes and under-representation of certain classes and demographic groups. This data scarcity can adversely impact AI performance. To address this issue, this study delves into the process of generating synthetic data, focusing on statistical, probabilistic, and Machine Learning (ML) based tabular data generation methods. A thorough analysis of high-quality, peer-reviewed articles was conducted across various databases. The results indicate that synthetic data not only increases the dataset volume but also enhances its quality by fostering diversity and improving the representation of diverse demographic groups. Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) and its variants are prevalent techniques, reporting up to a 12% enhancement in the classifier's classification performance. However, the quality of synthetic data is contingent upon the data generation process, the characteristics of real-world data used for modeling, and the metrics employed to evaluate data quality. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer-Verlag GmbH Austria, part of Springer Nature 2026.

In the context of tumor metabolic reprogramming, lactate accumulation plays a pivotal role, particularly in lung adenocarcinoma (LUAD). However, the spatial and cell-type-specific effects of lactate in cancer remain inadequately understood. This study integrates single-cell transcriptomics, spatial transcriptomics, spatial metabolomics, immunofluorescence, and TCGA survival data with machine learning models to elucidate these effects. High-lactate tumors were found to have increased epithelial and fibroblast populations, while T/NK cells and monocytes/macrophages were predominant in low-lactate samples. Spatial metabolomics revealed cell-type-specific lactate and pyruvate distributions, with endothelial cells showing minimal lactate accumulation. Endothelial subclusters in high-lactate tissues displayed angiogenic and stress-response signatures and were significantly associated with poor prognosis. Multiple machine-learning frameworks, including random forest, elastic-net regression, SVM, ANN, and decision-tree models, consistently identified endothelial and fibroblast programs as key determinants of high-lactate states and adverse clinical outcomes. In conclusion, our multi-omics spatial profiling reveals that lactate shapes the LUAD microenvironment by promoting angiogenesis, immune suppression, and prognostic stratification, suggesting lactate-centered pathways as potential therapeutic targets. © The Author(s) 2025.

In the contemporary landscape of pharmacotherapy, herb-drug interactions (HDIs) have gained significant attention due to the rising prevalence of herbal medicines and dietary supplements in conjunction with conventional drugs. These interactions can potentially impact drug efficacy and safety by influencing pharmacokinetic or pharmacodynamic processes, yet remain under-explored due to the intricacy and expense of experimental validation. This study introduces a novel graph-based computational framework designed to predict HDI effects. Our approach utilizes a heterogeneous, multi-relational graph that encompasses drug-drug interactions (DDIs), drug-target interactions (DTIs), and herb-target interactions (HTIs). To encapsulate the intricate semantics of this complex graph, we employ metapath-guided random walks to generate node embeddings, which are subsequently combined with molecular fingerprints, tabular descriptors, and target node encodings to train diverse supervised classifiers. Our experimental results substantiate the efficacy of our approach, particularly with tree-based models like XGBoost and Random Forest, demonstrating high predictive performance in multi-class classification tasks. These findings highlight the potential of AI-driven, graph-based methodologies in advancing integrative and precision medicine by facilitating scalable and accurate prediction of HDI effects. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer-Verlag GmbH Austria, part of Springer Nature 2026.

In the context of escalating stress-related health concerns, the development of precise and dependable stress detection systems is of paramount importance. This research presents a machine learning framework for stress identification, utilizing two extensively recognized multimodal physiological datasets: NEURO and WESAD. The framework employs a combination of classical machine learning models and deep learning architectures to categorize various stress levels. To effectively extract temporal and structural patterns from physiological time-series data, the raw signals are converted into image representations using Gramian Angular Summation Fields (GAFs), Gramian Angular Difference Fields (GAFd), and Markov Transition Fields (MTF). Unlike traditional methods that transform data along the time axis, our approach performs transformations along the feature axis, enabling the model to discern feature-centric patterns crucial for stress state differentiation. The Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) is utilized to boost classification performance by extracting spatial features from these visual representations. Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) visualizations are employed to identify the most significant input regions influencing the model's decision, thereby providing insights into the physiological markers of stress. The study reveals consistent strong generalization across both datasets, with deep learning models based on feature-axis transformations achieving approximately 98.3% accuracy, and classical models such as XGBoost and LightGBM attaining nearly 99% accuracy. The incorporation of Grad-CAM validates the significance of learned features, enhancing the model's interpretability. In conclusion, our approach underscores the potential of integrating sophisticated signal transformation techniques with explainable deep learning to create robust and interpretable stress detection systems.

Abdominal organ segmentation in medical imaging is a complex task, characterized by low contrast and anatomical intricacy. To tackle this challenge, we introduce MSAE-U-Net, a novel deep learning architecture, specifically designed for accurate and robust abdominal segmentation. The proposed architecture incorporates three significant innovations: the Multi-Scale Deformable Convolutional Block Attention Module (MS-D-CBAM), which enables multi-scale attention; the Adaptive Global Context Refinement ASPP (AGCR-ASPP), responsible for dynamic context refinement; and the Rectangular Self-Calibration Module (RCM), responsible for spatial feature recalibration. These components enhance both local detail and global context in segmentation. The performance of MSAE-U-Net was evaluated on the Dresden Surgical Anatomy dataset, where it demonstrated superior results with a Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) of 93.13%, an Intersection over Union (IoU) of 92.01%, and a precision of 94.67%. These results surpass those of existing models, such as U-Net, nnU-Net, and AbdomenNet. The superior performance of MSAE-U-Net suggests its potential for clinical applications, including preoperative planning and automated organ identification. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer-Verlag GmbH Austria, part of Springer Nature 2026.

In the global health landscape, cancer continues to be the second leading cause of mortality, with brain tumors accounting for a substantial portion of cancer-related fatalities. Early and precise diagnosis of brain tumors is vital for prompt therapeutic intervention and enhancing patient prognoses. The burgeoning field of medical imaging, specifically image classification, has significantly boosted the efficiency of computer-aided diagnostic systems in this regard. This study introduces five hybrid models, namely VGG16 + LSTM + SVM, VGG19 + LSTM + SVM, ResNet50 + LSTM + SVM, ResNet152 + LSTM + SVM, and EfficientNetB3 + LSTM + SVM, designed for the multi-class classification of brain tumors. These models leverage the feature extraction capabilities of convolutional neural networks (VGG16, VGG19, ResNet50, ResNet152, and EfficientNetB3) in conjunction with the sequential learning properties of Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks and the robust classification abilities of Support Vector Machines (SVM). The methodology commences with data augmentation to mitigate class imbalance and expand the diversity of the training dataset. The features extracted by the convolutional layers are processed through LSTM networks, which identify temporal dependencies, followed by classification using SVM. The experimental outcomes indicate that the proposed models surpass current leading methodologies, with the ResNet152 + LSTM + SVM model achieving 99% accuracy, 99% precision, 99% recall, and 99% F1 score when employing the RMSprop optimizer. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer-Verlag GmbH Austria, part of Springer Nature 2026.

In the contemporary landscape of telecommunications and computer vision, advancements have led to an exponential growth in functionalities and applications. Despite this progress, these domains have primarily developed independently, neglecting the potential synergies arising from the integration of their respective innovations. This study proposes the CONVERGE vision-radio concept, a novel paradigm that bridges the gap between these two fields, envisioning a harmonious fusion of knowledge termed "View-to-Communicate, Communicate-to-View." In complex environments, such as those characterized by obstructions or poor lighting, the integration of radio sensing and computer vision can significantly enhance problem-solving capabilities. Machine learning algorithms, capable of fusing heterogeneous and multi-modal data, play a crucial role in this integration, enabling the extraction of additional knowledge from raw and low-level data, thereby creating a higher-level abstraction that can substantially boost numerous applications. This paper outlines the fundamental concepts of the CONVERGE vision, including supporting use cases and the proposed architecture. The CONVERGE framework introduces a comprehensive suite of tools that seamlessly integrate wireless communications and computer vision, thereby establishing a cutting-edge experimental infrastructure. This infrastructure will provide the scientific community with open datasets, encompassing both experimental and simulated data, fostering research in various 6G verticals, including telecommunications, automotive, manufacturing, media, and health.

In the context of respiratory viral infections, including COVID-19, influenza, and respiratory syncytial virus (RSV), this study employs an integrative analysis of the COVID-19 Host Genetics Initiative (HGI) GWAS data (freeze 7) and publicly available multi-omics datasets to construct an explainable AI model for discerning host infection mechanisms across these viral illnesses. The model reveals shared antiviral pathways, particularly type I interferon (IFN) signaling, in host responses to all three viruses. However, it also identifies unique mechanisms: SARS-CoV-2 infection is characterized by strong coagulation and renin-angiotensin system dysregulation, along with sustained AP-1/MAPK activation. In contrast, influenza triggers more robust T-cell activation, while RSV instigates an excessive neutrophil-driven inflammatory response. Genetic risk pathway fingerprints from GWAS indicate that the severity of COVID-19 is linked to variants in IFN and inflammatory pathways. In contrast, host genetic effects in influenza suggest distinct receptor usage (sialic acid biosynthesis) with minimal overlap. Mendelian randomization (MR) pinpoints key causal proteins for COVID-19 severity, such as ABO (blood group glycosyltransferase) and inflammatory mediators, implying that host glycomic and immune factors play a significant role in disease outcomes. The model, integrated with these multi-omic features, accurately distinguishes COVID-19 from other viral infections, with SHAP interpretation confirming the predominance of the aforementioned mechanisms in model predictions. In summary, this cross-omics study offers a comprehensive comparative map of host responses in COVID-19 versus influenza and RSV, providing biologically interpretable insights into both common antiviral defenses and unique pathogenic pathways.
