Title: Unified End-to-End Framework for Simultaneous 3D Human Pose Estimation and Energy Expenditure Prediction

Background: This research tackles critical issues in 3D human pose estimation (HPE) and energy expenditure estimation (EEE), particularly in handling intricate activities, enhancing generalization, and concurrently improving both tasks within a unified framework.

Objective: The objective is to develop Pose2Met, a unified end-to-end framework that addresses 3D HPE and EEE concurrently.

Method: At the heart of this framework is STAPFormer, a Transformer model featuring a SpatioTemporal Aggregated Pose (STAP) representation for efficient and accurate motion modeling. Pose2Met introduces a unified pose–metabolism learning strategy, which optimizes both pose dynamics and metabolic patterns within a single learning paradigm. This allows the model to directly predict both 3D pose and energy expenditure from 2D pose inputs, offering performance comparable to traditional 2D-3D-expenditure pipelines while significantly boosting computational efficiency and robustness in practical applications.

Results: Experimental results demonstrate that STAPFormer achieves a Mean Per Joint Position Error (MPJPE) of 38.2 mm on Human3.6M, surpassing MixSTE and STCFormer. For EEE on Vid2Burn-ADL, it achieves a Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 22.1 kcal with pose-based input, comparable to video-based methods. Under the unified learning framework, 2D pose-based EEE approaches the accuracy of 3D pose-based prediction, indicating enhanced robustness and generalization.

Conclusion: The findings underscore the significance of high-quality motion representations for both HPE and EEE. Pose2Met shows promising potential for intelligent fitness and healthcare applications, offering a promising direction for bridging the gap between pose and expenditure estimation.

In the realm of sentiment analysis, the brevity and informal nature of social media messages pose significant challenges, as slang, emojis, and misspellings negatively impact predictive accuracy and hinder the understanding of model outputs. This study proposes a lightweight hybrid model, integrating a convolutional block, a bidirectional long short-term memory network (BiLSTM), a single multi-head self-attention layer, and a novel noise-invariant contrastive head (NICH). The NICH normalizes noisy tokens prior to encoding, enhancing the model's robustness. The model undergoes two training phases (frozen and fine-tuned word embeddings) and is assessed on two diverse datasets: Sentiment140 (comprising 1.6 million tweets) and IMDb (containing 50,000 movie reviews). The proposed model surpasses traditional baselines (logistic regression, support vector machine, random forest) and an Attention-BiLSTM reference model, while maintaining parity with a compact Transformer-based baseline (DistilBERT). On Sentiment140, the model achieves an accuracy of 90.63% and an F1 score of 0.9079, while on IMDb, it attains 91.90% accuracy and 0.9203 F1 score. The model's attention scores and a perturbation-based metric demonstrate consistent focus on sentiment-relevant tokens, offering token-level explanations beneficial for real-time monitoring of customer feedback and brand perception in noisy social media streams. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Nature Switzerland AG 2026.

In the burgeoning fintech sector, startups play a crucial role in driving financial innovation, yet they exhibit an alarmingly high rate of failure. This necessitates the development of sophisticated predictive tools to aid investment and policy decisions. This study proposes a deep learning framework utilizing 1D Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to classify fintech startup outcomes into four distinct categories: Initial Public Offering (IPO), Merger & Acquisition (M&A), closure, and Unicorn status, based on global Crunchbase data. The CNN model is designed to discern local and hierarchical patterns from structured data attributes such as team composition, funding velocity, investor diversity, and company age. To mitigate class imbalance, the study employs ADASYN oversampling. In comparison to conventional models like XGBoost, the proposed CNN model demonstrates competitive performance, achieving 90.8% accuracy and 89.6% macro F1, while XGBoost attains 92.9% accuracy and 91.9% macro F1. Notably, the CNN model exhibits enhanced sensitivity towards rare outcomes such as IPOs and Unicorns. The findings suggest tree-based models' dominance on structured data, yet CNNs offer a promising deep learning alternative for multiclass startup outcome prediction. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Nature Switzerland AG 2026.

In the realm of smart agricultural Internet of Things (IoT) systems, the dynamic management of resources is a significant challenge due to device heterogeneity and environmental variability. This study presents a novel framework that integrates 6G edge computing with reinforcement learning to optimize resource allocation. Leveraging the ultra-low latency and high bandwidth of 6G for swift communication and efficient task offloading, the framework employs reinforcement learning to adapt resource allocation strategies according to evolving environmental states and task demands. A customized reward function is designed to strike a balance between latency reduction, resource utilization, and energy efficiency. The experimental results indicate a reduction in latency by 35% and an improvement in resource utilization by 25% compared to conventional methods, with scalability confirmed under growing device density and task complexity. This research offers a scalable and adaptive solution for smart agriculture, applicable in various domains such as precision irrigation, autonomous farming, and drone surveillance. © The Author(s) 2025.

In the era of advanced big data and artificial intelligence, Large Language Models (LLMs) have gained significant traction in various domains, including healthcare. This study presents a novel research framework, the HLLM-Potential Framework, designed to evaluate the effectiveness of LLMs in analyzing both structured and unstructured medical data within the healthcare domain. The framework is validated through comparative experiments with traditional machine-learning and deep-learning models, utilizing publicly available and standardized cardiovascular datasets.

The study demonstrates that existing LLMs are capable of processing and leveraging diverse medical data for disease prediction and health-risk assessment. In structured cardiovascular disease prediction tasks, such as heart failure, the evaluated LLMs achieved accuracy and recall rates exceeding 80%. Furthermore, in unstructured cardiovascular data analysis, specifically ECG image classification, the multimodal LLM, Janus Pro 7B, achieved an overall accuracy rate of 85%.

The findings suggest that LLMs exhibit superior interactivity, generalization capabilities, and less reliance on data quality and feature engineering compared to traditional machine-learning and deep-learning models. This study underscores the potential of LLMs in the healthcare field due to their high efficiency, flexibility, and multimodal integration, positioning them as promising tools for future medical and healthcare applications.

In the realm of computer science, sparse signal recovery is a pivotal issue, with applications spanning various domains such as medical imaging, remote sensing, machine learning, and data science. The inherent non-negativity of the underlying signal, as observed in quantities like pixel intensities, chemical concentrations, or power spectra, necessitates a specialized approach to sparse recovery. Although extensive research exists on general sparse recovery and non-negative sparse recovery, a unified theoretical and algorithmic framework that seamlessly accommodates both has been lacking. This study introduces a unified feasible sequential quadratic programming (SQP) framework, which treats general and non-negative sparse recovery as distinct instances of a single constrained optimization model, differentiated only by the trust region geometry employed to ensure feasibility.

The framework is grounded in the Smoothed-ℓ0 (SL0) approximation, a smooth, non-convex substitute for the combinatorial ℓ0 norm. Leveraging the exact diagonal structure of the Hessian of the SL0 objective, we derive accurate second-order search directions, resulting in a superlinear convergence rate that significantly outperforms the first-order methods prevalent in the literature. For the non-negative case, we incorporate interior-point concepts directly into the SQP subproblem. Instead of relying on post hoc projections or hard thresholding, which may disrupt convergence and induce numerical instability near the boundary, we implement an ellipsoidal trust region to ensure all iterates remain within the non-negative orthant. This approach guarantees stability and theoretical validity. The non-negative method commences with a simple, robust all-ones vector as its initial state, avoiding the potential instabilities associated with pseudo-inverse-based initializations.

We prove global convergence of the unified framework under the Mangasarian–Fromovitz Constraint Qualification (MFCQ). In the realm of machine learning, Bayesian Causal Forests (BCF) have emerged as a potent tool for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTE) in scenarios where confounding variables are present. The efficacy of BCF, however, hinges on the precise estimation of propensity scores (PS). Incorrect PS estimation can result in biased treatment effect estimates. To mitigate this issue, we introduce Bayesian Causal Forests with Covariate Balancing Propensity Scores (BCF-CBPS), a novel approach that integrates covariate balancing within the propensity score estimation phase. By substituting conventional PS with CBPS, our method enhances covariate balance, thereby allowing BCF to more accurately estimate the true extent of treatment effect heterogeneity. Extensive Monte Carlo simulations and real-world applications corroborate that BCF-CBPS offers improved precision and robustness compared to traditional BCF and other machine learning methods for HTE estimation. These findings underscore the utility of merging covariate balancing with the BCF model for reliable estimation of treatment effects in intricate observational studies. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Nature Switzerland AG 2026.

This study introduces a machine learning framework for the analysis and classification of modern and contemporary Korean paintings, utilizing image data. The approach employs a vision-language pre-trained architecture, complemented by multi-layered analysis, to efficiently extract intricate formal attributes, such as color features from various spatial dimensions and quantified texture. The extracted feature vectors are subsequently clustered and assessed under majority-label assignment, yielding an overall accuracy of 82.4%, surpassing single-feature benchmarks, including RGB (82.0%), HSV (81.3%), histogram (51.0%), Local Binary Patterns (LBP, 68.8%), and Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM, 73.7%). The proposed method exhibits improved per-artist precision, enhanced boundary-case discrimination, and enhanced resilience for categories with limited samples. The resulting artist clusters showcase distinct color and texture patterns. Further analysis was conducted using automatic image captioning and zero-shot style assignment via image and text embedding matching. The results indicate that machine learning-based image analysis offers a reliable and objective methodology for identifying and distinguishing visual characteristics of modern and contemporary Korean paintings, providing a quantitative approach to art-historical interpretation. © The Author(s) 2026.

In the context of disaster management and sustainable land use planning, the precise identification of landslide-prone areas is crucial for effective disaster relief operations. Traditional detection methods often face challenges due to the labor-intensive nature, subjectivity, and inaccuracy, particularly in regions with inaccessible landslide distributions. This study introduces an innovative machine learning approach designed to enhance the identification of various landslide types, addressing the aforementioned limitations. The methodology is based on a multi-stage fusion architecture, which leverages knowledge about factors contributing to landslides. Initially, an information gain filter is employed for feature selection, followed by the training of linear and ensemble classification models using the selected features. Subsequently, the model makes landslide predictions and provides explanations for its decisions. Compared to other machine learning models, this ensemble model demonstrates superior performance in landslide prediction tasks. Furthermore, the proposed model employs effective visualization techniques to ensure accurate explainability. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Nature Switzerland AG 2026.

In the burgeoning realm of text mining, social networks have emerged as a significant resource, offering a wealth of user-generated text data through comments and posts. This data, particularly from social media platforms, is invaluable for various business applications due to its potential to reflect public opinion. Sentiment Analysis (SA), a subfield of Natural Language Processing, is instrumental in automatically categorizing opinions in text as positive, negative, or neutral. Its utility is particularly evident when applied to vast textual data from social media, enabling the understanding of public sentiment. However, the extraction of social media data is hampered by platform-specific APIs, privacy constraints, and limitations on data usage, making the acquisition of high-quality, representative datasets for research a challenge. This study introduces TSAPM-BERT, a BERT-based framework that incorporates a weighted attention mechanism, sentiment lexicon information, and learning rate optimization to improve aspect-level sentiment classification. The model's performance was assessed on a benchmark emotion dataset, with results showing a training accuracy of 99.37 and testing accuracy of 98.52, surpassing traditional machine learning and deep learning baselines. This research offers a replicable framework that reconciles high-accuracy sentiment classification with the practical constraints of social media data usage, bridging the gap between academic research and real-world applications. © The Author(s) 2026.

In this study, we introduce a novel image dataset specifically designed for the detection of surface defects in waterborne painted wood products. The dataset encompasses 13,400 high-resolution images, categorizing four distinct defect types: scratches, cracks, bubbles, and holes. The dataset is composed of 3,645 bubble defects, 3,498 scratch defects, 3,256 crack defects, and 3,001 hole defects. The images were obtained from an operational production facility in Jiangshan, China, utilizing specialized industrial cameras. The data underwent thorough annotation by professional annotators to ensure precision. This dataset serves as a valuable resource for integrating deep learning models into real-world industrial assembly lines, facilitating the development of automated machine learning solutions for multi-class defect detection. By employing these techniques, timely defect detection and rectification can be achieved, thereby ensuring the integrity of the finish and the quality of the final product. © The Author(s) 2026.

In the operational context of machine learning systems, the emergence of concept drift poses a significant challenge, as it negatively impacts the performance of pre-trained models and undermines the reliability of predictions. To preserve the accuracy and credibility of predictions, an efficient drift detection and adaptation mechanism is indispensable in these systems. While various supervised, semi-supervised, and unsupervised drift detection techniques have been explored, recent research has primarily concentrated on deep learning-based methods, particularly those based on autoencoders. Autoencoders, known for their versatility, offer diverse types and implementations, each designed to handle specific tasks. In the context of drift detection, the objective is to learn the data distribution using an autoencoder and measure the deviation of incoming data in terms of reconstruction loss. Although standard autoencoders are commonly used, other variations exist and can be assessed for enhanced drift detection performance. In addition to the type of autoencoder, the selection of appropriate parameters and hyperparameters is crucial for an autoencoder-based drift detection mechanism. This paper presents a framework to enhance the performance of autoencoder-based drift detection methods. We conduct a theoretical and empirical evaluation of various applicable autoencoder types, including denoising, variational, and standard, followed by a comprehensive method for selecting the optimal hyperparameters using grid search and the selection of drift detection method-specific parameters using both grid search and Bayesian optimization (BO). For the experimental analysis, we utilize AEDDM (autoencoder-based drift detection method) as the foundation to develop Opt-AEDDM—an optimized version of AEDDM. Extensive experiments on four synthetic datasets (Hyperplane, Gaussian, VD, and RBF) and four real-world datasets (NOAA, ELEC2, KDDCUP99, and Forest Covertype) demonstrate the applicability and effectiveness of the proposed framework in identifying the best autoencoder with the optimal set of hyperparameters and parameters, resulting in improved drift detection performance. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer-Verlag London Ltd., part of Springer Nature 202

In the realm of urban and regional studies, as well as public health monitoring, the utilization of accurate location data from social media platforms, such as Twitter, is crucial. However, only a minuscule 2% of tweets are geo-annotated, posing a significant challenge. This study proposes a classification framework that overcomes this limitation by accurately predicting tweet locations based solely on textual and contextual information within the tweet itself. The framework exhibits global applicability and dynamically adjusts its spatial granularity according to the tweet volume within a specific area, ranging from country-level predictions in less active regions to state-level predictions in high-activity zones. Extensive evaluations were conducted to assess the influence of various factors on the algorithm's performance across diverse global regions. Moreover, our findings indicate that excluding a modest percentage of tweets with limited location cues enhances the overall model's quality. © The Author(s) 2026.

In the realm of data-driven machine learning, the significance of data volume is undeniable, particularly in tasks where data is scarce or costly, such as predictive maintenance. This study investigates the utility of synthetic data generation in such scenarios, focusing on time series data augmentation for classification and regression problems. Unlike previous research, which primarily employed geometric transformations, interpolation, generative models like GANs and VAEs, this work delves into the under-explored application of denoising models, specifically diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs). The proposed D3A-TS methodology integrates DPMs, recently successful in image processing, for data augmentation in time series. Furthermore, the influence of meta-attributes in conditioning the data augmentation process is explored. The efficacy of this methodology is demonstrated through six diverse datasets, showcasing its adaptability to various input sizes and output types. An extensive ablation study is conducted to corroborate the obtained results, underscoring the potential of D3A-TS in creating synthetic data for training machine learning models. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Nature Switzerland AG 2025.

This study explores the integration of artificial intelligence (AI) in the creation of film and television scripts, delving into the prospects and obstacles in script generation, plot development, and character portrayal, facilitated by machine learning and natural language processing (NLP) technology. This paper proposes a script generation model utilizing deep neural networks, employing a vast dataset of film and television scripts for training and optimization of script structure, linguistic expression, and emotional expression. The model demonstrates promising results in generating short scripts with a commendable balance of creativity and structure, showcasing impressive fluency and accuracy. However, the model encounters challenges in generating lengthy scripts, managing intricate plots, and expressing nuanced emotions. To address these limitations, future research will focus on enhancing the model's capacity for complex plot processing and emotional expression, and optimizing computational efficiency and generation quality. The findings of this study offer a preliminary empirical foundation for the application of AI technology in creative industries, paving the way for technological advancements and practical explorations in script creation. © The Author(s) 2025.

In the context of Earth's energy and water cycles, soil moisture, particularly in deeper soil horizons, remains underrepresented in existing datasets due to a focus on surface layers. To bridge this gap, this study introduces a novel global, daily, multilayer soil moisture dataset (SWSM) for the timeframe 2002-2021, utilizing a machine learning technique, XGBoost. The SWSM offers estimates at a 0.05° spatial resolution for three depth intervals: 0-10 cm, 10-30 cm, and 30-60 cm. The dataset's validity was substantiated through rigorous comparison with in-situ observations, demonstrating high accuracy, with Pearson correlation coefficients surpassing 0.90 and root mean square errors below 0.05 across all depths. A feature importance analysis confirmed the dataset's physical coherence, revealing depth-dependent patterns consistent with established hydrological principles. The SWSM dataset, characterized by its extended temporal coverage, fine spatial resolution, and multi-layer structure, serves as a valuable tool for various applications, including hydrologic modeling, agricultural water management, and climate change studies. © The Author(s) 2025.

This study introduces ML-TWiX, a comprehensive dataset of monthly Total Water Storage Anomalies (TWSA) spanning from 1980 to 2012, distributed on a 0.5° × 0.5° global grid. The limited duration of the GRACE and GRACE Follow-On satellite missions, covering approximately two decades, restricts their applicability in long-term climate and hydrological investigations. To bridge this gap, ML-TWiX extends the GRACE-era record into the pre-GRACE period by employing an ensemble of three machine learning models: Random Forest, XGBoost, and Gaussian Process Regression. These models were trained on global hydrological and land surface model simulations to reconstruct TWSA. The individual reconstructions were then combined through ensemble averaging, providing a unified product with spatially explicit uncertainty estimates. The performance of ML-TWiX was verified against various independent datasets, such as satellite laser ranging, water mass balance closure, and global mean sea level budget estimates. ML-TWiX offers a continuous reconstruction of global TWSA, catering to diverse applications in hydrology, climate science, and water resource assessment. The copyright for this work belongs to the authors in 2026.

In the rapidly advancing technological landscape, the disconnect between the skills taught in educational institutions and the evolving demands of the industry poses a substantial challenge. The constant evolution of industries necessitates updated knowledge and new competencies from the workforce. This study proposes a machine learning-based framework designed to dynamically align industry skill requirements with educational curricula, thereby generating adaptive course recommendations. Data is collected from various sources such as job portals, industry reports, company career pages, and labor market analysis, to capture the most current skill demands across multiple sectors. The framework employs a sophisticated machine learning model, the Dynamic Termite Life Cycle Optimizer-driven Euclidean-Support Vector Machine (DTLC-Euclidean SVM), to forecast both current and emerging skill demands in the job market. By analyzing patterns in graduate skillsets and employer requirements, the system identifies skill gaps and adjusts educational offerings accordingly. The dynamic matching algorithm uses similarity metrics and k-Means Clustering techniques to map industry needs to existing course content, while an automated course generation module suggests new or updated courses to address identified skill deficiencies. The framework demonstrates high performance accuracy (96.8%), precision (97.6%), recall (96.2%), and f1-score (97.3%), underscoring its robustness and reliability in addressing the skill gap. This data-driven approach facilitates continuous curriculum adaptation, promoting stronger alignment between academia and industry. Ultimately, the framework empowers educators and policymakers to develop responsive, targeted educational programs that prepare students for real-world career opportunities, enhancing employability and meeting the demands of a rapidly evolving labor market. © The Author(s) 2025.

The significance of effective Physical Education (PE) instruction lies in fostering students' physical growth, engagement, and long-term health outcomes. Traditional evaluation methods often lack objectivity and fail to encompass the intricate aspects of PE instruction. This study aims to develop a comprehensive assessment framework for evaluating PE teaching effectiveness, leveraging Multimodal Machine Learning (MML). The framework processes complex, multi-source educational data, including instructional delivery (video and audio), physical movement patterns (via wearable sensors and motion capture), and student feedback. To maintain data integrity and consistency, two preprocessing methods are implemented: wavelet denoising for noise reduction in sensor signals and z-score normalization to standardize data across different modalities and ensure balanced input distributions. The Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG) is utilized for feature extraction, capturing minute movements and posture characteristics from video sources, and creating a spatial representation of physical activities. The core of the assessment framework is the Enhanced Marine Predators-driven Explainable Boosting Machine (EMP-EBM), a hybrid system that combines the global optimization strengths of the EMP with the transparent and interpretable structure of the EBM. The proposed EMP-EBM model demonstrated superior performance, achieving an accuracy of 98.07%, precision of 97.85%, recall of 98.31%, and an F1-score of 98.07%. This model's ability to identify and assess efficient PE teaching sessions using multimodal inputs underscores its potential to revolutionize the application of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in education, enhancing the evaluation process to be more objective, adaptive, and aligned with the multifaceted objectives of PE. © The Author(s) 2025.

In the realm of healthcare, the non-invasive, simultaneous monitoring of multiple patients' vital signs in various clinical settings remains a significant technological hurdle. This study presents a meticulously compiled radar-based dataset, created in collaboration with Ostrava University Training Hospital, for evaluating techniques aimed at multi-patient vital sign monitoring. The dataset encompasses recordings from sophisticated medical simulation mannequins (SimMan 3G Plus), replicating a diverse spectrum of physiological states, ranging from resting health to acute emergencies such as apnea, cardiac arrest, and severe respiratory distress.

To ensure versatility, we manipulated sensor positions (top, frontal, lateral views at 1-4 meters, 0°/45° angles), and radar parameters (ADC samples, chirp loops, ramp times, frame rates, gains), resulting in over 100 distinctly configured sessions. Preliminary analysis employing beamforming-based processing has shown promising results, with mean heart-rate and respiratory-rate errors approximating clinical thresholds (Mean Absolute Error (MAE) ≈ 6.6 beats per minute for heart rate, 1.47 breaths per minute for respiratory rate). This suggests the dataset's potential in fostering the development of advanced signal processing and machine-learning pipelines.

Adhering to FAIR (Findability, Accessibility, Interoperability, and Reusability) principles, the data is fully documented and publicly accessible, promoting reproducible research in the development of non-invasive multi-patient monitoring systems. © The Author(s) 2026.

In the context of elderly cardiac surgery, intraoperative acquired pressure injuries (IAPI) pose a significant risk due to skin fragility and compromised perfusion. Accurate risk prediction is crucial for effective prevention and personalized care. This study employs machine learning techniques to develop a predictive model for IAPI in patients aged 60 and above, utilizing 24 clinically relevant features from the MIMIC-IV database. Seven classifiers were integrated through a stacking ensemble, and their performance was evaluated using stratified fivefold cross-validation with 10 repetitions and bootstrapped confidence intervals (2,000 resamples). Missing data uncertainty was quantified using multiple imputations (m = 2,000). For external validation, the model was tested on 355 patients from Nanjing First Hospital, with resampling to address class imbalance. Calibration metrics and SHAP analysis with bootstrapping were used to ensure model reliability and interpretability. The ensemble model demonstrated strong discrimination in both internal (AUC = 0.857) and external (AUC = 0.783) validation, with consistent performance across imputations (mean AUC: 0.997 ± 0.004). Calibration analysis confirmed good agreement between predicted and observed risks. Key predictors such as atrial fibrillation, lymphocyte count, and operative time were clinically plausible and robustly identified. This study presents an externally validated and interpretable ensemble learning framework for early IAPI risk prediction in elderly cardiac surgery patients. The approach, which incorporates multiple imputation, bootstrapped calibration, and SHAP-based robustness, enhances methodological transparency and provides a clinically actionable tool for perioperative risk stratification and prevention. The clinical trial number for this study is NCT05855954, registered on ClinicalTrials.gov on March 21, 2023. © The Author(s) 2025.

Diabetes, a global health concern, poses significant challenges to millions worldwide. Early and accurate prediction of diabetes is essential for mitigating severe complications and enhancing patient care. Machine learning regression techniques have emerged as potent tools for diabetes prediction, enabling the modeling of intricate relationships between risk factors and disease progression. With the ongoing development of feature selection, regularization, and ensemble methods, the predictive performance of these algorithms has markedly improved, enabling more precise and personalized healthcare interventions. However, the rapid expansion of regression techniques, datasets, and evaluation metrics has led to a complex landscape, making it challenging to compare different approaches objectively. This study offers a comprehensive survey of contemporary trends in diabetes prediction, with a specific focus on regression models. The primary objectives of this research are as follows: 1) Examine the development and performance of regression-based machine learning techniques, evaluating how algorithmic advancements impact prediction accuracy and dependability. 2) Propose a standardized assessment framework for evaluating and benchmarking diabetes prediction models, ensuring fair and reproducible comparisons. 3) Assess the efficacy of leading regression algorithms in this domain, identifying which methods offer a suitable balance between interpretability and predictive power for healthcare applications. 4) Outline future research directions, emphasizing the need for robust, transparent, and patient-centric prediction models that can seamlessly integrate into clinical practice. © The Author(s) 2025.

In the context of Alzheimer's Disease (AD), a significant contributor to dementia cases, early and accurate diagnosis is crucial for effective intervention strategies. This study presents a hybrid machine learning framework, integrating SqueezeNet, a lightweight convolutional neural network, with an ensemble stacking of multiple machine learning models, to enhance AD detection using Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) scans. The framework utilizes a dataset of 3,382 MRI images, sourced from Kaggle, categorized into four classes: Mild, Moderate, Very Mild Dementia, and Non-Dementia. SqueezeNet is employed to extract efficient features, thereby reducing computational requirements while maintaining diagnostic precision. A 10-fold cross-validated stacking ensemble is employed, combining predictions from Multi-Layer Perceptrons with varying architectures (70 × 70 and 100 × 100), XGBoost, Cat Boost, and AdaBoost, to optimize robustness. The proposed model demonstrates superior performance, achieving a Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 94.9% and an accuracy of 89%, surpassing individual models such as XGBoost (83.2% accuracy). The model addresses class imbalance through rigorous validation, and SqueezeNet's fire module architecture ensures efficient computation with reduced parameter count, making it suitable for resource-limited clinical environments. The model's performance was assessed using four metrics: F1-score, recall, accuracy, and precision. The stacking model outperformed single classifiers, offering a scalable, reliable, and cost-effective tool for real-time clinical AD detection and classification. © The Author(s) 2026.

In the realm of malware classification, high-dimensional feature spaces pose substantial challenges to machine learning efficacy. This study offers a comparative assessment of four dimensionality-reduction techniques: Principal Component Analysis (PCA), Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection (UMAP), and Autoencoder-based reduction. These techniques were applied to opcode-frequency representations of malware, utilizing a comprehensive corpus containing 82,569 samples and 1796 unique opcodes. The investigation explores the impact of each reduction method across varying target dimensions and two classifier architectures: Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) and a three-layer Multilayer Perceptron (MLP). The results indicate that LDA demonstrates robust separability at lower dimensions, while PCA performs optimally in higher dimensions where variance preservation is crucial. Autoencoder-based reduction consistently delivers high accuracy with compact representations, whereas UMAP shows limited advantages for tabular opcode data. This research underscores the trade-offs between linear and non-linear reduction strategies, offering valuable insights for the selection of efficient feature compression methods in large-scale malware analysis. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer-Verlag France SAS, part of Springer Nature 2026.

In the context of the economic implications of genetic testing for identifying identical and non-identical twins, this study proposes a machine learning framework for predicting zygosity based on physical similarity features. The research utilizes data from the Iranian School-aged Twin Registry, collected from 2018 to 2021. To mitigate class imbalance, the Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) was employed. Eight machine learning algorithms, including K-nearest neighbors (KNN), support vector machine (SVM), logistic regression (LR), random forest (RF), decision tree (DT), and boosting techniques (Gradient Boosting Classifier, XGBoost, AdaBoost Classifier), were developed and optimized using GridSearchCV. The models were trained on an 80:20 train-test split, and their performance was evaluated using multiple metrics. XGBoost demonstrated the highest accuracy (85.57%) and F1-score (86.72%) among the evaluated algorithms. While XGBoost, Logistic Regression, and Gradient Boosting showed comparable results without statistically significant differences, XGBoost was selected due to its marginal lead. The SHAP algorithm was further utilized to analyze the feature importance of physical traits in zygosity prediction. This study establishes XGBoost with SMOTE oversampling as an effective approach for predicting twin zygosity through physical similarity questionnaires. Future research will aim to validate this model with genetic tests and compare its results for further confirmation.

The accurate identification of epileptic seizures from electroencephalogram (EEG) data is crucial for early diagnosis and informed clinical decision-making. However, centralized machine learning methods pose privacy concerns due to the sharing of sensitive patient data across institutions. This study introduces a privacy-preserving framework for epileptic seizure recognition, employing federated and explainable machine learning techniques. The study utilizes a publicly accessible EEG-derived dataset, categorized into five original classes, which were restructured as a binary classification task differentiating seizure and non-seizure episodes. The dataset was segmented at the patient level to prevent data leakage, with distinct training and testing sets. A simulated federated learning environment was established with five nodes, incorporating both independent and non-independent data distributions, and executed over fifty communication rounds using the Federated Averaging strategy. A variety of machine learning models, such as Support Vector Machine, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, and K Nearest Neighbors, were assessed using standardized hyperparameter configurations. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy, area under the ROC curve, confusion matrices, and learning curves. Explanatory power was attained through SHAP and LIME to offer insights into the model's decision-making process. The findings indicate that federated learning can deliver competitive performance while maintaining data privacy, and that explainable methods provide transparency in seizure classification decisions. This study serves as a proof-of-concept evaluation using an EEG-derived dataset, emphasizing the methodological feasibility rather than clinical application. © The Author(s) 2026.

In the era of digitalization, tables have emerged as a fundamental structure for organizing structured data in diverse domains such as healthcare, education, and finance. Automatic table recognition, which converts table content from images into editable and machine-readable formats, plays a crucial role in enhancing processing efficiency and accuracy. While convolutional neural network (CNN) and Transformer-based approaches have made significant strides, they still encounter difficulties in dealing with intricate table layouts, varying formats, and high computational expenses. To tackle these issues, we introduce the Spatial Pyramid Pooling-enhanced Multi-modal Linear Transformer (SPPLTR), a novel table-recognition model. This model combines spatial pyramid pooling with a causal linear-Transformer architecture, effectively capturing both structural and textual information in table images through a spatial pyramid context fusion module (SPCF) and a causal linear Transformer. Furthermore, the model integrates a multi-head causal linear attention (MHCLA) mechanism, along with automatic mixed precision (AMP) support, enhancing inference efficiency and stability. Experiments on the PubTabNet dataset reveal that SPPLTR outperforms most existing models across multiple evaluation metrics, achieving state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance. The proposed model presents a promising avenue for table recognition tasks and paves the way for future advancements in structured document comprehension. The SPPLTR code will be accessible at https://github.com/Zhanxin-Xin/SPPLTR. © The Author(s) 2025.

In the context of ensuring the integrity and dependability of wind turbine systems in cold environments, accurate icing detection is crucial to prevent structural failure. This study introduces an intelligent data integration framework that combines vibration-based structural dynamics with environmental data, employing a sequence of automated Operational Modal Analysis (OMA) and temperature correction. Five classification algorithms, including Optimized Threshold (OT), Random Forest (RF), XGBoost (XGB), Support Vector Machine (SVM), and Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU), were comprehensively assessed using time-series cross-validation on high-resolution data derived from a large-scale climate chamber. To cater to the safety-critical nature of the application, the framework prioritizes Recall to minimize False Negative (FN) errors and employs non-parametric statistical validation (Kruskal-Wallis and Dunn’s post-hoc tests) to measure model consistency. The findings indicate a "simplicity advantage" where the physics-informed OT model exhibited the highest operational reliability (Recall = 0.9600), surpassing sophisticated ensemble and deep learning techniques in terms of both safety and statistical robustness. This research underscores the effectiveness of careful feature-level fusion and domain-specific engineering in augmenting system resilience over algorithmic complexity. The proposed methodology serves as a scalable basis for multi-sensor fusion and adaptive predictive maintenance in renewable energy infrastructures. © The Author(s) 2026.

In the digital age, the proliferation of machine-generated text, notably deepfake content, poses significant challenges for social media platforms, threatening trust and fostering misinformation. This study aims to tackle the identification of machine-generated textual content on social media platforms, focusing on short texts such as tweets. We develop an intelligent framework to combat disinformation, leveraging the DeBERTaV3 model, the TweepFake dataset, and the PHEME dataset. This framework analyzes tweets and social media posts to distinguish between human-generated and machine-generated text. The proposed model's efficacy is assessed against several deep learning frameworks, including BERT, RoBERTa, SVM-RBF, random forest, SVM, CNN, and LSTM. The results demonstrate a high accuracy of 97.12% in detecting fake news, indicating the model's exceptional performance and reliability. The experimental findings suggest that the DeBERTa architecture, combined with data preprocessing techniques and embedding methods, facilitates efficient and accurate tweet classification, enabling the identification of the originator of a tweet. Furthermore, the scalability and computational efficiency of the proposed model are compared with those of other models, demonstrating its potential for large-scale implementation. © The Author(s) 2026.

In this study, we assess the performance of AI-based credit-scoring models against conventional statistical models, utilizing a dataset comprising 1000 loan applications as a case study. The primary research objective is to investigate the superiority of machine learning methods in terms of predictive accuracy, interpretability, and resilience to economic downturns, compared to traditional methods. Four model variants were developed: logistic regression and decision tree as representatives of traditional models, and XGBoost gradient-boosting ensemble and multilayer perceptron neural networks as AI-driven alternatives. The experimental setup employed R (v4.3.1), which implemented a 70:30 train-test stratified split, inner cross-validation, and harmony spatial search for hyperparameter tuning. The results indicate that XGBoost delivers an optimal balanced accuracy cumulative gain curve (cumulative gain, CGC: 0.89; area under the receiver operating characteristic, AUC: 0.89), surpassed by the neural network (CGC: 0.87; AUC: 0.87), and followed by logistic regression (CGC: 0.76; AUC: 0.76). SHAP analysis reveals that the amount of credit, loan duration, and borrower's age are critical predictors. During stress-test simulations, XGBoost demonstrates stability in prediction-making (AUC = 0.83), contrasting a significant drop in logistic regression results (AUC = 0.68). These findings substantiate the assertion that contemporary AI tools exhibit superior predictive capacity and robust interpretability, positioning them as promising alternatives to the systems prevalent in contemporary financial institutions. © The Author(s) 2025.

In the realm of clinical radiology, the precise assignment of imaging protocols is a pivotal aspect of workflows. This study investigates the degree to which the decision-making of GPT-4 aligns with human expertise in text-based classification of imaging protocols, and compares its performance and interpretability with fine-tuned models. The study tested GPT-4, a fine-tuned BERT model, and a fine-tuned LLaMA-3 model using physician-entered clinical indications corresponding to 11 head MRI protocol categories. The models' predictions were assessed against expert-validated ground truth labels using F1 scores, and the reasoning coherence of each model was evaluated by a board-certified radiologist. To examine a practical decision-support workflow, the study also evaluated LLaMA-3-assisted prompting, where a fine-tuned classifier's prediction was used as additional context for GPT-4, allowing for optional revision and explanation. GPT-4 achieved an F1 score of 0.83, lower than BERT (0.91), LLaMA-3 (0.93), and human experts (0.96). Despite underperforming in raw classification accuracy, GPT-4 consistently produced the most interpretable, human-like explanations, demonstrating a nuanced understanding of clinical language and imaging rationale. When conditioned by LLaMA-3, GPT-4's accuracy significantly improved, suggesting that collaborative work between general and specialized models can boost both performance and transparency. In conclusion, these findings suggest that GPT-4 can generate interpretable, text-based outputs that may support clinical decision-making when used with appropriate safeguards. © The Author(s) 2026.

In the context of clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC), a notoriously aggressive malignancy, the contributions of single-nucleotide variants in VHL, PBRM1, and SETD2 have been extensively studied. However, the impact of structural variations (SV), which can have broader genomic consequences, and the 3D genome architecture in ccRCC remain under-investigated. This study presents a comprehensive molecular characterization of these aspects in ccRCC. Through multi-omics analysis, we have identified novel SV-associated oncogenic targets and uncovered multidimensional 3D genome reorganization during ccRCC progression. Our findings reveal the intricate interplay between SV and 3D chromatin architecture, demonstrating how structural rearrangements contribute to oncogenic dysregulation via 3D genome reorganization. Notably, an overlooked pathogenic enhancer hijacking event was discovered and experimentally verified, resulting in the constitutive activation of the proto-oncogene SEMA5B. Furthermore, we developed a machine learning-based prognostic framework using enhancer hijacking signatures, offering a clinically applicable tool for ccRCC prognosis. This research provides significant insights into the role of SVs and 3D genome reorganization in ccRCC oncogenesis and offers a valuable resource for future ccRCC research. © The Author(s) 2026.

Anemia, characterized by reduced hemoglobin levels due to iron or vitamin deficiencies, blood loss, or genetic disorders like sickle cell anemia and thalassemia, affects over a billion individuals worldwide. Diagnosing anemia in resource-limited settings, such as rural areas, is often problematic due to costly laboratory tests, scarcity of medical personnel, and insufficient infrastructure. This study explores the application of artificial intelligence (AI) in anemia detection, focusing on three types: iron deficiency anemia, sickle cell disease, and thalassemia. We compare various AI techniques, including machine learning, deep learning, optimization algorithms, hybrid algorithms, and others, in terms of accuracy, essential features, research gaps, and potential future developments. Additionally, we assess the dataset sizes and their sources employed in these studies to identify any correlation with model accuracy. AI presents a promising solution for real-time anemia diagnosis, offering non-invasive, rapid, affordable, and accurate diagnostic capabilities, along with explainable AI techniques to boost physician trust and usability, particularly in remote locations. However, traditional invasive tests, such as complete blood count and peripheral blood smear examinations, remain the primary diagnostic methods due to their accuracy, despite being time-consuming, labor-intensive, and requiring medical expertise. Integrating AI into these invasive diagnostic methods can significantly alleviate the burden on healthcare professionals and aid in diagnostic confirmation.

In the context of Iraq's economic development, this study endeavors to create and assess machine learning models capable of forecasting the productivity of residential construction projects. The research employs three advanced prediction techniques: artificial neural networks (ANN), extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost), and support vector regression (SVR). Data from 67 housing projects were utilized, with seven input variables and one output variable representing project productivity (measured in real-time). The evaluation findings indicate that the XGBoost model exhibited the highest predictive precision, with a determination coefficient (R2) of 0.998 and a mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of 0.8%. The ANN model also demonstrated strong performance (R2 = 0.92, MAPE = 4.1%). Conversely, the SVR model showed lower accuracy (R2 = 0.813, MAPE = 6.6%). The analysis of feature importance further revealed that built-up area and the quantities of brick and concrete works were the primary determinants of productivity. These findings suggest that machine learning methodologies, particularly XGBoost and ANN, offer valuable and reliable tools for decision-making in the preliminary stages of housing project planning, as they predict project execution efficiency based on project-specific data. © The Author(s) 2025.

This study explores the potential of machine learning and computational methods in expediting materials discovery by accurately predicting material properties at minimal cost. A significant challenge lies in the preparation of input data for algorithms and the optimization of structure model parameters. To surmount the limitations of conventional battery materials, this research focuses on high-entropy materials, a distinctive class of valuable materials. The understanding of high-entropy materials' crystal structure design is pivotal in the pre-synthesis design and fabrication of materials for batteries and supercapacitors, facilitated by learning algorithms and quantum computing. This review delves into the application of quantum computing in high-entropy materials, particularly high-entropy MXenes. It further discusses the encoding and decoding of crystal structures, a crucial step in creating a database for high-entropy materials. The utilization of deep learning algorithms for material discovery prior to synthesis and the identification of high-entropy materials suitable for batteries and supercapacitors are also addressed. Lastly, the potential of new quantum computing and artificial intelligence approaches for determining the structure of high-entropy materials in energy fields is explored. (Figure presented.) © The Author(s) 2025.

This study proposes an advanced, AI-integrated framework designed to augment Management Information Systems (MIS) capabilities, focusing on sustainable supply chain optimization and environmental impact analysis. The framework employs a combination of classical machine learning regression models, including Linear Regression, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost, and Support Vector Regression, as well as a deep learning-based Gated TabTransformer (GTT) for predicting Greenhouse Gas (GHG) emissions. Furthermore, a Variational Graph Autoencoder (VGAE) is utilized for unsupervised anomaly detection, and a hybrid interpretability model, constructed with Explainable Boosting Machines (EBM) and SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP), is implemented for explainable AI. The results indicate that Linear Regression and ensemble models, such as Gradient Boosting (R2 = 0.9995, MAE = 0.0036), demonstrate superior performance in terms of accuracy and robustness compared to existing methods. The GTT model also demonstrates strong generalization (R2 = 0.9883), while the VGAE is capable of detecting anomalies in emission patterns by learning both topological and feature-level deviations. Moreover, the framework provides interpretable local and global insights for data-driven decision making, ensuring transparency. The proposed framework serves as a comprehensive solution that bridges sustainability, AI, and MIS, advancing the state-of-the-art in sustainability analytics within the supply chain domain. © The Author(s) 2025.

In the expanding realm of Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs), their utilization in both military and civilian domains has surged. However, their operation in an open and unsecured environment renders them highly vulnerable to routing attacks such as wormhole, sinkhole, sybil, and black hole attacks. Given the large number of small, low-cost devices in a WSN, they are often subjected to various types of assaults due to their deployment in exposed and unprotected locations. This research proposes an Intrusion Detection System (IDS) tailored for cluster-based WSNs, where each cluster elects a Cluster Head (CH), and collectively, all the clusters select a Group Head (GH) to monitor node behavior and network activity. The proposed model, Dual Level Node Authentication Model with Node Behavior Analysis (DLNA-NBA), integrates a machine learning model for precise intrusion detection in WSNs. The research's findings indicate that DLNA-NBA significantly enhances intrusion detection capacity, achieving 98.9% accuracy in dual-level node authentication and 99.2% accuracy in intrusion detection, surpassing conventional models. The enhanced node verification, efficient utilization of features, and effective behavioral pattern analysis collectively ensure secure communication and minimize false alarms in WSN environments. This research offers a promising solution to bolster the security of WSNs in the face of growing threats. © The Author(s) 2026.

The precise prediction of sports-related injuries is crucial for maintaining athlete health and enhancing performance. In this study, we assessed the efficacy of machine learning (ML) models in predicting injury risk in 300 male professional football players aged between 18 and 28, over two competitive seasons (2021-2022). Injuries were defined as musculoskeletal conditions resulting in at least one missed training session or match, confirmed via ICD-10 diagnoses. Daily data on training workload, recovery, wellness, heart-rate variability, cumulative minutes played, and injury history were gathered. The features were pre-processed using normalization, one-hot encoding, and feature selection techniques, such as LASSO regression and recursive feature elimination. Missing data (less than 3%) were filled using multiple imputation by chained equations, while class imbalance was handled with SMOTE and weighting. Logistic regression, decision tree, and random forest models were trained using 10-fold cross-validation and evaluated based on accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC. Random forests demonstrated superior performance, achieving an accuracy of 85.6 ± 2.1%, precision of 82.1 ± 1.9%, recall of 80.3 ± 2.4%, F1-score of 81.2 ± 2.2%, and AUC of 90.5 ± 1.6%. Explanatory AI techniques, including SHAP and LIME, highlighted prior injury, training intensity, and recovery time as the most significant predictors, enabling personalized risk assessment. These results suggest that ensemble ML methods offer robust, interpretable, and actionable insights for injury prevention, supporting data-driven strategies to optimize training and reduce injury occurrence. Future research should extend validation across multiple sports and incorporate additional physiological and genetic factors to boost predictive accuracy and generalizability. © The Author(s) 2025.

In the burgeoning field of precision medicine, artificial intelligence (AI) has emerged as a transformative force. This study aims to evaluate the cost-effectiveness of AI-driven precision medicine (AI-PM) by systematically reviewing and analyzing relevant economic evaluations. A comprehensive search was conducted across five major databases, followed by data extraction and risk-of-bias assessment using the Bias in Economic Evaluation (ECOBIAS) checklist. The study included 48 economic evaluations, with 31 being cost-utility analyses. Despite potential systematic optimism in the risk-of-bias assessment, AI-PM demonstrated cost-saving or cost-effectiveness in 89% of base-case analyses, with incremental cost-effectiveness ratios ranging from dominant to $129,174 per quality-adjusted life-year (QALY). The study further revealed modest health gains at minimal additional costs, as indicated by the interquartile ranges of incremental costs (-$259 to $28), QALY gains (0.001–0.019), and net monetary benefits ($18 to $986 at a willingness-to-pay threshold equal to one-time per-capita GDP). The study also identified modeling choices and system-level factors as significant contributors to the heterogeneity in estimated net monetary benefits. Additional value assessment unveiled low adaptability and underreporting of key value factors, underscoring the need for further research to address uncertainties in AI-PM adoption. This study provides valuable insights into the cost-effectiveness of AI-PM, highlighting its potential for cost savings and modest health gains, while also underscoring the need for improved value assessment and reporting.

Background: Urosepsis, a significant contributor to perioperative mortality following upper urinary tract stone surgery, necessitates an accurate and straightforward predictive model for postoperative sepsis. This study aims to develop such a model by integrating laboratory parameters using machine learning (ML) and the Shapley Additive Explanations (SHAP) algorithm.

Objective: The objective of this research is to create a simple and accurate predictive model for postoperative sepsis, leveraging a combination of ML techniques and the SHAP algorithm, to facilitate early risk stratification and personalized intervention in clinical practice.

Method: A comprehensive analysis of data from 7,464 patients, including 155 pre- and postoperative features, was conducted. Key variables were selected using Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator (LASSO) regression and correlation analysis. Eight ML algorithms were employed to develop the predictive model using 10-fold cross-validation. The model's performance was assessed using Receiver Operating Characteristic curve, learning curve, calibration plot, and decision curve. The SHAP algorithm was employed to analyze variable importance. The final model was converted into a publicly accessible application.

Results: Out of 155 variables, 116 (74.84%) showed significant differences between the urosepsis (n = 622, 8.33%) and non-urosepsis groups (n = 6,842, 91.67%). LASSO regression identified eight predictive variables: postoperative IL-6, SAA, PCT/ALB, NLPR, PT, ALB, HCT, and neutrophil. The Light Gradient Boosting Machine demonstrated the best performance, with Area Under the Curve (AUC) values of 1.0, 0.90, and 0.88 for the training, validation, and test cohorts, respectively. The model exhibited a good fit, strong calibration,

In the current educational landscape, the increasing integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) has evolved from an optional tool to a mandatory requirement, raising concerns about the psychological and emotional stress it induces. Conventional technology adoption models often overlook the challenges presented by obligatory digital environments, where design flaws in AI systems can lead to cognitive overload and student resistance. This study proposes and validates a student-centric framework to investigate the impact of AI system design, focusing on system reliability, task automation, and information density, on students' negative technological experiences and their subsequent effects on techno-resistance and academic performance. A quantitative research approach was adopted, gathering data from 229 undergraduate students in Malaysian universities. Exploratory factor analysis, correlation analysis, and multiple regression were employed to analyze relationships among the key constructs and to validate the proposed model. The results indicate that system reliability and information density significantly decrease students' negative experiences, while task automation does not. Negative technological experiences were found to be a significant mediator, intensifying techno-resistance and negatively impacting academic performance. These findings underscore the need for a reconsideration of the assumption that the mandatory use of AI technologies in education automatically leads to improved learning outcomes. This study redefines techno-resistance as an adaptive response to the cognitive and emotional strain caused by the poor design of AI systems in compulsory educational settings. It extends theoretical knowledge by integrating digital dissonance with sociotechnical and cognitive load perspectives, and provides practical guidance for the development of AI educational technologies that cater to student needs. © The Author(s) 2026.

In the realm of quantum computing, the potential for computational advantages in simulating quantum many-body systems is widely acknowledged. However, recent studies have demonstrated that classical machine learning algorithms can accurately predict various properties of quantum systems using classical data. While quantum advantage examples for learning tasks have been proposed, they are primarily associated with cryptographic functions and lack relevance to laboratory settings. This paper presents a significant advancement by proving quantum advantages for the physically pertinent task of learning quantum observables from classical (measured-out) data. We focus on two types of observables: first, we establish quantum learning advantages for linear combinations of Pauli strings, and subsequently, we expand our findings to a broader spectrum of unitarily parametrized observables. For each case, we demarcate clear boundaries between tasks that can be efficiently addressed classically and those requiring quantum computers for data analysis. Unlike previous studies, our classical hardness results are based on the weaker assumption that BQP hard processes cannot be simulated by polynomial-sized classical circuits. Furthermore, we propose a non-trivial quantum learning algorithm. Our findings offer insights into the utility of quantum resources for learning problems in quantum many-body physics and suggest potential practical directions for quantum learning improvements. © The Author(s) 2026.

Machine Learning research has witnessed a surge of interest in Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), a key component of deep learning, due to their potential to generate high-quality synthetic data. Initially conceived for data generation, GANs have demonstrated remarkable innovation in the machine learning field. Over the years, numerous architectural improvements have been proposed to enhance performance and training stability, leading to the development of notable models such as DCGAN, WGAN, StyleGAN, and BigGAN. These advancements have significantly improved the quality and diversity of generated data, addressing the challenges associated with the training process. Today, GANs are utilized in a wide range of applications, including high-resolution image synthesis, speech generation, medical data reconstruction, educational data simulation, and style transfer. However, despite these advancements, GANs continue to face persistent challenges, including convergence instability, mode collapse, hyperparameter sensitivity, and the difficulty of objectively evaluating synthetic data quality. This survey aims to provide a comprehensive analysis of 45 distinct GAN architectures, categorizing them based on learning paradigm, application domain, network design, associated datasets, and open challenges. It also outlines potential avenues for future research, including the integration of GANs with reinforcement learning, attention-based architectures, and large language models (LLMs), to generate meaningful, stable, and user-aligned content. This analysis offers a coherent perspective on current trends and future opportunities in the GAN landscape. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Nature Switzerland AG 2026.

In the era of pervasive urban sensing, data integration, and artificial intelligence, recommender systems have emerged as critical tools for delivering equitable, efficient, and citizen-centric urban services. This study systematically reviews scholarly literature spanning mobility, healthcare, energy, tourism, retail, and e-governance, providing a taxonomy of collaborative filtering, content-based methods, hybrid designs, deep learning, and context-aware approaches in urban decision-making. The review encompasses key smart-city domains, with a significant number of empirical studies found in mobility and tourism. However, the application of deep and graph-based techniques in governance and energy remains relatively scarce and underrepresented.

This review explores the integration of diverse data sources, such as IoT streams, geospatial signals, environmental indicators, and demographic attributes, to facilitate personalized services under constraints like latency, reliability, and privacy. It highlights progress in addressing sparsity and cold start issues through graph neural models, sequence modeling, and transfer learning. Additionally, it discusses operational enablers such as edge inference and streaming architectures for real-time recommendations.

The review underscores the importance of addressing risk and governance aspects, including privacy preservation, fairness, balanced exposure across neighborhoods, explainability, and mechanisms for audit and oversight. It also identifies potential opportunities in pollution management, citizen education, and participatory platforms to foster increased civic engagement. Furthermore, it outlines how hybrid physical-virtual interactions, digital twins, immersive interfaces, generative models, and emerging quantum algorithms may redefine personalization and oversight in city-scale settings.

Lastly, the study advocates for field evaluations and standardized benchmarks that simultaneously measure accuracy, latency, robustness to distribution shift, and equity in urban recommender systems. These measures are crucial for ensuring the fair and efficient delivery of urban services in the age of smart cities. © The Author(s) 2026.

In the realm of Alzheimer's disease (AD), genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have pinpointed the involvement of immune and metabolic pathways, yet the cellular implications remain elusive. To elucidate these, this study employs bidirectional two-sample Mendelian randomization to discern single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP)-mediated immunometabolic signatures associated with AD. The results reveal a complex interplay between immune cell types and metabolic pathways, with some showing positive correlation, while others display negative associations with AD.

Further investigation through integrated single-cell omics analysis unveiled distinct astrocyte subpopulations in AD patients' brains. One subpopulation, characterized by Glutamate-glutamine uptake and metabolism, was positively associated with AD, whereas another, marked by Amino acid metabolism and transport, exhibited a negative correlation. In contrast, peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) primarily showed AD-negative metabolic signatures coupled with diminished immune responses.

Building upon these findings, a blood transcriptome-based AD prediction model was developed and optimized using multiple machine learning techniques. This model, derived from blood immune cells, is negatively associated with AD and demonstrates applicability across both European and Asian populations. It offers potential for pre-symptomatic detection in familial AD cases, distinguishes AD from other neurodegenerative disorders, and is amenable to clinical implementation.

In summary, this study offers novel insights into the critical role of immunometabolism in AD and presents a practical predictive tool suitable for large-scale, routine population screening.

In the realm of ophthalmic surgery, this study aims to mitigate the variability in surgical outcomes for non-paretic horizontal strabismus by employing machine learning models to predict surgical correction dosages. These models are based on preoperative patient characteristics, with a focus on utilizing explainable artificial intelligence to discern feature significance.

The research commenced with a comprehensive retrospective analysis of patients undergoing strabismus surgery at the University Clinic of Ulm between 2003 and 2022, resulting in a refined dataset of 767 patients. A multilayer perceptron, constructed using the functional class from the torch neural network, was developed to evaluate the data and create Model 0.

The importance of features was assessed using approaches from explainable artificial intelligence to ensure the attributions were plausible. The results demonstrated that the Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno algorithm yielded the highest number of predictions within an acceptable range. For Model 0, the root mean square error for both labels (L1 and L2) were 0.54 mm and 0.71 mm, respectively. Approximately 69% and 55% of the data for L1 and L2, respectively, were predicted within the acceptable range.

The preoperative angle of deviation emerged as the most crucial factor influencing dosages, as confirmed by feature permutation and integrated gradients. This was followed by the near angle of deviation, the use of simultaneous cover test, Hirschberg test angle, and refraction of the patient's own glasses.

In conclusion, the developed neural network presents a potential tool for predicting dosages in strabismus surgery, potentially aiding surgeons in their decision-making process. Enhanced prediction accuracy is anticipated with an expansion of the data basis.

In the digital age, the proliferation of misinformation on social media platforms poses a significant threat to societal trust and security, with emotionally charged content often amplifying its dissemination via retweets, replies, and shares. This study aims to tackle the issue of identifying anomalous propagation subgraphs indicative of coordinated misinformation campaigns. We introduce the Multi-Graph Neural Network Attention-based Propagation Learning for Emotion-Aware Anomaly Detection framework (MAPLE). MAPLE integrates sentiment data from users' historical posts with network structures, employing a fusion mechanism that harmoniously combines three distinct Graph Neural Networks: Graph Convolutional Network, Graph Attention Network, and GraphSAGE. The resulting subgraph embeddings are assessed using a One-Class Support Vector Machine for anomaly detection. Theoretical analyses ensure guarantees on mutual information preservation, variance reduction, anomaly margin amplification, and entropy maximization. Experimental results on the PolitiFact and GossipCop datasets demonstrate that MAPLE consistently surpasses existing state-of-the-art baselines, enhancing F1-scores by 43% and 2.17% respectively, while maintaining robustness across datasets. Unlike previous approaches that consider structural or sentiment cues in isolation, MAPLE presents the first unified multi-Graph Neural Network fusion framework, incorporating emotional context and theoretical foundations for subgraph anomaly detection. © 2026 The Authors

In the realm of machine learning, feature selection (FS) plays a pivotal role, particularly in handling high-dimensional datasets that often contain redundant or irrelevant features, leading to increased computational complexity and reduced classifier generalization. This study introduces the Dynamic Binary Swordfish Movement Optimization Algorithm (DBSMOA), a novel nature-inspired binary optimizer, as a solution to the limitations of existing binary metaheuristics that rely on static control parameters. The DBSMOA is modeled on the foraging dynamics of swordfish, extending the original Swordfish Movement Optimization Algorithm (SMOA) by incorporating dynamic behavioral adaptation mechanisms. These mechanisms enable a self-adjusting balance between exploration and exploitation throughout the optimization process, ensuring efficient search and optimization. The algorithm employs a sigmoid-based probabilistic binary mapping and dynamically reassigns agent roles through a performance-driven elitist strategy, maintaining diversity and stability during search. Evaluations on 52 benchmark datasets reveal that DBSMOA achieves the lowest average classification error on 35 datasets and the smallest average subset size on 34 datasets, outperforming twelve state-of-the-art binary optimizers, including Binary Particle Swarm Optimization (bPSO), Binary Genetic Algorithm (bGA), and Binary Grey Wolf Optimizer (bGWO). The results underscore DBSMOA's competitive accuracy, compact feature subsets, and strong computational efficiency, underscoring its robustness, scalability, and suitability for high-dimensional feature selection and other binary optimization tasks.

In the context of cervical cancer, a significant precursor being high-grade cervical intraepithelial neoplasia (CIN2/3), current diagnostic methods, such as HPV testing and colposcopy, present challenges in terms of accessibility and invasiveness, particularly in resource-limited settings. To address this issue, this study aimed to create a non-invasive, machine learning (ML)-based model utilizing routine blood biomarkers for the assessment of high-grade CIN risk, potentially serving as a pre-colposcopy triage tool.

Data was gathered from two groups: 128 high-grade CIN (CIN2/3) and 120 low-grade CIN (CIN1) patients. A total of 29 clinical characteristics and blood test measurements were considered for model development. Four feature selection algorithms and 11 ML algorithms were employed to identify key predictors and train the model, respectively. The dataset was divided into training (70%) and testing (30%) cohorts. Model performance was evaluated using learning curves, ROC, AUC, Brier score, calibration curves, PR curves, DCA, and SHAP for feature importance assessment. A web-based calculator was developed for clinical deployment.

The model's key features were identified as creatinine (CREA), red blood cell count (RBC), neutrophil ratio (NEU%), direct bilirubin (DBIL), and monocyte count (MON). The Support Vector Machine (SVM) algorithm demonstrated the best predictive performance, with an AUC of 0.75 (95% CI: 0.69–0.80) and a Brier score of 0.21 (95% CI: 0.17–0.28). The web tool (https://dvhl6xsf29zmdewixjx7kz.streamlit.app) offers real-time risk stratification.

In conclusion, the model demonstrated strong performance across various

In the development of functional materials, the simultaneous optimization of multiple interdependent properties is essential for enhancing their performance in various applications, such as electronics and catalysis. Boron-doped graphene (BDG) is a prime example, as both its band gap and work function significantly impact its functionality. However, existing machine learning (ML) methods primarily focus on single-property prediction, employing hand-crafted features, which restricts their applicability. This study introduces an adaptive edge-aware graph convolutional neural network with multi-task learning (AEGCNN-MTL) for the simultaneous prediction of multiple material properties. The AEGCNN-MTL was tested on a dataset of 2613 BDG structures computed using density functional theory (DFT), achieving high accuracy (R² = 0.9905 for band gap and 0.9778 for work function). Compared to single-task graph neural network (GNN) baselines, AEGCNN-MTL demonstrated superior performance under identical training budgets. Furthermore, when applied to the QM9 benchmark, the framework showed competitive performance across 12 diverse quantum chemical properties, indicating its strong generalization capability. These findings underscore the potential of AEGCNN-MTL as a versatile and accurate tool for high-throughput, multi-property screening, and the data-driven discovery of multifunctional materials.

In this study, we introduce a novel and efficient method for generating a fingerprint of crystalline materials, designed to strike a balance between machine processing and human comprehension. This approach, termed Pettifor embedding, is applicable in both machine learning inference and the investigation of structure-property relationships. The Pettifor embedding consists of two primary components: one for crystal structure representation and another for chemical composition characterization. The chemical composition component is constructed within a non-orthogonal space, where each axis corresponds to a chemical element, and the angle between axes quantifies the similarity between elements. The chemical composition is then defined as a point on the unit sphere within this non-orthogonal space. We demonstrate that the Pettifor embeddings significantly outperform conventional elemental embeddings in compositional machine learning models. By employing the Pettifor embeddings to establish a distance metric and dimension reduction techniques, we create a global, two-dimensional map of the thermodynamically stable crystalline compound space. Surprisingly, these simplified maps effectively segregate material classes based on fundamental physical properties, underscoring their potential utility in material science and machine learning applications. © The Author(s) 2025.
